EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels remained high across central and northern Iowa especially along the West Fork of the Des Moines River. Rainfall through early to mid May caused the river to go back into flood at Emmetsburg and Humboldt for a short period in mid May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg crested at 11.15 feet on 13 May 2018 at 14:00 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled out boundary continued to linger across southern Iowa and northern Missouri for the third day/event in a row. South of the boundary, SBCAPE values were modest in around 2000 J/kg, however much of the action for Iowa was north of the boundary within an elevated MUCAPE environment of 2000+ J/kg. Add in lackluster effective bulk shear under 35 kts, and the result was predominantly storms with little residence time for consistent large hail development.  A number of storms produced sub-severe hail, but a couple did produce severe reports, including up to around golf ball sized hail in south-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels remained high across central and northern Iowa especially along the West Fork of the Des Moines River. Rainfall through early to mid May caused the river to go back into flood at Emmetsburg and Humboldt for a short period in mid May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt crested at 10.63 feet on 16 May 2018 at 08:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels remained high along the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville and additional heavy rain toward the middle to end of May led to the river going back into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville crested at 8.42 feet on 25 May 2018 at 17:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring snow storm occurred from the evening of March 23rd into the evening of March 24th.  This system brought heavy wet snow to much of eastern Iowa.   A very sharp cutoff of snowfall was seen with this system.  Snowfall totals between 2 and 8 inches were common, with the heaviest snow of 8 to a near record 14 inches falling in a 30 mile wide line from Waterloo, to Anamosa, to Clinton, IA for this late in the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of 9 to nearly 14 inches of snow were reported across the county ranging from about 9 inches in the southeast to nearly 14 inches around Wyoming Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell on parts of northeast Iowa from February 8th into the 9th as a storm system moved across the Midwest. The snow created hazardous driving conditions that prompted most schools to close on the 9th. Snowfall totals were generally in the 5 to 6 inch range with the highest reported total being 6.5 inches near Volga (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across Fayette County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Oelwein.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread snow event took place over Iowa and Illinois during the day through evening on the February, 5th, 2018.  In most locations, this event produced a widespread 3 to 6 inch accumulation in about 12 hours, which combined with very light winds, to produce moderate impacts corresponding to a Winter Weather Advisory. A band of much heavier snow fell on the south edge of the widespread snow event, and was only 20 to 30 miles wide, but generally stretched across Iowa and Illinois along a line from near Grinnell, Iowa, to near Wapello, Iowa, to just north of Galesburg, Illinois.  In that narrow axis, 7 to 10 inches of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather spotters reported 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system tracked east across the eastern Great Plains into northern Missouri and Illinois on from Feb. 8-9, 2018, bringing widespread snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. The heaviest snowfall fell along the Highway 20 corridor, where amounts of 6 to 8 inches were reported in Independence, Dubuque, and Freeport. To the south, around 2 to 5 inches fell from the Interstate 80 corridor to Highway 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 6.0 inches of snow in Asbury, Iowa. Amounts at the Dubuque Regional Airport totals up to 4.3 inches with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of snowfall across northwest Iowa.  Intensity waned for a short time in the afternoon and early evening, allowing a brief change to light freezing drizzle. Snowfall varied from 1 to 5 inches, with greatest amounts closer to the Minnesota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle occurred at times between 1300CST and 1800CST, but mainly snowfall occurred resulting in accumulations of 4.0 inches at Primghar and 3.3 inches at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across northeast Nebraska. Much of the area received from 3 to 6 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped to around zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 4.0 inches at Sheldon, Orange City, Rock Valley and 3 miles WNW of Everly, 3.6 inches at Rock Rapids and Spirit Lake, 3.5 inches at Akron, 3.2 inches at Sioux City and 2.5 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms early in the morning of March 5 produced a damaging lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike early in the morning of March 5 started a fire which burned down a corn crib.  The resulting fire damaged an auger and two farm trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall, along with runoff from melting snow from March 23-24, pushed much of the Little Sioux River back above flood stage after a few days below flood stage.  Reaches from Milford (MIFI4) to Spencer (SPWI4) to Linn Grove (LNNI4) again had waters invade mainly agricultural lowlands, along with city greenways in Spencer and Sioux Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt runoff on top of already high river levels produced minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along upper portions of the Little Sioux River. River levels reached 0.9 feet above flood stage near Milford (gage site MIFI4) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became heavy at times in the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 6 to 9 inches, including 9.0 inches at Sibley, 7.0 inches 4 miles east of Sioux Rapids, 6.5 inches at Spencer and near Everly, 6.2 inches at Sanborn and 6.0 inches at Primghar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms across northwest Iowa during the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became moderate to briefly heavy by late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 3 to 6 inches, including 5.0 inches at Cherokee, 4.5 inches at Rock Rapids, 4.1 inches at Washta, and 4.0 inches at Orange City, Sioux Center and Holstein and 3.2 inches at Remsen.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became heavy at times in the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 6 to 9 inches, including 9.0 inches at Sibley, 7.0 inches 4 miles east of Sioux Rapids, 6.5 inches at Spencer and near Everly, 6.2 inches at Sanborn and 6.0 inches at Primghar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall, along with runoff from melting snow from March 23-24, pushed much of the Little Sioux River back above flood stage after a few days below flood stage.  Reaches from Milford (MIFI4) to Spencer (SPWI4) to Linn Grove (LNNI4) again had waters invade mainly agricultural lowlands, along with city greenways in Spencer and Sioux Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt runoff on top of already high river levels produced minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along middle portions of the Little Sioux River. River levels reached 0.4 feet above flood stage near Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An absolutely bitter arctic air mass entrenched itself across the Midwest a couple of days prior to the New Year and continued to start 2018.  Air temperatures well into the negative teens to mid 20s were seen across Iowa throughout the event.  Fortunately, with the broad arctic high over the area, winds were not extreme, but were enough to drop wind chills well into the negative 30s and even negative 40s at times.  Suffice it to say, it was a prolonged stretch of dangerously cold air temperatures and wind chills.

A low pressure system was working it's way off the Rockies and across Kansas through January 21st, deepening along the way.  Late on the 21st and on through the 22nd the surface low turned NE and tracked across the SE portions of Iowa, leaving NW Iowa in the cross hairs for heavy snowfall and blizzard potential.  As the day progressed on the 22nd, an area of heavy snowfall developed over far NW Iowa where as much as a foot or more of snow fell in conjunction with gusty winds in excess of 40 mph and even 50 mph at times, resulting in prolonged periods of visibilities of less than 1/4 mile, especially during the morning and early afternoon hours.  A sharp snowfall gradient was seen towards the SE as amounts quickly fell to just a couple of inches or less within a county or two of the heaviest snowfall.

Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced minor flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 1.7 feet above flood stage at Canton (gage site CBSS2) on April 29. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced moderate flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas. Several farmhouses were isolated by the high water levels. River levels reached 3.6 feet above flood stage at Akron (gage site AKRI4) on April 25. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Rock River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the minor flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced minor flooding with impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 0.9 feet above flood stage on the Rock River at Rock Rapids (gage site RAPI4) on April 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough again progressed through the Rockies and evolved to a compact closed mid-level circulation drifting across eastern Nebraska and Iowa on April 18. Weak instability resulted in some scattered thunder with sleet and snow from the lower Missouri River valley through northwest Iowa. Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 2 and 6 inches snowfall across areas of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0400CST. Visibility dropped to a  mile or less at times between 0530-0850CST. Snowfall totaled 2.5 inches at Sioux City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0300CST. Visibility dropped to a  mile or less at times between 0500-0900CST. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Holstein and 2.6 inches 3 miles northeast of Battle Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0400CST. Visibility dropped to a half mile or less at times between 0400-1000CST. Snowfall totaled 2.6 inches at Rock Rapids.

Weak instability resulted in some scattered thunder with sleet and snow from the lower Missouri River valley through northwest Iowa. Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 2 and 6 inches snowfall across areas of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0400CST. Visibility dropped to a half mile or less at times between 0510-0820CST. Snowfall totaled 5.4 inches at Remsen and 4.8 inches at Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt in late March worked through the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River, resulting in minor to moderate flooding of mainly agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March 2018, the river above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) slowly fell to below flood stage by April 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt in late March worked through the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River, resulting in minor to moderate flooding of mainly agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March 2018, the river crested at moderate flood level at Akron (gage site AKRI4) 2.5 feet above flood stage on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced moderate flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 3.4 feet above flood stage near Brandon (gage site BBSS2) on April 28. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced minor flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 1.7 feet above flood stage at Canton (gage site CBSS2) on April 29. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With arctic air in place over northeast Iowa, air temperatures dropped into the teens below zero during the morning of January 1st. When combined with wind speeds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chills of 35 to 40 below were common. These dangerously cold wind chills started in late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 40 below zero were common across Chickasaw County during the morning of January 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27. A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN. This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28. A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN. ||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a period of light wintry mix, snow developed and winds increased across far southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, far northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska. Snowfall generally totaled 3 to 5 inches, with 4 to 6 inches near southern Sioux Falls. Other snowfall totals were 5.9 inches at Worthington MN, 5.0 inches at Canton SD, 4.0 inches near Vermillion SD, 3.5 inches near Larchwood IA, 3.0 inches at Luverne, MN and 2.3 inches at Concord NE. Visibility was reduced to a mile or less while heavier snowfall occurred, and winds were gusting to 37 mph at Slayton, 38 mph at Luverne and 46 mph at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than an inch of snowfall occurred during the overnight and morning hours, but with winds gusting around 40 to 50 mph, visibility fell to a mile or less briefly creating travel difficulty. Strong wind gusts continued well through the daylight hours on January 11 and maintained some areas of blowing snow with the base of an inch or two snow cover in place prior to the new snowfall. Peak winds were measured at 52 mph at Sioux City, 45 mph at Le Mars, 44 mph at Sheldon and Storm Lake, and 43 mph at Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm passed through the region on January 22nd. The path of the storm brought rain and warm air to much of northeast Iowa. Rainfall amounts averaged a half inch to 1.25 inches, but with frozen ground, most of this ran off into area rivers and streams. This runoff, combined with temperatures in the middle to upper 30s, caused some ice jams and produced isolated flooding. Flooding occurred along the Turkey River in Clayton County and along several streams in Winneshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains caused flooding to occur west of Ridgeway. 240th Street at the intersection with 315th Avenue was closed because of the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jams and runoff from heavy rain caused flooding along Silver Creek southeast of Kendallville. County Highway W14 was closed because of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but snowfall was diminished quite a bit by downslope impacts in far northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 8.0 inches at Cherokee and 7.2 inches at Washta. Treacherous travel resulted as winds gusted to 30 to 35 mph and combined with snowfall to produce several hours of visibility of a half mile or less centered around 1200CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An absolutely bitter arctic air mass entrenched itself across the Midwest a couple of days prior to the New Year and continued to start 2018.  Air temperatures well into the negative teens to mid 20s were seen across Iowa throughout the event.  Fortunately, with the broad arctic high over the area, winds were not extreme, but were enough to drop wind chills well into the negative 30s and even negative 40s at times.  Suffice it to say, it was a prolonged stretch of dangerously cold air temperatures and wind chills.

Wind chills from -35 to -45 continued through the morning hours across northwest Iowa as a strong arctic surface ridge continued to slide into the Northern Plains. This continued the extreme cold which initiated during late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 7 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 20 below to 30 below zero to produce bitterly cold wind chills of -40 to -45 through the morning hours of New Year's Day.  Actual temperatures reached record cold at several locations maintaining over a century of records, including Le Mars (-23), 2 miles southwest of Sioux Center (-25), Spirit Lake (-26) and Sioux City (-25). As southerly return flow developed behind the retreating ridge, not as extreme wind chills of -20 to -30 occurred from the evening of January 1st into mid morning of January 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind chills from -35 to -45 continued through the morning hours across northwest Iowa as a strong arctic surface ridge continued to slide into the Northern Plains. This continued the extreme cold which initiated during late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 7 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 20 below to 30 below zero to produce bitterly cold wind chills of -40 to -45 through the morning hours of New Year's Day.  Actual temperatures reached record cold at several locations maintaining over a century of records, including Le Mars (-23), 2 miles southwest of Sioux Center (-25), Spirit Lake (-26) and Sioux City (-25). As southerly return flow developed behind the retreating ridge, not as extreme wind chills of -20 to -30 occurred from the evening of January 1st into mid morning of January 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a relatively mild winter day Sunday, a low pressure system sweeping in from Canada and the Dakotas ushered in an arctic air mass.  The cold front began its entry into Iowa around 6pm CST Sunday and quickly moved across Iowa by about 3am CST Monday.  Behind the front were gusty northwest winds, with gusts approaching 40 mph at times.  Temperatures also fell quickly from the low to mid 20s ahead of the front to the single digits and single digits below zero just a couple hours later.  The result was a long duration event with wind chills well below zero.  Northern areas experienced wind chill values of -25 degrees F or less for 24 hours or more, while southern areas saw said wind chills for around 10 hours.  The worst time frame for wind chills were from roughly 6am on the 15th through 6am on the 16th.

A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14. The system produced from one to two inches of snow through January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 30 to 45 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous to life threatening wind chills from -35 to -40.  A minimum wind chill of -35 occurred at Spencer. Wind chills colder than -20 persisted consistently through 1700CST January 16. One to two inches snow fell with 1 inch measured at Everly and 2 inches at Spirit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14. The system produced a half inch to inch and a half snowfall through January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 25 to 40 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous wind chills from -25 to -35. A minimum wind chill of -32 occurred at Sheldon and Storm Lake, -28 at Cherokee and Sioux City, -27 at Orange City and -26 at Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Snow and strong winds developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a weak downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to the South Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of light freezing rain during the morning of December 1, light to occasionally moderate snowfall produced accumulations from 2 to 5 inches across Lyon county, with greatest amounts in the southeast portions of the county.  Snowfall of 3.8 inches was reported near George. Hazardous travel resulted as winds gusted to 35 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a mile or less between 1200-1600CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but snowfall was diminished quite a bit by downslope impacts in far northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 6.6 inches at Akron, 6.5 inches at Remsen and 6.0 inches six miles west-southwest of Hinton. Treacherous travel resulted as winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a half mile or less at times between 1000-1600CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air rushed southward into the region behind light snow producing system on the evening of January 11 and through the morning of January 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds at 10 to 20 mph combined with near zero and subzero temperatures to produce wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero. Minimum wind chill values of -30 at Storm Lake, -29 at Sheldon, -27 at Cherokee and -23 at Orange City were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Initially warm enough for rain, colder air rushed in as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph and brought a rapid change to snow early on January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through late afternoon. More than 4 inches of snow blanketed northwest Iowa, highlighted by a band from Sioux City to Spirit Lake from 9 to 15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 0000CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 35 to 45 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced near zero visibility starting before daybreak, which continued into late afternoon.  Schools were closed across the area on January 22, and Sioux City closed public buildings.  Two jackknifed semi-trucks blocked Highway 20 east of Sioux City around 1200CST, about which time a tow ban was issued across all of northwest Iowa.  The wind and snowfall caused 70 power outages around Sioux City, affecting around 800 customers.  Winds peaked midday with a gust to 44 mph at Sioux Gateway airport. Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1700CST, but travel was not advised well into Monday night. Total snowfall was measured at 12.1 inches at Sioux City.

Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1700CST, but travel was not advised well into the night. Total snowfall was measured at 3.5 inches at Holstein.

A peak wind gust of 47 mph was measured at Le Mars at 0635CST. Schools were closed across the area on January 22.  Around 1200CST, a tow ban was issued across all of northwest Iowa due to deteriorated conditions.  Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1600CST, but travel was not advised well into the night. Total snowfall of 11.3 inches was measured at Remsen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Initially warm enough for rain, colder air rushed in as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph and brought a rapid change to snow early on January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through late afternoon. More than 4 inches of snow blanketed northwest Iowa, highlighted by a band from Sioux City to Spirit Lake from 9 to 15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow around 0600CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 35 to 45 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced visibility frequently a quarter mile or less starting around mid morning, which continued into late afternoon. A peak wind gust of 45 mph was measured at Cherokee at 0655CST. Schools were closed across the area on January 22. County plows were removed from roads around 0900CST. By 1200CST, a tow ban was issued across all of northwest Iowa due to deteriorated conditions.  Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1600CST, but travel was not advised well into the night. Total snowfall of 4.0 inches was measured at Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system was working it's way off the Rockies and across Kansas through January 21st, deepening along the way.  Late on the 21st and on through the 22nd the surface low turned NE and tracked across the SE portions of Iowa, leaving NW Iowa in the cross hairs for heavy snowfall and blizzard potential.  As the day progressed on the 22nd, an area of heavy snowfall developed over far NW Iowa where as much as a foot or more of snow fell in conjunction with gusty winds in excess of 40 mph and even 50 mph at times, resulting in prolonged periods of visibilities of less than 1/4 mile, especially during the morning and early afternoon hours.  A sharp snowfall gradient was seen towards the SE as amounts quickly fell to just a couple of inches or less within a county or two of the heaviest snowfall.

After a relatively mild winter day Sunday, a low pressure system sweeping in from Canada and the Dakotas ushered in an arctic air mass.  The cold front began its entry into Iowa around 6pm CST Sunday and quickly moved across Iowa by about 3am CST Monday.  Behind the front were gusty northwest winds, with gusts approaching 40 mph at times.  Temperatures also fell quickly from the low to mid 20s ahead of the front to the single digits and single digits below zero just a couple hours later.  The result was a long duration event with wind chills well below zero.  Northern areas experienced wind chill values of -25 degrees F or less for 24 hours or more, while southern areas saw said wind chills for around 10 hours.  The worst time frame for wind chills were from roughly 6am on the 15th through 6am on the 16th.

Arctic air rushed southward into the region behind light snow producing system on the evening of January 11 and through the morning of January 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blustery northerly winds at 15 to 25 mph combined with near zero and subzero temperatures to produce wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero. Minimum wind chill values of -30 at Storm Lake, -29 at Sheldon, -27 at Cherokee and -23 at Orange City were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering boundary continued to plague the area as it waffled across southern Iowa and northern Missouri. Prior to the evening/overnight convection, lingering convection from the previous night moved out of Nebraska and into Iowa during the morning hours of the 13th.  Nearly all hail reports were sub-severe, however a storm or two was able to produce quarter sized hail reports.  Later that evening, low level jet induced convection initiated in an environment devoid of much effective bulk shear but with relative high amounts of MUCAPE in excess of 2000 J/kg.  Resulting storms were predominantly sub-severe, but a few updrafts were able to produce quarter to half dollar sized hail at times in the early morning hours of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a wide range in hail size from peas to quarters.  Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across northwest Iowa.  While most precipitation was in the form of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle on February 19 which resulted in a 0.05 to 0.20 inch ice accumulation, a second round of precipitation early on February 20 brought a dusting of less than an inch of snow which coated the ice and produced hazardous travel conditions. For the two day period, school delays were common for those not already out for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

Black Hawk Creek at Hudson went into flood briefly for a few hours due to heavy rain and snow melt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Hawk Creek at Hudson crested at 14.28 feet on 28 February at 18:15 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of Colorado across east central Nebraska and produced snow and strong winds across northeast Nebraska. This created blowing snow conditions | and whiteout conditions across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Winds gusted to 41 mph at Harlan, and 40 mph at Council Bluffs and Shenandoah. Snowfall ranged from about 1 inch at Council Bluffs to 6 inches in Monona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer reported 2 inches of snow at Logan. An observer in Little Sioux, IA reported 6 inches of snow.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota.  While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across northwest Iowa, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts 2 to 5 inches were common, with around an inch or less from the lower Missouri River valley eastward toward Storm Lake.  Roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous due to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across northwest Iowa, slowing progression as it reached far northern and eastern portions during the afternoon. Snowfall amounts varied from 0.3 inches at Le Mars and Remsen, 0.7 inches near Battle Creek and 1.0 inch at Sioux City, to 3.0 inches at Primghar, 3.5 inches at Sibley, and 3.9 inches 5 miles west of Spirit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow over a period of several hours brought from 2 to 4 inches of snowfall across northwest Iowa. Higher amounts were measured near Sergeant Bluff, Sioux Center and Storm Lake (4.0 inches), Holstein (3.5 inches), Sheldon and Cherokee (3.0 inches), near Spencer (2.9 inches), Rock Rapids and Le Mars (2.8 inches), Sibley (2.5 inches) and Lake Park (2.3 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary surface front set up just south of the Iowa-Missouri border and remained as such into the early morning hours before high pressure ushered the front southward.  Above the surface, the 850 mb front was situated further north into Iowa, setting up good isentropic lift.  Additionally, slantwise instability and a shortwave moving through the mid-upper level flow provided an additional boost to lift.  In all snofall fell across much of the state from just north of I-80 to the Minnesota border.  The heaviest amounts resided roughly between the Highway 20 and 30 corridors where as much as around 10 inches fell.

A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of snowfall across northwest Iowa.  Intensity waned for a short time in the afternoon and early evening, allowing a brief change to light freezing drizzle. Snowfall varied from 1 to 5 inches, with greatest amounts closer to the Minnesota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle occurred at times between 1300CST and 1600CST, but mainly snowfall occurred resulting in accumulation of 2.5 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A roughly NE to SW oriented cold front that was slowly progressing eastward stalled out about half way across the state, awaiting storms to fire up along and ahead of it during the afternoon and evening hours. Similar to previous days, the convective environment was not tremendous, but was enough to warrant at least limited concern.  MUCAPE values approached 1500 - 2000 J/kg by the afternoon hours, especially over the southern parts of the state, along with bulk shear values around 30 to 40 kts by mid to late afternoon. As a result, a few strong to severe storms initiated by mid-afternoon and produced periods of severe hail into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rain and quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering boundary continued to plague the area as it waffled across southern Iowa and northern Missouri. Prior to the evening/overnight convection, lingering convection from the previous night moved out of Nebraska and into Iowa during the morning hours of the 13th.  Nearly all hail reports were sub-severe, however a storm or two was able to produce quarter sized hail reports.  Later that evening, low level jet induced convection initiated in an environment devoid of much effective bulk shear but with relative high amounts of MUCAPE in excess of 2000 J/kg.  Resulting storms were predominantly sub-severe, but a few updrafts were able to produce quarter to half dollar sized hail at times in the early morning hours of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter relayed report of quarter sized hail.  The report came via social media and the location was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving through the mid-upper level flow entered and traversed Iowa from roughly NW to SE throughout the day on the 5th.  After quickly saturating the atmosphere, period of moderate to heavy snow fell across large parts of central Iowa. In the end, numerous locations reported receiving between 4 and 7 inches of snowfall.

An upper level weather system moved across eastern Nebraska and produced a fairly narrow band of moderate snowfall on February of 1 to 5 inches of snow from Saline and Seward Counties in Nebraska to Harrison County and Pottawattamie Counties in Iowa. The heaviest amounts included 2.7 inches at 68th and Dodge in Omaha, 3 inches west of Oakland, 4 inches at Scribner, NE , and 5 inches southwest of Dunlap, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of 3 inches of snow 3 miles west of Oakland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system moved across eastern Nebraska and produced a fairly narrow band of moderate snowfall on February of 1 to 5 inches of snow from Saline and Seward Counties in Nebraska to Harrison County and Pottawattamie Counties in Iowa. The heaviest amounts included 2.7 inches at 68th and Dodge in Omaha, 3 inches west of Oakland, 4 inches at Scribner, NE , and 5 inches southwest of Dunlap, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative weather observer reported 5 inches of snow.

As with many snowfall events, a sizable low pressure system slid south of Iowa roughly from central Kansas into central and southeast Missouri.  As a result, Iowa was left solidly within the precipitation shield as warm air was brought up over the warm front and wrapped back into the low pressure system.  Temperatures ahead of the system were in the upper 30s to low 40s in most cases.  As temperatures cooled from infiltrating cold air rain turned over to snow, heavy at times. Snowfall generally began after 10 pm on the 23rd and lasted through much of the morning on the 24th before tapering off from northwest to southeast.  The heaviest snowfall amounts were seen over northern Iowa from about Estherville to Mason City and down to Waterloo, where as much as 12 to 18 inches of snow fell.  Areas further south saw a slower transition to snow and fewer bouts of heavy snowfall, resulting in amounts around 4 to 8 inches on the southern edges of the winter storm warning. However a period of freezing rain did lead to some ice accumulations in areas along Highway 30 prior to the period of snow.

Snowfall generally began after 10 pm on the 23rd and lasted through much of the morning on the 24th before tapering off from northwest to southeast.  The heaviest snowfall amounts were seen over northern Iowa from about Estherville to Mason City and down to Waterloo, where as much as 12 to 18 inches of snow fell.  Areas further south saw a slower transition to snow and fewer bouts of heavy snowfall, resulting in amounts around 4 to 8 inches on the southern edges of the winter storm warning. However a period of freezing rain did lead to some ice accumulations in areas along Highway 30 prior to the period of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge briefly went into minor flood in early March from some snowmelt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge crested at 14.61 ft on 5 March 2018 at 0645 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a rather extraordinary event, a small area, likely around 100 square miles or less, received heavy snowfall of around 6 to 8 inches.  The heaviest snowfall was concentrated between Ames and Des Moines, roughly centered over Interstate 35.  Towns in the path included Slater, Huxley, Cambridge, and Alleman.  Travel just a few miles in any direction, and snowfall amounts fell to around 2 inches or less.  ||The general setup had a low pressure system sliding southeast of Iowa on a path from eastern Nebraska into central Missouri. A relatively typical winter setup, leaving areas of Iowa in line for precipitation (liquid and frozen) as warm air lifted over the warm front and wrapped around/into the low pressure system.  Smaller-scale banded features prevailed in effectively training heavy snowfall over the same area for a number of hours in the early morning hours of the 17th.

Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across northeast Nebraska. Much of the area received from 3 to 6 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped to around zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 4.0 inches at Sheldon, Orange City, Rock Valley and 3 miles WNW of Everly, 3.6 inches at Rock Rapids and Spirit Lake, 3.5 inches at Akron, 3.2 inches at Sioux City and 2.5 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow over a period of several hours brought from 2 to 4 inches of snowfall across northwest Iowa. Higher amounts were measured near Sergeant Bluff, Sioux Center and Storm Lake (4.0 inches), Holstein (3.5 inches), Sheldon and Cherokee (3.0 inches), near Spencer (2.9 inches), Rock Rapids and Le Mars (2.8 inches), Sibley (2.5 inches) and Lake Park (2.3 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain on February 19th and 20th produced a coating of ice on untreated surfaces across northeast Iowa. The precipitation fell with temperatures below freezing as a slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Missouri River Valley toward the Great Lakes. Ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch were reported in several locations and most schools were either closed or delayed on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Untreated surfaces became coated in ice as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle and rain occurred across Mitchell County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary surface front set up just south of the Iowa-Missouri border and remained as such into the early morning hours before high pressure ushered the front southward.  Above the surface, the 850 mb front was situated further north into Iowa, setting up good isentropic lift.  Additionally, slantwise instability and a shortwave moving through the mid-upper level flow provided an additional boost to lift.  In all snofall fell across much of the state from just north of I-80 to the Minnesota border.  The heaviest amounts resided roughly between the Highway 20 and 30 corridors where as much as around 10 inches fell.

A shortwave moving through the mid-upper level flow entered and traversed Iowa from roughly NW to SE throughout the day on the 5th.  After quickly saturating the atmosphere, period of moderate to heavy snow fell across large parts of central Iowa. In the end, numerous locations reported receiving between 4 and 7 inches of snowfall.

Quickly on the heels of a sizable snowstorm across central and northern Iowa, another shortwave quickly moved through the mid and upper level flow. While low level support was tough to come by with cold air advection on going, the shortwave was coincident with a saturated dendridic growth zone that resulted in widespread light to moderate snowfall, including a couple localized 6 to 7 inch reports.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota.  While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across northwest Iowa, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts 2 to 5 inches were common, with around an inch or less from the lower Missouri River valley eastward toward Storm Lake.  Roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous due to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across northwest Iowa, slowing progression as it reached far northern and eastern portions during the afternoon. Snowfall amounts varied from 0.3 inches at Le Mars and Remsen, 0.7 inches near Battle Creek and 1.0 inch at Sioux City, to 3.0 inches at Primghar, 3.5 inches at Sibley, and 3.9 inches 5 miles west of Spirit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two sisters were killed in a two vehicle accident north of Decorah (Winneshiek County) on February 5th. Roads were still partially snow covered from snow that fell two days earlier on February 3rd. The driver of one vehicle lost control on the slippery roads, crossed the center line and was sliding sideways when struck by the second vehicle. The two sisters that were killed were in the first vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sisters were killed in a two vehicle accident that occurred on slippery roads as a result of snow that fell two days prior to the accident. Roads were still partially snow covered and slippery at the time of the accident. The driver of one vehicle lost control, crossed the center line and was sliding sideways when struck by the second vehicle. The two sisters that were killed were in the first vehicle along with three brothers who all sustained injuries in the accident. The driver of the second vehicle also was injured as a result of the crash.

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota.  While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across northwest Iowa, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts 2 to 5 inches were common, with around an inch or less from the lower Missouri River valley eastward toward Storm Lake.  Roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous due to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across northwest Iowa, slowing progression as it reached far northern and eastern portions during the afternoon. Snowfall amounts varied from 0.3 inches at Le Mars and Remsen, 0.7 inches near Battle Creek and 1.0 inch at Sioux City, to 3.0 inches at Primghar, 3.5 inches at Sibley, and 3.9 inches 5 miles west of Spirit Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: To open up the month of May, Iowa saw a pair of thunderstorm rounds move through. First, storms that initiated the afternoon and evening prior in central and eastern Nebraska and progressed into and across Iowa overnight and through the morning of the 1st.  That first line of storms was primarily elevated in nature within an environment of 1000+ J/kg MUCAPE and lackluster effective bulk shear, resulting in mostly sub-severe hail and wind gusts. As the environment cleared out during the morning hours, MUCAPE values approached 2000 J/kg near the cold front across northwest and western Iowa. Effective bulk shear values remained lackluster, under 35 kts in most cases.  Resulting storms that fired up near and ahead of the surface cold front during the mid-afternoon hours generally struggled to sustain rotating updrafts, but were able to drop multiple instances of severe hail, approaching 2 inches in diameter occasionally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail via the mPING application.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A roughly NE to SW oriented cold front that was slowly progressing eastward stalled out about half way across the state, awaiting storms to fire up along and ahead of it during the afternoon and evening hours. Similar to previous days, the convective environment was not tremendous, but was enough to warrant at least limited concern.  MUCAPE values approached 1500 - 2000 J/kg by the afternoon hours, especially over the southern parts of the state, along with bulk shear values around 30 to 40 kts by mid to late afternoon. As a result, a few strong to severe storms initiated by mid-afternoon and produced periods of severe hail into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system tracked east across the eastern Great Plains into northern Missouri and Illinois on from Feb. 8-9, 2018, bringing widespread snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. The heaviest snowfall fell along the Highway 20 corridor, where amounts of 6 to 8 inches were reported in Independence, Dubuque, and Freeport. To the south, around 2 to 5 inches fell from the Interstate 80 corridor to Highway 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 6.5 inches of snow 1 mile east northeast of Independence, Iowa. A report of 8 inches was received 2 miles north northeast of Hazelton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another upper level storm system tracked east across the Plains and into Iowa the morning of February 10th, bringing snow to portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Snow covered roads and reduced visibilities were seen as the snow fell between midnight and 10 am.  Snowfall totals between 1 and 4 inches were common, with a few locally higher amounts in Keokuk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 7.9 inches of snow at Sigourney, Iowa and 6.0 inches reported 3 miles west of Richland, Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary surface front set up just south of the Iowa-Missouri border and remained as such into the early morning hours before high pressure ushered the front southward.  Above the surface, the 850 mb front was situated further north into Iowa, setting up good isentropic lift.  Additionally, slantwise instability and a shortwave moving through the mid-upper level flow provided an additional boost to lift.  In all snofall fell across much of the state from just north of I-80 to the Minnesota border.  The heaviest amounts resided roughly between the Highway 20 and 30 corridors where as much as around 10 inches fell.

A low pressure system was slowly working its way into and through the Upper Plains, while high pressure lingered over the Ohio Valley.  As a result, a tightening surface pressure gradient was seen across the western half of Iowa that resulted in widespread gusts into the 40 to 50 mph range.  A single site recorded a 50 knot gust and the same site nearly recorded sustained winds of 40 mph for an hour or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS recorded a 58 mph or 50 knot wind gust, as well as sustained winds around 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain on February 19th and 20th produced a coating of ice on untreated surfaces across northeast Iowa. The precipitation fell with temperatures below freezing as a slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Missouri River Valley toward the Great Lakes. Ice accumulations up to a tenth of an inch were reported in several locations and most schools were either closed or delayed on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Untreated surfaces became coated in ice as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle and rain occurred across Floyd County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough again progressed through the Rockies and evolved to a compact closed mid-level circulation drifting across eastern Nebraska and Iowa on April 18. Weak instability resulted in some scattered thunder with sleet and snow from the lower Missouri River valley through northwest Iowa. Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 5 and 10 inches snowfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation started in the early morning hours, but changed rapidly to snow after 0400CST.  Visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less at times between 0530-0930CST.|Snowfall totalled 6.5 inches 4 miles east of Sioux Rapids and 5.5 inches at Storm Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms. Not included in this episode was the spotty severe hail and wind which occurred in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota prior to the onset of snowfall during the morning and afternoon of April 13. |Eventually, influx of colder air would change all precipitation over to snow, and ushered in a record-breaking snowfall of 8 to 16 inches (some locations over 20 inches) and fierce winds gusting 50 to 60 mph to southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa and extreme northeast Nebraska. Snow drifts over 6 feet were reported in many locations. Extremely low visibility persisted for almost a day for most locations, making travel very difficult if not impossible. ||One person was killed in Rock County when the snowmobile he was riding collided with an interstate guard rail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life threatening conditions developed during the morning of April 14, as the earlier mix of rain, sleet and snow changed to all snow, and brutal winds (gusting as high as 48 mph at Sioux City at 42 mph near Moville) whipped visibility to less than a quarter mile at times through the evening of April 14. Travel was not recommended for much of the two day period, if not impossible. A storm total snowfall of 5.7 inches was measured at Sioux City with 3.0 inches near Holly Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed after 0200CST, becoming widespread as visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less at times between 0545-0755CST. Snowfall totalled 7.0 inches at Cherokee and 4 miles north of Cleghorn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of snowfall developed in the early morning hours of April 3, continuing at times through the afternoon. Winds shifted northwest and increased substantially with gusts of 30 to 40 mph at times through the afternoon. Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Sheldon and 3.0 inches at Primghar and Sanborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow developed across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of March 23rd. This band remained nearly stationary for several hours before dissipating during the afternoon of the 24th. The band produced snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches an hour that quickly caused roads to become snow covered. Snowfall amounts approached a foot and half in some locations. A very tight gradient existed on the north side of the band where snowfall amounts quickly dropped to little or nothing. The highest reported amounts were 16 inches near Osage (Mitchell County) and Rockford (Floyd County) and 17 inches near Nora Springs (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 16 inches of snow fell across Mitchell County. The highest reported total was 16 inches near Osage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed quickly in the morning and remained heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 6.7 inches at Spirit Lake and 6.1 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3. A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed after daybreak on April 3, with winds increasing substantially and gusting to 30 to 40 mph through early evening. Visibility dropped to a half mile to a mile from 1100-1800CST, creating hazardous travel conditions and a few spots with travel not recommended across northwest Iowa near the Minnesota border. Snowfall amassed to 6.0 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed quickly in the morning and remained heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 7.5 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly and 7.2 inches at Spencer and 6.0 inches at Peterson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow developed across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of March 23rd. This band remained nearly stationary for several hours before dissipating during the afternoon of the 24th. The band produced snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches an hour that quickly caused roads to become snow covered. Snowfall amounts approached a foot and half in some locations. A very tight gradient existed on the north side of the band where snowfall amounts quickly dropped to little or nothing. The highest reported amounts were 16 inches near Osage (Mitchell County) and Rockford (Floyd County) and 17 inches near Nora Springs (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 13 to 17 inches of snow fell across Floyd County. The highest reported total was 17 inches near Nora Springs. Travel along the Avenue of the Saints (U.S. Highway 218) was reduced to one lane for most of the day with several vehicles stuck in the middle of the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville crested at 10.22 feet on 29 March 2018 at 1800 UTC.

Marginally warmer temperatures aloft allowed for some light mixed precipitation early on April 18, but a quick transition to snow occurred in the pre-dawn hours, leaving behind between 2 and 6 inches snowfall across areas of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0600CST. Visibility dropped to a half mile or less at times between 0630-1200CST. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Sibley and 5.5 inches at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small part of northeast Iowa was covered with 4 to 6 inches of snow on March 5th. In addition to the snow, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph created near white-out conditions at times with lots of blowing and drifting snow. Most schools in the area were closed on the 5th and the 6th. The highest reported snow amount was 6 inches in Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Waukon. Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph created near white-out conditions as times from blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern Iowa found itself in the cross hairs of yet another April winter storm, the 4th to this point.  A surface low pressure system slid off the Rockies on the 17th and worked its way across northern Kansas.  By the early morning hours on the 18th, the surface low tracked into central Missouri, placing Iowa north of the surface low and in line for showers, strong storms, and snow from the broad lift associated with warm air advection north of the warm front.  A few strong to severe storms were seen prior to snowfall onset, with temperatures were generally right around freezing. Warm air aloft also contributed to a tricky transition to snow forecast.  After initial thunderstorms and rain, snowfall began around 6 to 7 am across much of northern Iowa.  By the time precipitation moved out during the late afternoon and evening, as much as a foot of snow fell near the Minnesota border. Accumulating snow was seen as far south as about Highway 30.  ||Note: Another event was created for this day for the severe weather aspect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt in late March worked through the upper and middle portions of the Little Sioux River, resulting in minor and mainly agricultural land flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March 2018, the river crested at minor flood level at Milford (gage site MIFI4) 0.9 feet above flood stage on April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa received between 2 and 5 inches of snow as another early spring storm moved across the Upper Midwest from April 13th into the 15th. In addition to the falling snow, additional impacts occurred as wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph produced blowing and drifting snow that significantly reduced the visibility at times. The highest reported snowfall total was 5.4 inches in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 49 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 3 inches of snow fell across Floyd County. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph created additional impacts with blowing and drifting snow that significantly reduced the visibility at times. The highest recorded wind gust was 49 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with rain, freezing rain, and sleet was seen across the area during the morning of Wednesday, April 18th; especially north and west of a line from Ottumwa to Dubuque. This resulted in slick roads and hazardous travel. Towards midday, the mixed precipitation had changed to snow north of Interstate 80. Snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches were reported along and north of Highway 20.  .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.5 inches 4 miles west northwest of Fountain Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system ejected off of the Rockies and into SE Colorado late on April 2nd and began an eastward track across southern Kansas and central Missouri. With Iowa firmly north of the surface low, concerns revolved around cold air and snowfall potential with the deformation band on the back side of the surface low followed by a quick hitting shortwave. As the event unfolded throughout April 3rd, northern Iowa found itself within the best environment to see periods of heavy snowfall. By the end of the event, a few spots in northern Iowa saw up to around 8 inches of heavy snowfall.

With Iowa firmly north of the surface low, concerns revolved around cold air and snowfall potential with the deformation band on the back side of the surface low followed by a quick hitting shortwave. As the event unfolded throughout April 3rd, northern Iowa found itself within the best environment to see periods of heavy snowfall. By the end of the event, a few spots in northern Iowa saw up to around 8 inches of heavy snowfall.

Northern Iowa found itself in the cross hairs of yet another April winter storm, the 4th to this point.  A surface low pressure system slid off the Rockies on the 17th and worked its way across northern Kansas.  By the early morning hours on the 18th, the surface low tracked into central Missouri, placing Iowa north of the surface low and in line for showers, strong storms, and snow from the broad lift associated with warm air advection north of the warm front.  A few strong to severe storms were seen prior to snowfall onset, with temperatures were generally right around freezing. Warm air aloft also contributed to a tricky transition to snow forecast.  After initial thunderstorms and rain, snowfall began around 6 to 7 am across much of northern Iowa.  By the time precipitation moved out during the late afternoon and evening, as much as a foot of snow fell near the Minnesota border. Accumulating snow was seen as far south as about Highway 30.  ||Note: Another event was created for this day for the winter weather aspect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel to quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight low pressure system tracked its way out of north central Kansas on the 12th and slowly meandered northeast near the Nebraska, Kansas, Iowa borders by the early afternoon on the 13th.  A relatively stationary warm front draped itself across far southern Iowa, separating temperatures in the upper 70s to the south and 50s to low 60s to the north of the boundary respectively by the afternoon.  With the triple point in west to southwest Iowa, storms had little issue initiating by the mid-afternoon hours, quickly growing downstream both south and north of the warm front. With much of the Iowa north of the front, storms were elevated in nature, but produced large amounts of hail and damaging winds. The severe reports began just before 4pm and storms continued to remain near-severe to severe well into the evening, eventually transitioning away from hail as the primary threat and towards damaging winds.  Even as the severe storm threat diminished very strong winds remained, including gusts in excess of 60 mph, in the presence of a very tight surface pressure gradient. The same system produced blizzard conditions across northern Iowa on the 14th. Please see the separate entry for the blizzard portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCIM Radio in Carroll relayed listener report of roof damage at McAtee Tire.  Also reported power outages. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong late season winter storms brought a mix of additional heavy snowfall and heavy rainfall to northwest Iowa after April 8.  River levels already high from earlier melt and rainfall quickly returned to flood, with many continuing in flood through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned to the Little Sioux River in mid-April, and was generally minor with impacts limited to near river agricultural areas. Periodic rain and snow brought 1 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent in the week prior to a return to flood stage at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) on April 17. The river crested in moderate flood stage (1.5 feet above flood stage) on April 26, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain as well as the city park in Sioux Rapids. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding returned to portions of Little Sioux River in late April, with impact generally limited to near river agricultural areas. Periodic rain and snow brought 1 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent in the two weeks prior to a crest at minor flood stage near Montgomery (gage site MSLI4) on April 25 (0.1 feet above flood stage).

Just a few days after a snowfall event, northern Iowa found itself in the cross hairs once again. This time a low pressure system was slowly making its way southeast through South Dakota, eastern Nebraska, and southwest Iowa. As a result, much of Iowa found itself in the warm air advection and wrap around wings of the surface low. Accumulating snowfall was reported as far south as about Highway 30, while the heaviest snowfall was kept north of Highway 20. As much as 7 to 9 inches of snow fell in areas in and around Cerro Gordo, Emmet, and Winnebago counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa were covered with up to 6 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region. Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day or released early on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 6 inches of snow fell across Howard County. The highest reported total was 6.3 inches in Elma.

A potent low pressure system meandered its way across the central Plains on the 13th through 15th, eventually occluding, as it tracked eastward along the Nebraska-Kansas and Iowa-Missouri borders.  Because of its slow movement and tight surface pressure gradient, blizzard conditions were seen over large parts of northwest and northern Iowa during the day of the 14th of April. The worst blizzard conditions lasted roughly from early to mid morning through mid to late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were quite tremendous in spots, with as much as 12 to 16 inches reported in a number of locations across far northern Iowa. The same system produced severe weather across central and southern on the 13th. Please see the separate entry for the severe weather portion of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing drizzle and a resulting light glaze followed a light dusting of snowfall to create hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cooler air locked in near the surface, progression of a mid-level wave from the west during the daytime hours of November 28 increased warm advection atop the cold layer, resulting in periods of light freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in a light accumulation of ice on roadways and treacherous driving conditions.  A single-car rollover accident occurred 1 mile south of George on County Road L14 at 1350CST when the vehicle lost control on an ice-covered bridge, crossed the center line and rolled into the opposite ditch.  The driver (50-year old female) and passenger (46-year-old male) were both injured (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cooler air locked in near the surface, progression of a mid-level wave from the west during the daytime hours of November 28 increased warm advection atop the cold layer, resulting in periods of light freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, mixed at times with a little sleet, created hazardous road conditions. A few minor accidents were reported around Sioux Center and Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some of the first thunderstorms of the year dropped hail on portions of northeast Iowa. Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across the region during the morning and evening of April 13th. Most of the hail was sub-severe, but some quarter sized hail was reported near Ossian (Winneshiek County) in the morning. Quarter sized hail also fell in the evening near Marble Rock (Floyd County), New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and Cresco (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell near Ossian.

Snowfall amounts were quite tremendous in spots, with as much as 12 to 16 inches reported in a number of locations across far northern Iowa. The same system produced severe weather across central and southern on the 13th. Please see the separate entry for the severe weather portion of this event.

A tight low pressure system tracked its way out of north central Kansas on the 12th and slowly meandered northeast near the Nebraska, Kansas, Iowa borders by the early afternoon on the 13th.  A relatively stationary warm front draped itself across far southern Iowa, separating temperatures in the upper 70s to the south and 50s to low 60s to the north of the boundary respectively by the afternoon.  With the triple point in west to southwest Iowa, storms had little issue initiating by the mid-afternoon hours, quickly growing downstream both south and north of the warm front. With much of the Iowa north of the front, storms were elevated in nature, but produced large amounts of hail and damaging winds. The severe reports began just before 4pm and storms continued to remain near-severe to severe well into the evening, eventually transitioning away from hail as the primary threat and towards damaging winds.  Even as the severe storm threat diminished very strong winds remained, including gusts in excess of 60 mph, in the presence of a very tight surface pressure gradient. The same system produced blizzard conditions across northern Iowa on the 14th. Please see the separate entry for the blizzard portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report from media in Atlantic of damage to outbuildings west of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some of the first thunderstorms of the year dropped hail on portions of northeast Iowa. Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across the region during the morning and evening of April 13th. Most of the hail was sub-severe, but some quarter sized hail was reported near Ossian (Winneshiek County) in the morning. Quarter sized hail also fell in the evening near Marble Rock (Floyd County), New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and Cresco (Howard County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong late season winter storms brought a mix of additional heavy snowfall and heavy rainfall to northwest Iowa after April 8.  River levels already high from earlier melt and rainfall quickly returned to flood, with many continuing in flood through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned to the Little Sioux River in mid-April, and was generally minor with impacts limited to near river agricultural areas. Periodic rain and snow brought 1 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent in the week prior to a return to flood stage near Milford (gage site MIFI4) on April 14.  The river crested in minor flood stage (0.8 feet above flood stage) on April 16, before briefly dropping below flood stage on April 19. The river returned quickly above flood stage on April 20 and crested in moderate flood stage 2.8 feet above flood stage on April 26, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14. Much of the river remained in flood at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall produced moderate flooding which produced significant impact to lowland agricultural areas, and inundated SD Highway 46 for several days. River levels reached 5.9 feet above flood stage above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) on April 24. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter estimated a 60 mph wind gust in Harlan.
