EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third spring storm of April dropped a band of heavy snow on portions of northeast Iowa on April 18th. Amounts of 5 to 13 inches were reported and the highest reported total was 12.8 inches in Stacyville (Mitchell County). With snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches, roads quickly became snow covered and slippery, but given the time of year, melting started soon after the snow stopped and the roads were promptly cleared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 10 inches of snow fell across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 10 inches near Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Part I: Several rivers went into flood as heavy rainfall, heavy snow and resultant melting snow all led to higher streamflows in the latter half of April across portions of central and northern Iowa. Flooding at 3 locations including the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg and Humboldt remained above flood stage into May 2018. Some of the rivers went in and out of flood a few times during the period. There are 3 parts to this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River crested at 12.01 feet on 30 April 2018 at 01:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with rain, freezing rain, and sleet was seen across the area during the morning of Wednesday, April 18th; especially north and west of a line from Ottumwa to Dubuque. This resulted in slick roads and hazardous travel. Towards midday, the mixed precipitation had changed to snow north of Interstate 80. Snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches were reported along and north of Highway 20.  .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 2.0 inches measured at the Dubuque Regional Airport to 2.5 inches by a trained spotter 1 mile west of Grove Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winnebago River at Mason City crested at 10.72 feet on 14 April 2018 at 16:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong late season winter storms brought a mix of additional heavy snowfall and heavy rainfall to northwest Iowa after April 8.  River levels already high from earlier melt and rainfall quickly returned to flood, with many continuing in flood through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned to the Little Sioux River and tributaries in mid-April, and was generally minor with impacts limited to near river agricultural areas. Periodic rain and snow brought 1 to 3 inches of liquid equivalent in the week prior to a return to flood stage on the Ocheyedan River near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) on April 15. The river crested in minor flood stage (0.7 feet above flood stage) on April 15, before quickly  dropping below flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong late season winter storms brought a mix of additional heavy snowfall and heavy rainfall to northwest Iowa after April 8. River levels already high from earlier melt and rainfall quickly returned to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned to the Ocheyedan River in late April, with minor impacts to near river agricultural areas. Snow melt following the mid month blizzard brought a return to flood stage near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) on April 22. The river crested in minor flood stage (1.0 feet above flood stage) on April 24, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong late season winter storms brought a mix of additional heavy snowfall and heavy rainfall to northwest Iowa after April 8. River levels already high from earlier melt and rainfall quickly returned to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upstream runoff of snow melt and rainfall brought flooding to the lower portions of the Little Sioux River in late April.  The flooding near Cherokee (gage site CHKI4) was minor with impacts limited to near river agricultural areas.  The river crested in minor flood stage (0.7 feet above flood stage) on April 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds ahead of southeastward moving cold front in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 49 mph was measured at Harlan Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the area brought rain changing to snow on April 3.  The heaviest snow fell along the Highway 20 corridor where 1 to 2 inches of snow was common with less than an inch south to the Highway 30 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Manchester reported 1.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the area brought rain changing to snow on April 3.  The heaviest snow fell along the Highway 20 corridor where 1 to 2 inches of snow was common with less than an inch south to the Highway 30 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation at the Dubuque Regional Airport was 2.6 inches. Precipitation began as freezing rain that coated elevated surfaces in a glaze of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with rain, freezing rain, and sleet was seen across the area during the morning of Wednesday, April 18th; especially north and west of a line from Ottumwa to Dubuque. This resulted in slick roads and hazardous travel. Towards midday, the mixed precipitation had changed to snow north of Interstate 80. Snowfall accumulations of 1 to 3 inches were reported along and north of Highway 20.  .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer reports ranged from 0.6 inches in Cedar Rapids to Coggon reported 1.9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Part III: Several rivers went into flood as heavy rainfall, heavy snow and resultant melting snow all led to higher streamflows in the latter half of April across portions of central and northern Iowa. Flooding at 3 locations including the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg and Humboldt remained above flood stage into May 2018. Some of the rivers went in and out of flood a few times during the period. There are 2 parts to this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg crested at 11.39 feet on 27 April 2018 at 3:00 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville remained in flood from the previous month through much of the beginning of April, finally going back below flood stage on the 9th of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville crested at 8.96 feet on 4 April 2018 at 1:00 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from Wyoming across Nebraska to northwest Missouri and produced snow across northeast Nebraska and Southwest Iowa.  Snowfall amounts were light ranging from less than 0.5 inch in Pottawattamie County to 2 inches in Monona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Harrison County, snowfall ranged from 0.5 to 2 inches.  An observer reported 2 inches of snow near Little Sioux, IA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northeast Iowa were covered with up to 6 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region. Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day or released early on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 6.5 inches in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across Floyd County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight low pressure system tracked its way out of north central Kansas on the 12th and slowly meandered northeast near the Nebraska, Kansas, Iowa borders by the early afternoon on the 13th.  A relatively stationary warm front draped itself across far southern Iowa, separating temperatures in the upper 70s to the south and 50s to low 60s to the north of the boundary respectively by the afternoon.  With the triple point in west to southwest Iowa, storms had little issue initiating by the mid-afternoon hours, quickly growing downstream both south and north of the warm front. With much of the Iowa north of the front, storms were elevated in nature, but produced large amounts of hail and damaging winds. The severe reports began just before 4pm and storms continued to remain near-severe to severe well into the evening, eventually transitioning away from hail as the primary threat and towards damaging winds.  Even as the severe storm threat diminished very strong winds remained, including gusts in excess of 60 mph, in the presence of a very tight surface pressure gradient. The same system produced blizzard conditions across northern Iowa on the 14th. Please see the separate entry for the blizzard portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel sized hail.

The severe reports began just before 4pm and storms continued to remain near-severe to severe well into the evening, eventually transitioning away from hail as the primary threat and towards damaging winds.  Even as the severe storm threat diminished very strong winds remained, including gusts in excess of 60 mph, in the presence of a very tight surface pressure gradient. The same system produced blizzard conditions across northern Iowa on the 14th. Please see the separate entry for the blizzard portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clarion AWOS recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system meandered its way across the central Plains on the 13th through 15th, eventually occluding, as it tracked eastward along the Nebraska-Kansas and Iowa-Missouri borders.  Because of its slow movement and tight surface pressure gradient, blizzard conditions were seen over large parts of northwest and northern Iowa during the day of the 14th of April. The worst blizzard conditions lasted roughly from early to mid morning through mid to late afternoon. Snowfall amounts were quite tremendous in spots, with as much as 12 to 16 inches reported in a number of locations across far northern Iowa. The same system produced severe weather across central and southern on the 13th. Please see the separate entry for the severe weather portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville ASOS measured a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northern Iowa found itself in the cross hairs of yet another April winter storm, the 4th to this point.  A surface low pressure system slid off the Rockies on the 17th and worked its way across northern Kansas.  By the early morning hours on the 18th, the surface low tracked into central Missouri, placing Iowa north of the surface low and in line for showers, strong storms, and snow from the broad lift associated with warm air advection north of the warm front.  A few strong to severe storms were seen prior to snowfall onset, with temperatures were generally right around freezing. Warm air aloft also contributed to a tricky transition to snow forecast.  After initial thunderstorms and rain, snowfall began around 6 to 7 am across much of northern Iowa.  By the time precipitation moved out during the late afternoon and evening, as much as a foot of snow fell near the Minnesota border. Accumulating snow was seen as far south as about Highway 30.  ||Note: Another event was created for this day for the winter weather aspect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

A weak upper-level disturbance tracking through the central Plains triggered an early-day cluster of thunderstorms that intensified rapidly while moving through northeast Nebraska and west-central Iowa. Wind gusts well into the 70-mph range were recorded in Antelope, Madison, and Burt counties with the passage of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed by high winds in Woodbine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an early morning flash flood event in eastern Iowa, with some damaging winds near the Mississippi River, a line of slow moving supercell thunderstorms moved southeast from northern Iowa, into portions of eastern Iowa the evening of June 9th. Damaging winds, large hail, a few tornadoes, and torrential rain accompanied the storms.  This event brought flash flooding, and led to some minor to moderate river flooding on the Cedar, Iowa, and Wapsipinicon Rivers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted north of Osage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening of June 9th. These severe storms produced up to 75 mph wind gusts in and near Rockford (Floyd County) with  extensive damage in town. At least 300 hundred trees were destroyed with some structural damage to buildings. A light pole at the high school football field was blown down onto storage buildings for the baseball and softball fields. An estimated 80 mph wind gust occurred west of Floyd (Floyd County). These storms also produced up to golf ball sized hail near Nora Springs (Floyd County) and west of Charles City (Floyd County). A section of the Avenue of the Saints was closed south of Charles City to allow utility crews to repair power lines. This complex was then followed by another round of thunderstorms later in the evening and the combined rainfall produced flash flooding that sent water over numerous roads across parts of Floyd, Fayette and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed several small creeks out of their banks over the southwest part of Fayette County. Several roads west and southwest of Maynard were covered by floodwaters, including State Highway 150 between Maynard and Oelwein.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as damaging infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 5 to 10 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 7.04 inches at Rock Rapids and 9.19 inches near Lester. Major flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated as the Rock River at Rock Rapids (gage site RAPI4) crested 7.8 feet above flood stage on June 22. The river fell below flood stage on June 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as damaging infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 5 to 10 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 7.04 inches at Rock Rapids, 9.15 inches at Rock Valley and 9.19 inches near Lester. Major flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated. The Little Rock River, a tributary which has confluence with the Rock River south of Doon, washed out a rail line sometime overnight on June 22. A train derailment occurred around 430CDT, with 33 cars leaving the track, 10 leaking an estimated 160,000 gallons of heavy crude oil into the flood waters. The city well at Rock Valley was shut down for a period following the spill.  A trailer park and several businesses were flooded as the Rock River at Rock Valley (gage site RKVI4) crested 5.3 feet above flood stage on June 22. A secondary major crest 3.5 feet above flood stage occurred on June 26. The river fell below flood stage on June 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an early morning flash flood event in eastern Iowa, with some damaging winds near the Mississippi River, a line of slow moving supercell thunderstorms moved southeast from northern Iowa, into portions of eastern Iowa the evening of June 9th. Damaging winds, large hail, a few tornadoes, and torrential rain accompanied the storms.  This event brought flash flooding, and led to some minor to moderate river flooding on the Cedar, Iowa, and Wapsipinicon Rivers.

June ended with two days of excessive heat across northeast Iowa. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 110 on both June 29th and 30th, although the high heat indices were more widespread on the 29th. Air temperatures in the upper 80s to middle 90s with dew points in the middle 70s combined to create the excessive heat. The highest calculated heat index was 111 from a mesonet station in McGregor (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 110 were common across Winneshiek County on June 29th and 30th. The highest calculated heat index was 110 from a mesonet station northwest of Frankville. Decorah had a peak heat index of 104.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level storm system tracked from the northern Rockies into northern High Plains with an associated surface low developing east across the Dakotas. Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours from north-central into eastern Nebraska along a trailing cold front with storms organizing into a bow-echo complex which produced numerous severe wind gusts and wind damage over northeast Nebraska and extended into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi-truck was blown over on I-29, about 3 miles south of Blencoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just south of an outflow from morning convection, sunshine returned temperatures to around 90 degrees, with stifling dew point readings near 80 degrees. Heat index values reached 100-105 for several hours during the early to mid afternoon, before the outflow expanded to cool readings below advisory criteria.

An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 40 to 50 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved toward midday on March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 45 mph at Sioux City, 40 mph at Le Mars, 39 mph at Cherokee and Spencer, and 37 mph at Sheldon. There were brief blizzard or near blizzard conditions during the evening and overnight in portions of Lyon, Sioux, O'Brien, Plymouth and Woodbury counties, but not persistent enough to be defined as a blizzard.  Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches across northwest Iowa, including 5.7 inches at Sheldon, 4.0 inches at Holstein,  Cherokee and Lake Park, and 3.7 inches at Akron, Remsen and Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of excessive overnight rainfall, another round of thunderstorms developed along a cold frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Deep unidirectional wind shear of 50-60 knots and up to 2000 J/kg MLCAPE with strong frontal forcing yielded a linear structure.  Large DCAPE contributed to outflow development, and as storm orientation evolved, a localized QLCS tornado developed near Superior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive tree damage occurred at a property on the south side of Hartley, with numerous large limbs in excess of 6 inches in diameter broken and a few trees split due to strong winds. A two-story home experienced broken windows and some minor roof and siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of four to nine inches the first few days of July brought extended impacts from flooding to the Little Sioux River and tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were flooded between Everly and Spencer, with a few township roads inundated as river levels on the Ocheyedan River climbed to 2.5 feet above flood stage near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) on July 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of four to nine inches the first few days of July brought extended impacts from flooding to the Little Sioux River and tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were flooded, and 170th Avenue was flooded north of State Highway 9 as river levels on the Little Sioux River climbed to 1.7 feet above flood stage near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of four to nine inches the first few days of July brought extended impacts from flooding to the Little Sioux River and tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county and township roads, were inundated for almost a week, with major damage to 180th St. between 170th and 190th Avenues. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Little Sioux River were severely impacted by major flooding as river levels climbed to crests of 4.7 feet and 4.5 feet above flood stage near Milford (gage site MIFI4) on July 1 and 5, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas from far southern South Dakota and northeast Nebraska into adjacent northwest Iowa were on the northern fringes of an area of low pressure moving across the Central Plains. Light ice accumulations were followed by light snowfall, resulting in slick roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel conditions producing several minor accidents resulted from a period of light freezing rain which brought ice accumulations up to two tenths of an inch. Light snowfall followed with less than an inch accumulation. Several power outages were reported around the Sioux City metro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with periods of rainfall through mid March caused minor flooding along portions of the Little Sioux River. Minor flooding occurred at Linn Grove (LNNI4) impacting mainly lowland agricultural locations and the city park in Sioux Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snow melt along with periods of rainfall through mid March caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along portions of the Little Sioux River.  The river reached a crest of 0.9 feet above flood stage near Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) on March 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall, along with runoff from melting snow from March 23-24, pushed much of the Little Sioux River back above flood stage after a few days below flood stage.  Reaches from Milford (MIFI4) to Spencer (SPWI4) to Linn Grove (LNNI4) again had waters invade mainly agricultural lowlands, along with city greenways in Spencer and Sioux Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt runoff on top of already high river levels produced minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along upper portions of the Little Sioux River. River levels reached 0.9 feet above flood stage near Milford (gage site MIFI4) by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of four to nine inches the first few days of July brought extended impacts from flooding to the Little Sioux River and tributaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 40 to 50 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved toward midday on March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 45 mph at Sioux City, 40 mph at Le Mars, 39 mph at Cherokee and Spencer, and 37 mph at Sheldon. There were brief blizzard or near blizzard conditions during the evening and overnight in portions of Lyon, Sioux, O'Brien, Plymouth and Woodbury counties, but not persistent enough to be defined as a blizzard.  Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches across northwest Iowa, including 5.7 inches at Sheldon, 4.0 inches at Holstein,  Cherokee and Lake Park, and 3.7 inches at Akron, Remsen and Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection. With cold temperatures at the surface and warmer temperatures aloft, a mix of precipitation types occurred. Most freezing rain was brief and light across northwest Iowa, with snow the predominant type, generally less than an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent light snowfall started with a brief mix of light freezing rain, resulting in a light glaze of icing. Total snowfall at Sibley through the afternoon on March 10 was 1.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in less than a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, but enough to create hazardous travel conditions during the morning and midday hours of March 10.  Snowfall following icing conditions was generally a half inch or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of September 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 4 inches produced some flash flooding across Allamakee and Clayton Counties. High water was reported in and around Dorchester (Allamakee County) and a mudslide occurred north of Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Spillville. The river crested a half foot above the flood stage at 9.5 feet on September 4th. The river briefly fell below flood stage before going out of its banks again. The second crest was over four and a half feet above the flood stage at 13.67 feet on September 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks near Eldorado. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 15.12 feet on September 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed across northeast South Dakota during the overnight, progressing southeast along the mid-level thermal gradient into northwest Iowa by late morning.  Increased shear due to the deviant motion allowed a greater longevity to the storm, which made use of modest elevated instability to produce large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down up to 2 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third consecutive day, a boundary found itself draped from NE to SW roughly cutting the state of Iowa in half.  Similar afternoon conditions resulted as well with 1000 - 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, little CIN, and borderline to good effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and pushing 50 kts at times.  With storms more closely rooted to the boundary, low level helicity in excess of 150 m2/s2 raised at least some concern for tornadoes. After a few initial discrete cells, storms eventually congealed into a progressive QLCS that moved across central Iowa and produced 3 weak tornadoes just east of the Des Moines metro area along with a few reports of wind damage and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage associated with a short lived tornado just adjacent to Des Moines River.  Tornado was reported by public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: To open up the month of May, Iowa saw a pair of thunderstorm rounds move through. First, storms that initiated the afternoon and evening prior in central and eastern Nebraska and progressed into and across Iowa overnight and through the morning of the 1st.  That first line of storms was primarily elevated in nature within an environment of 1000+ J/kg MUCAPE and lackluster effective bulk shear, resulting in mostly sub-severe hail and wind gusts. As the environment cleared out during the morning hours, MUCAPE values approached 2000 J/kg near the cold front across northwest and western Iowa. Effective bulk shear values remained lackluster, under 35 kts in most cases.  Resulting storms that fired up near and ahead of the surface cold front during the mid-afternoon hours generally struggled to sustain rotating updrafts, but were able to drop multiple instances of severe hail, approaching 2 inches in diameter occasionally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported two barns damaged west of Creston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A roughly NE to SW oriented cold front that was slowly progressing eastward stalled out about half way across the state, awaiting storms to fire up along and ahead of it during the afternoon and evening hours. Similar to previous days, the convective environment was not tremendous, but was enough to warrant at least limited concern.  MUCAPE values approached 1500 - 2000 J/kg by the afternoon hours, especially over the southern parts of the state, along with bulk shear values around 30 to 40 kts by mid to late afternoon. As a result, a few strong to severe storms initiated by mid-afternoon and produced periods of severe hail into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail ongoing for the about 10 minutes, including up to golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third consecutive day, a boundary found itself draped from NE to SW roughly cutting the state of Iowa in half.  Similar afternoon conditions resulted as well with 1000 - 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, little CIN, and borderline to good effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and pushing 50 kts at times.  With storms more closely rooted to the boundary, low level helicity in excess of 150 m2/s2 raised at least some concern for tornadoes. After a few initial discrete cells, storms eventually congealed into a progressive QLCS that moved across central Iowa and produced 3 weak tornadoes just east of the Des Moines metro area along with a few reports of wind damage and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported an apparent microburst that caused damage to a house and tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predominantly stalled out east-west oriented surface boundary found itself situated across southern Iowa on the 11th and drifted slowly into northern Iowa by the 12th.  Given the boundary location, storms that initiated were elevated in nature and predominantly limited to a hail and heavy rain threat.  The convective environment contained MUCAPE values throughout this time frame in the 1000-2000 J/kg range coincident with roughly 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear.  Generally, convection was initiated with the aide of  the low level jet, leading to relatively widespread multi-cellular clusters versus discrete cells.  In all, severe hail was difficult to come by, while heavy rainfall was more prevalent in the 1.5 to 2.5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled out boundary continued to linger across southern Iowa and northern Missouri for the third day/event in a row. South of the boundary, SBCAPE values were modest in around 2000 J/kg, however much of the action for Iowa was north of the boundary within an elevated MUCAPE environment of 2000+ J/kg. Add in lackluster effective bulk shear under 35 kts, and the result was predominantly storms with little residence time for consistent large hail development.  A number of storms produced sub-severe hail, but a couple did produce severe reports, including up to around golf ball sized hail in south-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of May, between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell across northeast Iowa. Runoff from this rain produced some flooding along the Cedar, Upper Iowa and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Spillville. The river crested over five feet above the flood stage at 14.42 feet on the 4th. Several fire departments and law enforcement agencies help residents sandbag against the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds were also reported, but a wind speed estimate was not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a warm, fairly humid day across the southeast half to two thirds of Iowa with highs in the mid 80s and dew points in the upper 60s. A surface boundary was situated to the north and west of the state, along with lingering outflows within the state from previous convection. SBCAPE values generally peaked in the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the afternoon, devoid of supportive shear though. Storms initiated through parts of central Iowa on northeast along a remnant boundary. The severe nature of the storms were relatively short-lived and relegated to the first few hours of the event.  A single severe wind gust was recorded, but a number of sub-severe to large severe hail reports were received, including a report of 2 inch hail near Huxley, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported estimated golf ball sized hail along I-35 south of Ames. Report came via text message picture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy air mass was in place across the region with highs into the mid 90s and dew points into the mid 60s to low 70s. Resulting SB and MUCAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg values. Other supporting factors for sustained severe storms were relatively lacking with little effective helicity to speak of, let alone effective shear. DCAPE, however, was fairly robust in the 1400-1700 J/kg range.  Additionally, a weak north-south boundary was laid up across western Iowa that saw a brief landspout near Plover during initial development of storms. Overall, the majority of reports through central and northern Iowa were severe hail and winds from short-lived convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail and small branches down between Saint Anthony and Clemons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of May, between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell across northeast Iowa. Runoff from this rain produced some flooding along the Cedar, Upper Iowa and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Upper Iowa River out of its banks in Dorchester. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 15.26 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predominantly stalled out east-west oriented surface boundary found itself situated across southern Iowa on the 11th and drifted slowly into northern Iowa by the 12th.  Given the boundary location, storms that initiated were elevated in nature and predominantly limited to a hail and heavy rain threat.  The convective environment contained MUCAPE values throughout this time frame in the 1000-2000 J/kg range coincident with roughly 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear.  Generally, convection was initiated with the aide of  the low level jet, leading to relatively widespread multi-cellular clusters versus discrete cells.  In all, severe hail was difficult to come by, while heavy rainfall was more prevalent in the 1.5 to 2.5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail.  Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy air mass was in place across the region with highs into the mid 90s and dew points into the mid 60s to low 70s. Resulting SB and MUCAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg values. Other supporting factors for sustained severe storms were relatively lacking with little effective helicity to speak of, let alone effective shear. DCAPE, however, was fairly robust in the 1400-1700 J/kg range.  Additionally, a weak north-south boundary was laid up across western Iowa that saw a brief landspout near Plover during initial development of storms. Overall, the majority of reports through central and northern Iowa were severe hail and winds from short-lived convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous 3 to 4 inch diameter tree branches down throughout Mason City along with a few larger trees down as well.  Hail sized was unknown at the time.  Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: To open up the month of May, Iowa saw a pair of thunderstorm rounds move through. First, storms that initiated the afternoon and evening prior in central and eastern Nebraska and progressed into and across Iowa overnight and through the morning of the 1st.  That first line of storms was primarily elevated in nature within an environment of 1000+ J/kg MUCAPE and lackluster effective bulk shear, resulting in mostly sub-severe hail and wind gusts. As the environment cleared out during the morning hours, MUCAPE values approached 2000 J/kg near the cold front across northwest and western Iowa. Effective bulk shear values remained lackluster, under 35 kts in most cases.  Resulting storms that fired up near and ahead of the surface cold front during the mid-afternoon hours generally struggled to sustain rotating updrafts, but were able to drop multiple instances of severe hail, approaching 2 inches in diameter occasionally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported up to penny size hail lasting about 15 to 30 seconds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a warm, fairly humid day across the southeast half to two thirds of Iowa with highs in the mid 80s and dew points in the upper 60s. A surface boundary was situated to the north and west of the state, along with lingering outflows within the state from previous convection. SBCAPE values generally peaked in the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the afternoon, devoid of supportive shear though. Storms initiated through parts of central Iowa on northeast along a remnant boundary. The severe nature of the storms were relatively short-lived and relegated to the first few hours of the event.  A single severe wind gust was recorded, but a number of sub-severe to large severe hail reports were received, including a report of 2 inch hail near Huxley, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed a picture of half dollar to ping pong ball sized hail on the north side of Ames. The image came via a text message picture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third consecutive day, a boundary found itself draped from NE to SW roughly cutting the state of Iowa in half.  Similar afternoon conditions resulted as well with 1000 - 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, little CIN, and borderline to good effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and pushing 50 kts at times.  With storms more closely rooted to the boundary, low level helicity in excess of 150 m2/s2 raised at least some concern for tornadoes. After a few initial discrete cells, storms eventually congealed into a progressive QLCS that moved across central Iowa and produced 3 weak tornadoes just east of the Des Moines metro area along with a few reports of wind damage and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported at least 60 mph winds along with copious amounts of small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: General surface high pressure remained in the region, keeping the area hot. With moisture off the from remnants of Alberto and general southerly flow down to the Gulf of Mexico, moisture was fairly plentiful with surface dew points in the mid to upper 60s. Support for sustained severe weather was fairly lackluster, with limited hail CAPE, negligible effective shear, but overall SBCAPE values did exceed 3000 to 4000 J/kg. DCAPE values were relatively high in excess of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, raising concern for strong downdrafts/microbursts.  ||Lingering convection from western Kansas the day before slowly worked towards western Iowa in the morning hours as waves ejected from a western upper level trough. Additionally, weak warm air advection aided initial storm initiation across the western half of Iowa ahead of the larger wave moving off the Rockies. Initially, numerous storms were seen by early afternoon with most being sub-severe. Outflows form early storms also proceeded to initiate subsequent storms, eventually congealing briefly into a linear looking structure. Nearly all severe reports received were wind related from microbursts and collapsing storms as well as one landspout near Ames. Power outages occurred with the storms with at over 9,000 customers without power at one time or another between Ames and Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level wave pushed across the northern Plains on May 8, with a frontal zone moving in during the evening hours. Even with modest instability with MLCAPE from 500-1000 J/kg, storms erupted during the evening hours, and a few storms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level wave pushed across the northern Plains on May 8, with a frontal zone moving in during the evening hours. Even with modest instability with MLCAPE from 500-1000 J/kg, storms erupted during the evening hours, and a few storms produced large hail.

A cold front moving across northwest Iowa interacted with a weakly capped environment with 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE to produce scattered surface-based storms during the late afternoon with large hail up to golf ball size.  Isolated storms also developed behind the cold front during the evening with marginally large hail.

A cold front moving across northwest Iowa interacted with a weakly capped environment with 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE to produce scattered surface-based storms during the late afternoon with large hail up to golf ball size.  Isolated storms also developed behind the cold front during the evening with marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media with picture. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies in the morning and southwest surface winds led to modest temperatures in the low to mid 80s by mid-afternoon along with fairly high humidity with dew points in the mid to upper 60s. The bulk of the shower and storm action had pushed off to the eastern edges of Iowa and beyond by this time, however vigorous cumulus clouds were seen by the afternoon. While the environment did not support a widespread opportunity for funnel clouds, decent low level lapse rates around 8 deg C/km were seen locally with 75 to 100 J/kg 0-3 km MLCAPE and 50 to 100 m2/s2 0-3 km helicity were able to overcome an approximately 1500 m LCL and produce a brief Funnel cloud southwest of the Des Moines metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a brief funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: To open up the month of May, Iowa saw a pair of thunderstorm rounds move through. First, storms that initiated the afternoon and evening prior in central and eastern Nebraska and progressed into and across Iowa overnight and through the morning of the 1st.  That first line of storms was primarily elevated in nature within an environment of 1000+ J/kg MUCAPE and lackluster effective bulk shear, resulting in mostly sub-severe hail and wind gusts. As the environment cleared out during the morning hours, MUCAPE values approached 2000 J/kg near the cold front across northwest and western Iowa. Effective bulk shear values remained lackluster, under 35 kts in most cases.  Resulting storms that fired up near and ahead of the surface cold front during the mid-afternoon hours generally struggled to sustain rotating updrafts, but were able to drop multiple instances of severe hail, approaching 2 inches in diameter occasionally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel hail with a few quarter size pieces as well as the storm moved through Panora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of May, between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell across northeast Iowa. Runoff from this rain produced some flooding along the Cedar, Upper Iowa and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Turkey River out of its banks near Eldorado. The river crested almost three feet above the flood stage at 15.9 feet on the 4th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Clermont. The river crested less than a foot above the flood stage at 826.26 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued periods of heavy rainfall kept levels of the upper and middle Little Sioux River high through much of the month of May.  Flooding continued out of April, and was renewed again through mid to late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from April, and was generally minor with impacts limited to near river agricultural areas until falling below flood stage near Milford (gage site MIFI4) on May 6. Rainfall over the first two weeks of May totalled from 3 to 5 inches in the upper Little Sioux Basin, including 5.01 inches at Lake Park, 4.85 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford and 4.69 inches in Spirit Lake. The river rose back above flood stage on May 9 and crested in moderate flood stage (2.7 feet above flood stage) on May 14, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain. The river dropped below flood stage on May 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued periods of heavy rainfall kept levels of the upper and middle Little Sioux River high through much of the month of May.  Flooding continued out of April, and was renewed again through mid to late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Little Sioux River continued from April, and was generally minor with impacts limited to near-river agricultural areas and the city park greenway in Spencer until falling below flood stage (gage site SPWI4) on May 8.  However, rainfall was relentless over the first two weeks of May, totaling a widespread 3 to 5 inches in the upper Little Sioux Basin, including 5.01 inches at Lake Park, 4.85 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford, 4.69 inches in Spirit Lake, 3.95 inches near Everly and 3.20 inches at Spencer. The river rose back above flood stage on May 9 and crested again in minor flood stage (2.9 feet above flood stage) on May 13, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain. The river dropped below flood stage on May 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued periods of heavy rainfall kept levels of the upper and middle Little Sioux River high through much of the month of May.  Flooding continued out of April, and was renewed again through mid to late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Little Sioux River continued from April, impacting near-river agricultural areas. However, rainfall was relentless over the first two weeks of May, totaling a widespread 3 to 5 inches in the upper Little Sioux Basin, including 5.01 inches at Lake Park, 4.85 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford, 4.69 inches in Spirit Lake, 3.95 inches near Everly and 3.20 inches at Spencer. There were multiple crests at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) during the following three weeks, with the highest crest reaching moderate flooding (1.7 feet above flood stage) on May 15, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain as well as the city park in Sioux Rapids. The river dropped below flood stage on May 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predominantly stalled out east-west oriented surface boundary found itself situated across southern Iowa on the 11th and drifted slowly into northern Iowa by the 12th.  Given the boundary location, storms that initiated were elevated in nature and predominantly limited to a hail and heavy rain threat.  The convective environment contained MUCAPE values throughout this time frame in the 1000-2000 J/kg range coincident with roughly 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear.  Generally, convection was initiated with the aide of  the low level jet, leading to relatively widespread multi-cellular clusters versus discrete cells.  In all, severe hail was difficult to come by, while heavy rainfall was more prevalent in the 1.5 to 2.5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS office recorded heavy rainfall of 1.72 inches over the last 24 hours, including 1.45 inches since 1 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a warm, fairly humid day across the southeast half to two thirds of Iowa with highs in the mid 80s and dew points in the upper 60s. A surface boundary was situated to the north and west of the state, along with lingering outflows within the state from previous convection. SBCAPE values generally peaked in the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the afternoon, devoid of supportive shear though. Storms initiated through parts of central Iowa on northeast along a remnant boundary. The severe nature of the storms were relatively short-lived and relegated to the first few hours of the event.  A single severe wind gust was recorded, but a number of sub-severe to large severe hail reports were received, including a report of 2 inch hail near Huxley, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported quarter sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was split and a power pole was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy air mass was in place across the region with highs into the mid 90s and dew points into the mid 60s to low 70s. Resulting SB and MUCAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg values. Other supporting factors for sustained severe storms were relatively lacking with little effective helicity to speak of, let alone effective shear. DCAPE, however, was fairly robust in the 1400-1700 J/kg range.  Additionally, a weak north-south boundary was laid up across western Iowa that saw a brief landspout near Plover during initial development of storms. Overall, the majority of reports through central and northern Iowa were severe hail and winds from short-lived convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail falling on the south side of Maxwell along with strong winds. No wind damage was observed at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy air mass was in place across the region with highs into the mid 90s and dew points into the mid 60s to low 70s. Resulting SB and MUCAPE values were in the 3000 to 4000 J/kg values. Other supporting factors for sustained severe storms were relatively lacking with little effective helicity to speak of, let alone effective shear. DCAPE, however, was fairly robust in the 1400-1700 J/kg range.  Additionally, a weak north-south boundary was laid up across western Iowa that saw a brief landspout near Plover during initial development of storms. Overall, the majority of reports through central and northern Iowa were severe hail and winds from short-lived convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 60 plus MPH wind gusts, large trees down, and power lines down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed public report with picture. Time estimated from radar.

To open up the month of May, Iowa saw a pair of thunderstorm rounds move through. First, storms that initiated the afternoon and evening prior in central and eastern Nebraska and progressed into and across Iowa overnight and through the morning of the 1st.  That first line of storms was primarily elevated in nature within an environment of 1000+ J/kg MUCAPE and lackluster effective bulk shear, resulting in mostly sub-severe hail and wind gusts. As the environment cleared out during the morning hours, MUCAPE values approached 2000 J/kg near the cold front across northwest and western Iowa. Effective bulk shear values remained lackluster, under 35 kts in most cases.  Resulting storms that fired up near and ahead of the surface cold front during the mid-afternoon hours generally struggled to sustain rotating updrafts, but were able to drop multiple instances of severe hail, approaching 2 inches in diameter occasionally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was reported with a picture of an estimated 2 inch hail stone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued periods of heavy rainfall kept levels of the upper and middle Little Sioux River high through much of the month of May.  Flooding continued out of April, and was renewed again through mid to late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Little Sioux River continued from April, impacting near-river agricultural areas. However, rainfall was relentless over the first two weeks of May, totaling a widespread 3 to 5 inches in the upper Little Sioux Basin, including 5.01 inches at Lake Park, 4.85 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford, 4.69 inches in Spirit Lake, 3.95 inches near Everly and 3.20 inches at Spencer. There were multiple crests at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) during the following three weeks, with the highest crest reaching moderate flooding (1.7 feet above flood stage) on May 15, inundating a large amount of pasture and crop land along the flood plain as well as the city park in Sioux Rapids. The river dropped below flood stage on May 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water from snow melt and rainfall continued to work through the Big Sioux River early in May. Flooding remained generally minor with impacts limited to agricultural areas near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow fall through minor flood stages persisted along much of the Big Sioux River during much of the first week of May, with impacts generally limited to flooding of agricultural areas near the river. Flooding ended above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) on May 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt in late April worked down the Mississippi River and caused some northeast Iowa river flooding to occur over the first half of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt that occurred in late April worked downstream through the Mississippi River and caused some flooding at McGregor. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 18.34 feet on the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt that occurred in late April worked downstream through the Mississippi River and caused some flooding at Guttenberg. The river crested almost two feet above the flood stage at 16.93 feet on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early part of May, between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell across northeast Iowa. Runoff from this rain produced some flooding along the Cedar, Upper Iowa and Turkey Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain pushed the Cedar River to go out of its banks in Charles City. The river crested less than a foot above the flood stage at 12.73 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: General surface high pressure remained in the region, keeping the area hot. With moisture off the from remnants of Alberto and general southerly flow down to the Gulf of Mexico, moisture was fairly plentiful with surface dew points in the mid to upper 60s. Support for sustained severe weather was fairly lackluster, with limited hail CAPE, negligible effective shear, but overall SBCAPE values did exceed 3000 to 4000 J/kg. DCAPE values were relatively high in excess of 1000 to 1200 J/kg, raising concern for strong downdrafts/microbursts.  ||Lingering convection from western Kansas the day before slowly worked towards western Iowa in the morning hours as waves ejected from a western upper level trough. Additionally, weak warm air advection aided initial storm initiation across the western half of Iowa ahead of the larger wave moving off the Rockies. Initially, numerous storms were seen by early afternoon with most being sub-severe. Outflows form early storms also proceeded to initiate subsequent storms, eventually congealing briefly into a linear looking structure. Nearly all severe reports received were wind related from microbursts and collapsing storms as well as one landspout near Ames. Power outages occurred with the storms with at over 9,000 customers without power at one time or another between Ames and Des Moines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 4 to 6 inch tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For a third consecutive day, a boundary found itself draped from NE to SW roughly cutting the state of Iowa in half.  Similar afternoon conditions resulted as well with 1000 - 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, little CIN, and borderline to good effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and pushing 50 kts at times.  With storms more closely rooted to the boundary, low level helicity in excess of 150 m2/s2 raised at least some concern for tornadoes. After a few initial discrete cells, storms eventually congealed into a progressive QLCS that moved across central Iowa and produced 3 weak tornadoes just east of the Des Moines metro area along with a few reports of wind damage and sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel nearly to the ground, and now back up.  Still saw a lower of the cloud at the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 5 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall in the Rock River basin from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 10 inches, resulting in moderate flooding.  The Rock River at Rock Rapids (gage site RAPI4) crested 3.5 feet above flood stage on September 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving severe thunderstorms with damaging winds, large hail, and even one tornado, moved east across Iowa into Illinois the afternoon and evening of June 20th. These storms brought significant flash flooding to the the Quad Cities.

A weak upper-level disturbance advanced from the central High Plains into mid Missouri Valley, supporting the development of a surface low over central Nebraska during the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon ahead of the surface low across eastern Nebraska with activity spreading east into western Iowa by evening.  The storms produced damaging straight-line winds with a gust to 76 mph observed near Pilger in Stanton County. A few brief tornadoes were also reported in Stanton, Burt, and Mills Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused flooding on US Highway 30 where 12 inches of standing water was on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar to July 28th, the overall setup for any weather of substance was lacking. Relative surface high pressure remained over the state with fairly light and variable wind fields, highs in the mid to upper 70s, and dew points in the upper 50s to low 60s.  Other supporting features remained unsubstantial with MUCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, bulk effective shear under 30 kts, negligible 0-1 km SRH, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or greater. Once again, local wind fields may have contributed to helicity generation, but was not picked up in larger scale mesoanalysis. Scattered light convection once again was seen across parts of the state, amounting to little more than occasion isolated thunder and moderate rainfall, though a pair of funnel clouds were reported around the Waterloo area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL TV reported a brief funnel cloud west of the UNI Dome along W 27th St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, nearly stationary boundary was oriented across Iowa from southwest to northeast during the 13th. Storms initiated along and near the boundary during the overnight hours from the 12th to the 13th and onward through the day. The environment on the warm side of the front was CAPE filled with SBCAPE values in excess of 3000-4000 J/kg. Additionally, much like the convective season to this point, other supporting severe weather parameters were lacking such as effective shear (under 30 kts), DCAPE (under 1000 J/kg), and hail CAPE (under 500 J/kg) to name a few. The result was typically short lived strong storms, but a few were able to at least briefly organized near the front or along outflow boundaries and produce sporadic strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were taken to the hospital after being struck by lightning at a golf course. Both men were injured and then released the following day from the hospital. One suffered nerve damage and the loss of hearing on one of his ears.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atlantic Municipal Airport AWOS recorded a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather was not favored on the day, however the setup did favor the development of funnel clouds and low level rotation. A low pressure system remained meandering within Iowa and occluding.  Overall, MUCAPE values were under 1000 J/kg, but lifted condensation levels around 500 m and surface vorticity maxima suggested the potential for funnel clouds even without much low level CAPE.  By the end of the day, a handful of funnel cloud reports were seen scattered across central Iowa and around Waterloo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a media report and Facebook post of a funnel cloud near Eagle Center in Blackhawk County. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall across the Sioux Falls metro area from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 7 inches. Moderate flooding resulted with significant flooding of crop and pasture land as the Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) crested 3.3 feet above flood stage on September 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall across the Sioux Falls area and southward in the Big Sioux basin from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 7 inches. Major flooding resulted with all crop and pasture land in the half-mile wide flood plain inundated. The Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) crested 6.0 feet above flood stage on September 22, the third highest crest on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KICD relayed report heavy rainfall of about 3 inches since 2:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media, KWWL, relayed a report of 2 inch hail from a viewer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread upstream rainfall in the Big Sioux basin from September 18-20 totaled generally 5 to 10 inches, resulting in major flooding. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least a second consecutive day, the set up over Iowa was such that funnel clouds were favored.  The environment was once again quite moist in the low levels with lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or less along with resident weak boundaries, and 0-3 km MLCAPE values around 75-150 J/kg.  Convection that initiated was weak and struggled to maintain or grow into anything substantial. Overall, the only noteworthy reports were those of funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a public report of a funnel cloud. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly traditional setup for severe weather was in place over the state, a nice departure from events to this point in the season, with a cold front situated to the northwest moving towards and into the area. Convective initiation was expected along and ahead of the frontal boundary during the afternoon and evening hours, which came to fruition. Supporting features of the environment included SB and MUCAPE values around 1000-2000+ J/kg and effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range. More borderline support for severe potential was seen for hail with CAPE values in the hail growth region around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values around 800-1000 J/kg. All in all, initiated storms were either of the isolated or multi-cellular variety throughout much of the event. With a semi-supportive environment for severe weather, the storms acted as much with multiple reports of nickel to quarter sized hail and wind damage including large tree branches and farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system produced scattered thunderstorms in eastern Iowa and northwestern Illinois in the late afternoon into early evening hours. These storms produced damaging winds of 60 to over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported tree limbs down on a road associated with strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE  with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system produced scattered thunderstorms in eastern Iowa and northwestern Illinois in the late afternoon into early evening hours. These storms produced damaging winds of 60 to over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported cars were stalled out on Main Street in Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several county and rural roads across the county were under water from the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE  with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.

A weak upper-level disturbance advanced from the central High Plains into mid Missouri Valley, supporting the development of a surface low over central Nebraska during the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon ahead of the surface low across eastern Nebraska with activity spreading east into western Iowa by evening.  The storms produced damaging straight-line winds with a gust to 76 mph observed near Pilger in Stanton County. A few brief tornadoes were also reported in Stanton, Burt, and Mills Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down near Pacific Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance advanced from the central High Plains into mid Missouri Valley, supporting the development of a surface low over central Nebraska during the afternoon. Severe thunderstorms developed by mid afternoon ahead of the surface low across eastern Nebraska with activity spreading east into western Iowa by evening.  The storms produced damaging straight-line winds with a gust to 76 mph observed near Pilger in Stanton County. A few brief tornadoes were also reported in Stanton, Burt, and Mills Counties.

A fairly traditional setup for severe weather was in place over the state, a nice departure from events to this point in the season, with a cold front situated to the northwest moving towards and into the area. Convective initiation was expected along and ahead of the frontal boundary during the afternoon and evening hours, which came to fruition. Supporting features of the environment included SB and MUCAPE values around 1000-2000+ J/kg and effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range. More borderline support for severe potential was seen for hail with CAPE values in the hail growth region around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values around 800-1000 J/kg. All in all, initiated storms were either of the isolated or multi-cellular variety throughout much of the event. With a semi-supportive environment for severe weather, the storms acted as much with multiple reports of nickel to quarter sized hail and wind damage including large tree branches and farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported their fence blown down in their yard. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches over a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa caused the Iowa River to rise above major flood stage at Marengo, about two to three days after the end of the heavy rain events. The extended duration between the forecast and actual major river flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin caused the Mississippi River to rise above major flood stage at Marengo. The extended duration between the forecast and actual lower end major river flooding occurrence allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream. Marengo rose above major flood stage of 18.5 feet at 345 PM CST on 5 September. Then crested around 19.14 feet at 630 AM CST on September 6th and fell below 18.5 feet at approximately 715 AM CST on 7 September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On an otherwise mundane day, a small handful of funnel clouds were reported across northern Iowa. The general setup, as hinted, was lackluster with relative high pressure over the region, light wind fields, highs only in the 70s, and dew points in the upper 50s to low 60s. Convective and supporting parameters were generally weak with MUCAPE values around 500-1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear generally under 30 kts, negligible 0-1 km SRH, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or greater to name a few. While the wind fields were light, they may have added to localized helicity with their more chaotic directions versus any directional organization. Sporadic convection did initiate, but amounted to little more than showers and a rogue funnel cloud report or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kossuth County Sheriff's office and KCCI viewer reported a small funnel  just below cloud base. Time estimated from radar.
