EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KICD reported heavy rainfall of 3.40 inches since this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.09 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system helped develop a line of thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska that moved northeast into western Pottawattamie and Harrison Counties during the afternoon of the 17th.  These storms had heavy rain and produced a downburst with damaging winds between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds that blew a semi truck and a camper off of Interstate 29 at mile marker 72, which is about 3 miles south of Missouri Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of September 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 4 inches produced some flash flooding across Allamakee and Clayton Counties. High water was reported in and around Dorchester (Allamakee County) and a mudslide occurred north of Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused a mudslide to occur along State Highway 76 north of Marquette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of September 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 4 inches produced some flash flooding across Allamakee and Clayton Counties. High water was reported in and around Dorchester (Allamakee County) and a mudslide occurred north of Marquette (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed some streams out of their banks across Floyd County. The flooding closed some roads including 110th Street, 280th Street, 290th Street, 300th Street, Ivory Avenue and Ocean Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an early morning flash flood event in eastern Iowa, with some damaging winds near the Mississippi River, a line of slow moving supercell thunderstorms moved southeast from northern Iowa, into portions of eastern Iowa the evening of June 9th. Damaging winds, large hail, a few tornadoes, and torrential rain accompanied the storms.  This event brought flash flooding, and led to some minor to moderate river flooding on the Cedar, Iowa, and Wapsipinicon Rivers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system produced scattered thunderstorms in eastern Iowa and northwestern Illinois in the late afternoon into early evening hours. These storms produced damaging winds of 60 to over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported cars were stalled out on Rosevelt Road.  Water was also observed over the westbound US 34 highway on ramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued to ease on portions of the Big Sioux River from around Akron southward to near Richland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River levels on the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) continued to fall in early October. Impacts were limited to minor agricultural flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of excessive overnight rainfall, another round of thunderstorms developed along a cold frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Deep unidirectional wind shear of 50-60 knots and up to 2000 J/kg MLCAPE with strong frontal forcing yielded a linear structure.  Large DCAPE contributed to outflow development, and as storm orientation evolved, a localized QLCS tornado developed near Superior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up near the apex of a QLCS segment southwest of Superior, touching down with minor crop damage south of Iowa Highway 9 east of 300th Avenue.  The tornado tracked quickly to the east-northeast, crossing Highway 9 just west of Superior and causing its most significant damage (rated EF1) to an office building at an ethanol plant. Winds were measured to 99 mph at a nearby weather station at the plant, which displaced walls and partially collapsed some of the second story of the office building, as well as removing sections of the roof.  The tornado then turned to a slightly more eastward track across the far northern edge of Superior where it continued to cause damage to trees and damaged or destroyed several small outbuildings.  The tornado then tracked east of Superior causing damage to larger trees and crops until crossing over into Emmet county around one-half mile north of 150th Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several county and rural roads across the county were under water from the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On an otherwise mundane day, a small handful of funnel clouds were reported across northern Iowa. The general setup, as hinted, was lackluster with relative high pressure over the region, light wind fields, highs only in the 70s, and dew points in the upper 50s to low 60s. Convective and supporting parameters were generally weak with MUCAPE values around 500-1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear generally under 30 kts, negligible 0-1 km SRH, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or greater to name a few. While the wind fields were light, they may have added to localized helicity with their more chaotic directions versus any directional organization. Sporadic convection did initiate, but amounted to little more than showers and a rogue funnel cloud report or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small funnel cloud was reported just below the cloud base. KCCI relayed the viewer report. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly traditional setup for severe weather was in place over the state, a nice departure from events to this point in the season, with a cold front situated to the northwest moving towards and into the area. Convective initiation was expected along and ahead of the frontal boundary during the afternoon and evening hours, which came to fruition. Supporting features of the environment included SB and MUCAPE values around 1000-2000+ J/kg and effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range. More borderline support for severe potential was seen for hail with CAPE values in the hail growth region around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values around 800-1000 J/kg. All in all, initiated storms were either of the isolated or multi-cellular variety throughout much of the event. With a semi-supportive environment for severe weather, the storms acted as much with multiple reports of nickel to quarter sized hail and wind damage including large tree branches and farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE  with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain moves across areas just south of Interstate 80 in far eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. These storms produced flash flooding in a few locations from near Muscatine through Cambridge, Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over several roads was reported to be over 3 feet deep and flowing quickly. Several vehicles were stalled. One person was knocked off their feet by the quickly flowing water, and nearly swept into a nearby drainage ditch filled with fast-moving water. The person managed to regain their footing and move to safety.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported half dollar sized hail via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.25 inches from about 9 pm through 10:30 pm last night. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the late evening of September 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain from Fayette into Clayton Counties, with some locally higher amounts around 5 inches. This heavy rain produced a mudslide near McGregor (Clayton County) where a campground had to be evacuated. Street flooding occurred in Arlington (Fayette County) and water covered other roads across Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After thunderstorms with locally heavy rain moved across Fayette County, water covered some roads across the county and street flooding occurred in Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 5 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall from September 18-20 totaled generally 6 to 10 inches, resulting in minor flooding of agricultural land. The Little Floyd River near Sanborn (gage site LFSI4) crested 2.4 feet above flood stage on September 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of minor flooding of crop and pasture land as the Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) crested 0.3 feet above flood stage on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent rains through September 4, lacking significant rainfall rates in a largely moist neutral thermodynamic profile, produced locally heavier amounts from 2 to 4 inches from far southeast South Dakota into adjacent sections of northwest Iowa. Smaller creeks and streams showed stronger response to the rainfall by the end of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A day of periodic moderate rain bringing 2.89 inches to Akron and 3.37 inches to Le Mars resulted in minor flooding to small streams by late evening on September 4. Significant agricultural flooding occurred as Perry Creek near Millnerville (gage site PCMI4) crested 2.2 feet above flood stage around 0000CST on September 5, falling very quickly below flood stage by 0500CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dryness expanded near the Missouri border during July with severe drought conditions developing in far southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions developed in central and southern Wayne county due to acute short term dryness by late July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very juicy, at least with regards to CAPE, environment was in place over the area on the 4th, with SBCAPE values approaching 5000+ J/kg. Fortunately, other supporting parameters were generally lacking, with effective shear under 30 kts, modest CAPE values in the hail growth zones, and nearly negligible low level helicity ahead of the outflows. Lending towards a potential wind threat though were DCAPE values in excess of 1200 J/kg. Throughout the overnight, morning, and afternoon, storms initiated along a cold front west of the state, then progressed eastward ahead of the front. In all cases, storms were modest to strong at times given the CAPE environment, but could not sustain themselves. A lone severe wind gust was recorded across far north central Iowa early in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a 61 mph wind gust. His car door was caught by the wind when he was opening it, resulting in the door hitting his face and breaking his nose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A number of low level jet induced storms fired up along a warm front in eastern North Dakota and northeast South Dakota during the overnight hours of the 9th into the 10th. The overall environment was not substantial, but was conducive for storms. MUCAPE values overnight were around the 1000 to 2000 J/kg range in the Dakotas along with a tight gradient of 30 to 50 kt effective shear values near and along the frontal boundary. The storms moved relatively equal to the mean wind direction, to the southeast or south southeast through the night. As the sun rose, storms were in southwest Minnesota and continuing to move towards Iowa. Storms continued to follow an increasing CAPE gradient down into Iowa, even as effective shear waned. For the most part, one lone strong to severe storm survived and produced reports of 50 to 60 mph winds and subsequent tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of 50 to 60 mph winds and tree damage, some of which came from the mPING application.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, nearly stationary boundary was oriented across Iowa from southwest to northeast during the 13th. Storms initiated along and near the boundary during the overnight hours from the 12th to the 13th and onward through the day. The environment on the warm side of the front was CAPE filled with SBCAPE values in excess of 3000-4000 J/kg. Additionally, much like the convective season to this point, other supporting severe weather parameters were lacking such as effective shear (under 30 kts), DCAPE (under 1000 J/kg), and hail CAPE (under 500 J/kg) to name a few. The result was typically short lived strong storms, but a few were able to at least briefly organized near the front or along outflow boundaries and produce sporadic strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported wind gusts of up to 60 mph east of Sac City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front produced an area of thunderstorms with numerous reports of hail up to the size of quarters, damaging winds of 60 to 70 mph, and two brief tornadoes.

A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 5 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall throughout the Little Sioux Basin during the period of September 18-21 totaled generally 4 to 8 inches, resulting in major flooding. Significant crop and pasture land was flooded, and several roads were inundated including 170th Avenue south of State Highway 9, 190th Avenue near 240th St. and 180th Street between 170th and 190th Avenues. The Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) crested at 4.0 feet above flood stage on September 24. The river remained in minor flood at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 5 to as much as 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall throughout the Little Sioux Basin during the period of September 18-21 totaled generally 4 to 8 inches, resulting in major flooding.  Sections of West Park Street, 1st Street and 2nd Street were flooded, and water encroached into several businesses in the south part of Spencer near WalMart along highways 71 and 18. Water also covered portions of county road B-40 southeast of Spencer.  The Little Sioux River at Spencer (gage site SPWI4) crested at 6.6 feet above flood stage on September 21, and remained in minor flood state at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.14 inches and that roadside ditches were full of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary, weak boundary remained draped across the state in a roughly north-south orientation, along which a few funnel clouds were reported. In general, the environment was such that funnel clouds were possible, including lifted condensation levels under 750 meters, 0-3 km CAPE values around 50 to 100 J/kg, and a general boundary along which helicity would be maximized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports of funnel clouds in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least a second consecutive day, the set up over Iowa was such that funnel clouds were favored.  The environment was once again quite moist in the low levels with lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or less along with resident weak boundaries, and 0-3 km MLCAPE values around 75-150 J/kg.  Convection that initiated was weak and struggled to maintain or grow into anything substantial. Overall, the only noteworthy reports were those of funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff's Office reported a funnel cloud accompanying a rain shower northwest of Allison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance tracked from the central High Plains through the mid Missouri Valley, encouraging the development of a surface low from southeast South Dakota into eastern Nebraska.  Meanwhile, an associated cold front progressed east into eastern Nebraska by afternoon with a stationary boundary extending from the surface low through southwest Iowa into northern Missouri. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa along these surface features with activity merging into a bow-echo complex that subsequently tracked into northwest Missouri by late evening. Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE  with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three farm storage buildings were destroyed 3 miles south of Thurman.

Brief tornadoes occurred  with a supercell over Cass County, NE  with intense, straight-line winds affecting the I-29 corridor in Fremont County, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 9 semi-trucks were overturned on Interstate 29  west of Thurman.  Two drivers were injured and taken by ambulance to a hospital. Interstate 29 was closed in both directions after the damaging wind gust overturned the trucks. Numerous trees and power lines were down west of Thurman. In Thurman, there were a lot of trees uprooted along with damage to roofs of homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly traditional setup for severe weather was in place over the state, a nice departure from events to this point in the season, with a cold front situated to the northwest moving towards and into the area. Convective initiation was expected along and ahead of the frontal boundary during the afternoon and evening hours, which came to fruition. Supporting features of the environment included SB and MUCAPE values around 1000-2000+ J/kg and effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range. More borderline support for severe potential was seen for hail with CAPE values in the hail growth region around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values around 800-1000 J/kg. All in all, initiated storms were either of the isolated or multi-cellular variety throughout much of the event. With a semi-supportive environment for severe weather, the storms acted as much with multiple reports of nickel to quarter sized hail and wind damage including large tree branches and farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported large limbs down in town of around 6 to 8 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported generally quarter sized hail falling in the last 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved in from Dallas County.  This was a short segment that remained in agricultural fields with little damage before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.25 inches from the afternoon storms.

Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.71 inches so far today. Report came via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ottumwa Regional Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 4.07 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WHO relayed heavy rainfall report at their station of 2.67 inches via social media. WHO VIA SOCIAL MEDIA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a rotating wall cloud with funnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4.75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of August 28th. These storms dropped hail from Floyd County east into Clayton County. The largest hail size reported was two inches in diameter in St. Lucas (Fayette County). The storms also produced some wind damage with trees blown down in Charles City (Floyd County) and power lines downed in Nashua (Chickasaw County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter hail was reported in St. Lucas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a small tree uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.58 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.60 inches over the last 24 hours.

Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.50 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe storm produced 1 hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size was reported near Spencer Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an internet antenna on the roof. The parts of the antenna were found laying in the grass about 10 yards away. The home was properly grounded, but still led to damage of most of the electronics in the home, including many of the electrical outlets and led some smoke but no fire inside the home. The strike also broke open a water line near the exit point in the basement. Homeowner was home at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft. Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary, with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow. Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 3 to 6 inches across extreme northwest Iowa. Snowfall of 6.8 inches was measured at Rock Rapids, with 5.1 inches near Larchwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported wind gusts to 60 mph along with half inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 5.38 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for thunderstorms was seen across Iowa with much of the state in the warm sector ahead of an impending frontal passage. Supporting factors were relatively lacking though including effective bulk shear under 30 kts, fairly high lifted condensation levels, and a lack of instability within hail growth areas, to name a few. As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS employee reported quarter sized hail. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of thunderstorms rolled through the state on the 5th and into the beginning of the 6th, providing the state with widespread rainfall and strong to severe thunderstorms. The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down, power lines down and power outages. Some streets were partially blocked by trees with some damage to structures. Time estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches of 10 to 12 inch diameter snapped off trees. Some power lines down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved over the area. There was a significant amount of tropical moisture with this system, which was conducive to very efficient and prolonged rainfall rates. This rain caused localized areas of flash flooding, created a small landside, and closed many roads. 5 to 12 inches of rainfall was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mud slide due to heavy rain occurred on Highway 44. The highway department was called and the road was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported a tree down on a house. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth consecutive day, strong to severe storms affected portions of the Iowa, primarily across western and southwestern Iowa in this instance.  A surface low pressure system was situated near the South Dakota, Iowa, Nebraska borders by early afternoon and was continuing to move southward along the Missouri River.  The warm front was draped southeast across western Iowa, before situating itself mostly in an east-west orientation across from roughly south-central Iowa and north-central Missouri on eastward.  ||With dew points in the low to mid 70s within the warm sector and decent to good mid level lapse rates, instability values were quite large in the 3000 to 5000 J/kg neighborhood. Effective shear was best just north of the warm front, but remained in excess of 30 kts within much of the warm sector.  DCAPE values were also on the higher side in the 1100 to 1500 J/kg range. In the end, storms initiated in eastern Nebraska by mid-afternoon and slowly grew upscale through the evening. By mid-late evening, storms grew borderline severe, with a handful of severe reports including wind gusts to 70 mph and a lone severe hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff received report of 60 to 70 mph winds about 4 miles north of New Market.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.24 inches over the last 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.15 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near the intersection of Hwy T17 and Hwy 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.51 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of thunderstorms rolled through the state on the 5th and into the beginning of the 6th, providing the state with widespread rainfall and strong to severe thunderstorms. The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.16 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.30 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.47 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 2 to 3 large trees down. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator recorded a 68 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved over the area. There was a significant amount of tropical moisture with this system, which was conducive to very efficient and prolonged rainfall rates. This rain caused localized areas of flash flooding, created a small landside, and closed many roads. 5 to 12 inches of rainfall was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported. Water was halfway up on some cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.24 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth consecutive day, strong to severe storms affected portions of the Iowa, primarily across western and southwestern Iowa in this instance.  A surface low pressure system was situated near the South Dakota, Iowa, Nebraska borders by early afternoon and was continuing to move southward along the Missouri River.  The warm front was draped southeast across western Iowa, before situating itself mostly in an east-west orientation across from roughly south-central Iowa and north-central Missouri on eastward.  ||With dew points in the low to mid 70s within the warm sector and decent to good mid level lapse rates, instability values were quite large in the 3000 to 5000 J/kg neighborhood. Effective shear was best just north of the warm front, but remained in excess of 30 kts within much of the warm sector.  DCAPE values were also on the higher side in the 1100 to 1500 J/kg range. In the end, storms initiated in eastern Nebraska by mid-afternoon and slowly grew upscale through the evening. By mid-late evening, storms grew borderline severe, with a handful of severe reports including wind gusts to 70 mph and a lone severe hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gusts to 70 mph.

The warm front was draped southeast across western Iowa, before situating itself mostly in an east-west orientation across from roughly south-central Iowa and north-central Missouri on eastward.  ||With dew points in the low to mid 70s within the warm sector and decent to good mid level lapse rates, instability values were quite large in the 3000 to 5000 J/kg neighborhood. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail up to quarter sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported tree down with winds measured at 61 mph from home weather station.

Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed a picture of their rain gage with about 3.1 inches of heavy rainfall since last night. Location was about 4 mi north of the High Trestle Trail Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for thunderstorms was seen across Iowa with much of the state in the warm sector ahead of an impending frontal passage. Supporting factors were relatively lacking though including effective bulk shear under 30 kts, fairly high lifted condensation levels, and a lack of instability within hail growth areas, to name a few. As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail. This is a delayed report.

Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.85 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a 58 mph wind gust.
