EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for thunderstorms was seen across Iowa with much of the state in the warm sector ahead of an impending frontal passage. Supporting factors were relatively lacking though including effective bulk shear under 30 kts, fairly high lifted condensation levels, and a lack of instability within hail growth areas, to name a few. As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported up to golf ball sized hail, including damage to the top of his truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was situated over the upper Midwest, with accompanying shortwaves moving through the flow. No major/discernible surface system was present, though weak boundaries were in the area. Thermodynamically, around 2000 J/kg of SBCAPE was present along with around 30 kts effective bulk shear, a nose of 800 J/kg downdraft CAPE, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or less.  Overall, the convective activity was scattered in nature across the state with the lack of a real focusing mechanism, and with predominantly lackluster supporting parameters the majority of storms remained sub-severe. In the end, a lone funnel cloud was reported and with a severe wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL relayed a viewer report of a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City ASOS, near Clear Lake, recorded a 71 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from consecutive nights of thunderstorms caused several rivers across portions of central and northern Iowa to go into flood. Streamflows were high across much of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winnebago River at Mason City crested at 11.34 feet on 10 June 2018 at 06:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from consecutive nights of thunderstorms caused several rivers across portions of central and northern Iowa to go into flood. Streamflows were high across much of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shell Rock River at Shell Rock crested at 15.45 feet on 11 June 2018 at 11:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported half dollar sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WHO-TV relayed heavy rainfall report of 2.40 inches.

As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.18 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level wave and frontal boundary pushed across the area during the afternoon hours. Storms erupted as temperatures cooled aloft by early afternoon, tapping instability of 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE. Deep-layer shear over 50 knots was primarily unidirectional, but storms maintained largely isolated nature along the boundary, producing periodic large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rain events through mid June led to river flooding across portions of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Skunk River at Ames on US 30 crested at 22.93 feet on 15 June 2018 at 17:00 UTC.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Squaw Creek at Ames Lincoln Way crested at 12.19 feet on 15 June 2018 at 10:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rain events through mid June led to river flooding across portions of central Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.03 inches over the last 24 hours.

While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 4.23 inches over the last 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was situated over the upper Midwest, with accompanying shortwaves moving through the flow. No major/discernible surface system was present, though weak boundaries were in the area. Thermodynamically, around 2000 J/kg of SBCAPE was present along with around 30 kts effective bulk shear, a nose of 800 J/kg downdraft CAPE, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m or less.  Overall, the convective activity was scattered in nature across the state with the lack of a real focusing mechanism, and with predominantly lackluster supporting parameters the majority of storms remained sub-severe. In the end, a lone funnel cloud was reported and with a severe wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a severe wind gust of 63 mph measured from their home weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonable to seasonably cool day across the area was greeted with a number of funnel cloud reports across north central Iowa. The mesoscale environment was not tremendous with predominantly lackluster supporting initiation or organizational features including effective shear, overall forcing/focusing mechanisms, and so forth, but with around 2000+ J/kg SBCAPE, weak boundaries around, and moderately steep low level lapse rates, and lifted condensation levels around 1000 m, updrafts were able to produce the aforementioned funnels across northern Iowa, where the environment was most supportive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement relayed that there were several reports of a funnel cloud in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The events of the day came in two waves. An initial wave of heavy rainfall ahead of the surface low, and then severe weather near the triple point in northern Iowa as the surface low crossed Iowa.||For wave one, southern Iowa, storms were induced overnight of the 23rd into the 24th across eastern Kansas and made its way ENE towards and across southern Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 24th. Fortunately, given the timing, storms were elevated and able to remain sub-severe. Instead, with precipitable water values approaching two inches, heavy rainfall was experienced even with the progressive nature of the storms. ||Wave two took place across northern Iowa in and around the surface low triple point as it moved across the state. Within the warm sector and near the boundaries, the severe weather potential and supporting variables were present. Support for severe weather included SBCAPE of 3000+ J/kg, 30-40+ kts of effective bulk shear, and 100+ m2/s2 effective storm relative helicity. In the end, fortunately, generally a single cluster of strong storms formed and moved across northern Iowa. Severe weather produced included large hail to about golf ball size, damaging winds, and a funnel cloud report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of thunderstorms rolled through the state on the 5th and into the beginning of the 6th, providing the state with widespread rainfall and strong to severe thunderstorms. The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported tree branches down at Beed's Lake State Park Campground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a large 8 to 10 inch diameter tree branch snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported mostly pea to nickel sized hail with a few to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KICD Radio relayed heavy rainfall report of 3.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.51 inches from yesterday and overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for thunderstorms was seen across Iowa with much of the state in the warm sector ahead of an impending frontal passage. Supporting factors were relatively lacking though including effective bulk shear under 30 kts, fairly high lifted condensation levels, and a lack of instability within hail growth areas, to name a few. As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported quarter sized hail, via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.40 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level wave and frontal boundary pushed across the area during the afternoon hours. Storms erupted as temperatures cooled aloft by early afternoon, tapping instability of 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE. Deep-layer shear over 50 knots was primarily unidirectional, but storms maintained largely isolated nature along the boundary, producing periodic large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted for five minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported up to 65 mph wind gusts along with pea sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.01 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a 2 foot diameter tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of thunderstorms rolled through the state on the 5th and into the beginning of the 6th, providing the state with widespread rainfall and strong to severe thunderstorms. The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported tree branches down, including one on a house. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Solidly within southwest upper level flow, the area found itself within an active period with multiple rounds of thunderstorms. Morning convection amounted to little more than moderate showers and thunderstorms, giving way to a short break before a potent short wave, increased low level jet, and increased theta-e advection initiated another round of strong to severe thunderstorms. Even with morning convection and lingering cloud cover, MUCAPE values approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the early to mid evening hours, along with marginally supportive effective bulk shear (about 30 kts), and precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches. As convection strolled northeast, a few strong to severe cores went up, providing periods of heavy rainfall and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a large tree snapped at its base along with several other small branches and limbs down. Spotter reported losing power as well.  This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported up to around baseball or larger sized hail. Time estimated from radar.

Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail around ping pong ball in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Solidly within southwest upper level flow, the area found itself within an active period with multiple rounds of thunderstorms. Morning convection amounted to little more than moderate showers and thunderstorms, giving way to a short break before a potent short wave, increased low level jet, and increased theta-e advection initiated another round of strong to severe thunderstorms. Even with morning convection and lingering cloud cover, MUCAPE values approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the early to mid evening hours, along with marginally supportive effective bulk shear (about 30 kts), and precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches. As convection strolled northeast, a few strong to severe cores went up, providing periods of heavy rainfall and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported numerous trees down in and around Sheffield. Highway 65 was reported blocked by down power lines as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of low-lying crop and pasture land occurred at river levels crested at 0.3 feet above flood stage on Waterman Creek near Sutherland (gage site SWCI4) on October 10.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reached 190th Avenue near 240th Street, and low-lying agricultural lands were flooded as the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) crested 1.1 feet above flood stage on October 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 3.09 through 9:30 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a delayed report. Public reported larger than quarter sized hail in Traer via multiple reports on social media including from KWWL, KCCI, KCRG.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for thunderstorms was seen across Iowa with much of the state in the warm sector ahead of an impending frontal passage. Supporting factors were relatively lacking though including effective bulk shear under 30 kts, fairly high lifted condensation levels, and a lack of instability within hail growth areas, to name a few. As a result, primarily unorganized thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening hours. A handful of storms were able to provide strong updrafts in the roughly 2000 to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE environment with brief periods of sub-severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of thunderstorms rolled through the state on the 5th and into the beginning of the 6th, providing the state with widespread rainfall and strong to severe thunderstorms. The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 60 plus MPH winds near John F Kennedy park. Time estimated.

The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 plus inch range, though damaging winds and severe hail were also observed. ||The setup included a a boundary off to the west and northwest that allowed for convection to initiate in South Dakota and eventually move towards the state. Additionally, as the day wore on, the boundary in question moved into Iowa and allowed for additional rounds of convection to form.  During the early morning hours of the 5th, a very unstable air mass over the South Dakota, Nebraska, Iowa, Minnesota was present, including MUCAPE values around 3000+ J/kg. Additionally, marginally supportive effective bulk shear was present (30 to 40 kts) and downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg.  During the daytime hours, the environment predominantly became less supportive for severe storms with reductions in effective bulk shear and downdraft CAPE, though a highly unstable environment remained. Resulting reports included the majority of severe reports being wind related during the early morning to morning hours, followed by rounds of strong, but sub-severe, storms throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4.48 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall setup for convective weather, especially anything strong to severe, was poor across the area. No strong synoptic systems were in the neighborhood, and supporting severe weather parameters such as effective shear and extreme CAPE were negligible. While no severe weather was reported or experienced, a number of funnel clouds were reported within an environment of low lifted condensation levels (500-750 m), localized helicity (outflows), and moderate SBCAPE (1000-2000 J/kg). Additionally, with precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.8 inches, localized heavy rainfall was experienced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel cloud. This is a delayed report and location is an approximation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded heavy rainfall of 2.02 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level trough approached during the late night and early morning hours, scattered post-frontal thunderstorms developed. Feeding off 1000-1500 J/kg elevated instability and effective-layer shear up to 50 knots resulted in isolated storms producing considerable large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was ping pong to golf ball size, lasting up to eight minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado from picture posted on social media.  Brief path across agricultural land east southeast of Eldora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued across portions of the state through the latter portion of June that led to another round of river flooding. Several points along the West Fork of the Des Moines remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Cedar Falls crested at 90.06 feet on 25 June 2018 at 01:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued across portions of the state through the latter portion of June that led to another round of river flooding. Several points along the West Fork of the Des Moines remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge crested 4 times during the period the river was in flood. It remained in flood into July. The first crest was at 11.24 feet on 21 June 2018 at 07:00 UTC, the second at 12.75 feet on 23 June 2018 at 04:45 UTC, the third at 12.88 feet on 24 June 2018 at 21:15 UTC, and the fourth crest at 20.26 feet on 27 June 2018 at 22:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.28 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.01 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media, KICD Radio, relayed public report of heavy rainfall of 2.2 inches near Lost Island Lake over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Solidly within southwest upper level flow, the area found itself within an active period with multiple rounds of thunderstorms. Morning convection amounted to little more than moderate showers and thunderstorms, giving way to a short break before a potent short wave, increased low level jet, and increased theta-e advection initiated another round of strong to severe thunderstorms. Even with morning convection and lingering cloud cover, MUCAPE values approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the early to mid evening hours, along with marginally supportive effective bulk shear (about 30 kts), and precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches. As convection strolled northeast, a few strong to severe cores went up, providing periods of heavy rainfall and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several gusts of 55 to 60 mph measured with home weather station. There was also hail, but of unknown size.

Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in the county with two roads still closed near Livermore. Deep washouts had occurred on some county roads in the northeastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued across portions of the state through the latter portion of June that led to another round of river flooding. Several points along the West Fork of the Des Moines remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt crested twice through the period of flooding. The river remained in flood into July. The first crest was at 12.35 feet on 24 June 2018 at 23:30 UTC, and the second one was at 12.64 feet on 28 June 2018 at 16:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued across portions of the state through the latter portion of June that led to another round of river flooding. Several points along the West Fork of the Des Moines remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raccoon River at Perry crested at 18.04 feet on 23 June 2018 at 14:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.75 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of August 1st. These storms dropped some nickel sized hail in New Hampton (Chickasaw County).

Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Dodge Regional Airport AWOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.17 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a 67 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCCI relayed a report from a viewer of a large tree down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 3.86 inches yesterday and overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An eventful day played out across Iowa, carried out in two parts. First was a wave of strong to severe storms during the morning hours across much of northern Iowa, followed by a subsequent wave of storms across southern Iowa. Respectively, the storms produced primarily severe hail and then severe winds and heavy rainfall.  ||Environmentally, the setup was relatively prime for organized convection and to a degree, training storms.  A relatively stationary front (slowly drifting southward later in the day) laid across Iowa from southwest to northeast throughout the day on the 28th. On the warm side of the boundary,  MUCAPE values were in excess of 2000 J/kg already early in the morning, growing to 3000-4000 J/kg by the afternoon. Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded heavy rainfall of 3.19 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers developed over northeast Iowa during the afternoon of August 10th. A funnel cloud formed with one of these showers and was visible near Maynard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Maynard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.3 inches over the last 24 hours.

Other supporting factors for organized convection were present as well, including 40 to 50+ kts of effective bulk shear, hail CAPE in excess of 600 J/kg (especially in the morning round), and effective storm relative helicity around or in excess of 200 m2/s2. Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of agricultural land occurred northwest of Spencer as the Little Sioux River above Spencer (gage site SPII4) crested 0.9 feet above flood stage on October 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level jet streak moved into southeast South Dakota during the late evening of August 4, a cluster of storms developed in south central South Dakota along and in advance of a surface trough axis where elevated instability was over 2000 J/kg. These storms moved in a general east to southeast direction into northwest Iowa, becoming outflow dominant with damaging winds and even a rare nighttime tornado.

What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported wind gusts to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of August 28th. These storms dropped hail from Floyd County east into Clayton County. The largest hail size reported was two inches in diameter in St. Lucas (Fayette County). The storms also produced some wind damage with trees blown down in Charles City (Floyd County) and power lines downed in Nashua (Chickasaw County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Charles City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter near Des Moines International Airport reported heavy rainfall of about 3 inches yesterday and overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported ping pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Solidly within southwest upper level flow, the area found itself within an active period with multiple rounds of thunderstorms. Morning convection amounted to little more than moderate showers and thunderstorms, giving way to a short break before a potent short wave, increased low level jet, and increased theta-e advection initiated another round of strong to severe thunderstorms. Even with morning convection and lingering cloud cover, MUCAPE values approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range by the early to mid evening hours, along with marginally supportive effective bulk shear (about 30 kts), and precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches. As convection strolled northeast, a few strong to severe cores went up, providing periods of heavy rainfall and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 4.30 inches since 830 pm Sunday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Synoptically speaking, widespread severe weather potential was not in the cards for the Iowa area with a fairly stationary boundary well to the south and little to speak of with regards to surface features. That said, elevated storms were possible across southern Iowa with low level jet initiated storms each of the very early morning hours of August 31st and then again during the evening hours. The initial early morning round propagated slowly northward after growing from storms in northeast Kansas, while the evening round initiated across southwest Iowa. ||Environmentally, there was some support for at least brief organization with effective bulk shear in excess of 30 to 40 kts and MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 to 3000 J/kg during both events. When all was said and done, only a small handful of hail reports were received, with the large being 2 inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail along with periods of heavy rainfall including 1.2 inches of rain in about 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a rope funnel near the intersection of P57 and Highway 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several instances of large branches down on trees on the east side of town.

In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station near the intersection of Cherry St and Rea Rd measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall dominated the story on the 19th and into the 20th as western Iowa found itself in a favorable setup for sustained moderate to briefly heavy rainfall.  Initially, showers and storms along a weak frontal boundary as part of a larger upper level trough. Showers continued throughout the day, especially as a more organized surface low moved towards southern Iowa out of eastern Kansas. Given the area's location in relation to the surface low, severe weather was of little concern with nominal to moderate CAPE at best and little other support for organized storms of any kind. Precipitable water values were in excess of 1.5 inches through the 19th and well into the 20th, helping produce efficient and continuous rainfall as the upper level trough and surface low slowly moved through. While truly heavy instantaneous rainfall was relatively hard to come by, the sustained light to moderate rainfall produced heavy rainfall values in excess of 4 inches in a number of locations, including values as high was 5.8 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 4.70 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.89 inches over roughly the last 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported storm total heavy rainfall of 4.24 inches.

Understandably, composite indices such as the significant hail parameter and the supercell composite parameter returned values that raised concerns for severe weather potential. ||At the end of the day, reports were split into two distinct areas. The first was large hail over northern Iowa with the early morning convection, followed by damaging winds and heavy rainfall with the afternoon and evening convection over southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail on the north side of Latimer at about 1255 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Synoptically speaking, widespread severe weather potential was not in the cards for the Iowa area with a fairly stationary boundary well to the south and little to speak of with regards to surface features. That said, elevated storms were possible across southern Iowa with low level jet initiated storms each of the very early morning hours of August 31st and then again during the evening hours. The initial early morning round propagated slowly northward after growing from storms in northeast Kansas, while the evening round initiated across southwest Iowa. ||Environmentally, there was some support for at least brief organization with effective bulk shear in excess of 30 to 40 kts and MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 to 3000 J/kg during both events. When all was said and done, only a small handful of hail reports were received, with the large being 2 inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a few hail stones quarter in size, along with a lot of pea sized hail.
