What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported large trees and power lines down in Murray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous outflow boundaries from overnight convection re-fired across southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa around mid afternoon as diurnal heating weakened the cap and increased MLCAPE to around 2000 J/kg. The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, but overall deep-layer shear remained quite weak with right-moving and mean storm motion vector magnitudes at 10 knots or less northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall rates of 2 to 3 inches an hour produced significant street flooding in Lake Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from 3 to 6 inches in a couple hours brought a rapid onset to flash flooding around the Iowa Great Lakes.  Significant street flooding began in Arnolds Park, with water flowing across and blocking Highway 71 at 1633CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A setup for some rotating storms was in place over much of central and southern Iowa. A low pressure system situated northwest of the state slid to the northeast throughout the day, eventually moving into western and central Minnesota. Given its fairly mature frontal situation, an occluded front dropped into northwest Iowa with the warm front extending E/SE from central Iowa and the cold front dropping back to the SW. At the surface within the warm sector observations were favorable with temperatures into the mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Looking in more detail, lifted condensation levels were quite low with values under 750 meters, MUCAPE values were modest in the 1000-3000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40 kts, and effective storm relative helicity was around 200 m2/s2 to name a few. Resulting composite indices correctly highlighted an increased chance for supercells and tornadic potential as seen with supercell composite values in excess of 12 and significant tornado parameter values in the 3 to 5 range in the afternoon. All of which taking into account the general setup, understandably unable to account for storms potentially tapping into additional shear and helicity generated from rooting themselves along outflow boundaries. ||The result was a sizable tornado outbreak across Iowa, including more than a dozen confirmed tornadoes. The tornadoes of most note occurred in the NE Des Moines Metro (specifically Bondurant), Pella, and Marshalltown. The Bondurant area actually experienced two tornadoes, which were concurrently on the ground, but fortunately only one actually caused damage (EF2) within town. The Pella tornado tacked on the north side of town, directly hitting the Vermeer plant, causing EF3 damage. The Marshalltown tornado entered the town from the NW and tracked into the downtown area before exiting and weakening east of town. Widespread damage was experienced in Marshalltown including to the Lennox plant and the County Courthouse.  All in all, while numerous injuries were experienced and millions of dollars of damage, no direct fatalities were reported from the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a funnel cloud near the intersection of Highway 65 and Highway 210.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving occluded surface low continued to trek into and across Iowa, provided another opportunity for some periods of moderate to heavy rainfall and funnel clouds. Unlike the day prior, overall conditions were not quite as conducive for heavy rainfall and numerous funnel clouds, but were still an option. Overall instability was less with MUCAPE values only around 1000 J/kg, but with boundaries in the area and lifted condensation levels around 750 m, the possibility of a few funnel clouds was present. Overall, the day was quiet by recent days standards, with mostly showers to weak thunderstorms present. One funnel cloud report was made, and at least one instance of total heavy rainfall of 2 inches or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser reported a funnel cloud 2 miles west of the intersection of Highway 30 and Highway 330 along Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff's Office reported multiple roads covered in and around Livermore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unremarkable day setup-wise resulted in a few strong storms during the evening hours that produced a small handful of damaging winds and wind gust reports. ||A frontal boundary was positioned well west/northwest of the state, leaving Iowa firmly within the warm sector. Surface observations reflected at least as much with temperatures into the low 90s concurrent with dew points in the low to mid 70s. MUCAPE values, as one might expect, approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range but was devoid of supportive shear and any concentrated lift. Lingering morning showers and convection were working their way up from northeast Kansas, traveling to the northeast, before tapping into the moist, unstable Iowa air mass. Fortunately, with the lack of supportive shear profiles and hail CAPE (under 500 j/kg), severe weather was hard to come by. Though with DCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, a few storms were able to produce severe downdrafts and gust fronts leading to some minor to moderate tree damage and recorded wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported large tree limbs broken.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, occluded surface low pressure system was situated northwest of the area with its associated boundaries draped across western and southern Iowa.  The environment south of the warm front/pseudo-stationary front was ripe with MUCAPE in excess of 300 J/kg, but devoid of many other supporting variables. Effective shear was borderline at best around 30 kts and DCAPE and hail CAPE were generally mediocre. Lending some credence to heavy rain, precipitable water values were around 1.7 inches or greater by the afternoon and the expectation of little overall movement to the surface fronts. ||Storms initiated in the early afternoon near/along the warm/stationary front and were predominantly sub-severe given the inability to organize. Even so, a few strong to damaging wind gusts were observed early along with a quarter inch hail report. As storms drifted north of the boundary, they quickly weakened and turned into more of rain threat. As the afternoon and evening progressed, numerous other storms initiated around the cold front and occluded fronts further to the north and west and slowly drifted eastward. Areas in northern Iowa found themselves within an area that saw multiple slow-moving storms track across, resulting in heavy rainfall and a couple flash flooding reports. The greatest totals were public reported in the 3 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued across portions of the state through the latter portion of June that led to another round of river flooding. Several points along the West Fork of the Des Moines remained in flood into July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Fork of the Des Moines River at Algona crested twice through the period of flooding. The first crest was 18.69 feet on 22 June 2018 at 22:15 UTC, the second crest was 19.33 feet on 26 June 2018 at 10:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With already high water levels from periodic early June rains, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains and interacted with a nearly stationary region of baroclinicity during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 3 to 6 inches of rainfall in the lower portions of the basin, with amounts from 7 to 11 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes. Crop and pasture land was inundated for almost a month, along with many roads and city parks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of prolific rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 4 to 7 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20 and 24. Specific weekly totals included 3.65 4 miles east of Sioux Rapids, 4.03 at Spencer and 6.70 near Everly. Record flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated, as well as the city park in Sioux Rapids as the river crested at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) at 5.4 feet above flood stage on June 27. The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous outflow boundaries from overnight convection re-fired across southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa around mid afternoon as diurnal heating weakened the cap and increased MLCAPE to around 2000 J/kg. The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, but overall deep-layer shear remained quite weak with right-moving and mean storm motion vector magnitudes at 10 knots or less northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches in a couple of hours created flash flooding in the upper Little Sioux River basin. State highway 9 was closed between 150th and 160th St. when a couple feet of water encroached on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A setup for some rotating storms was in place over much of central and southern Iowa. A low pressure system situated northwest of the state slid to the northeast throughout the day, eventually moving into western and central Minnesota. Given its fairly mature frontal situation, an occluded front dropped into northwest Iowa with the warm front extending E/SE from central Iowa and the cold front dropping back to the SW. At the surface within the warm sector observations were favorable with temperatures into the mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Looking in more detail, lifted condensation levels were quite low with values under 750 meters, MUCAPE values were modest in the 1000-3000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40 kts, and effective storm relative helicity was around 200 m2/s2 to name a few. Resulting composite indices correctly highlighted an increased chance for supercells and tornadic potential as seen with supercell composite values in excess of 12 and significant tornado parameter values in the 3 to 5 range in the afternoon. All of which taking into account the general setup, understandably unable to account for storms potentially tapping into additional shear and helicity generated from rooting themselves along outflow boundaries. ||The result was a sizable tornado outbreak across Iowa, including more than a dozen confirmed tornadoes. The tornadoes of most note occurred in the NE Des Moines Metro (specifically Bondurant), Pella, and Marshalltown. The Bondurant area actually experienced two tornadoes, which were concurrently on the ground, but fortunately only one actually caused damage (EF2) within town. The Pella tornado tacked on the north side of town, directly hitting the Vermeer plant, causing EF3 damage. The Marshalltown tornado entered the town from the NW and tracked into the downtown area before exiting and weakening east of town. Widespread damage was experienced in Marshalltown including to the Lennox plant and the County Courthouse.  All in all, while numerous injuries were experienced and millions of dollars of damage, no direct fatalities were reported from the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly traditional setup for severe weather was in place over the state, a nice departure from events to this point in the season, with a cold front situated to the northwest moving towards and into the area. Convective initiation was expected along and ahead of the frontal boundary during the afternoon and evening hours, which came to fruition. Supporting features of the environment included SB and MUCAPE values around 1000-2000+ J/kg and effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range. More borderline support for severe potential was seen for hail with CAPE values in the hail growth region around 500 J/kg and DCAPE values around 800-1000 J/kg. All in all, initiated storms were either of the isolated or multi-cellular variety throughout much of the event. With a semi-supportive environment for severe weather, the storms acted as much with multiple reports of nickel to quarter sized hail and wind damage including large tree branches and farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local radio station relayed report of grain bin damage north of Bristow. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief reprieve from multiple days of activity, another round of storms greeted large parts of central Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 14th, through the rest of the morning, and into the afternoon. Traditional severe weather in the form of hail and damaging winds were sporadic, while heavy rain and flash flooding was the main player. The hail and wind reports were low end severe with generally quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and winds less than 70 mph. Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.91 inches over the last 24 hours.

What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported water in basements along with street flooding in Livermore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, occluded surface low pressure system was situated northwest of the area with its associated boundaries draped across western and southern Iowa.  The environment south of the warm front/pseudo-stationary front was ripe with MUCAPE in excess of 300 J/kg, but devoid of many other supporting variables. Effective shear was borderline at best around 30 kts and DCAPE and hail CAPE were generally mediocre. Lending some credence to heavy rain, precipitable water values were around 1.7 inches or greater by the afternoon and the expectation of little overall movement to the surface fronts. ||Storms initiated in the early afternoon near/along the warm/stationary front and were predominantly sub-severe given the inability to organize. Even so, a few strong to damaging wind gusts were observed early along with a quarter inch hail report. As storms drifted north of the boundary, they quickly weakened and turned into more of rain threat. As the afternoon and evening progressed, numerous other storms initiated around the cold front and occluded fronts further to the north and west and slowly drifted eastward. Areas in northern Iowa found themselves within an area that saw multiple slow-moving storms track across, resulting in heavy rainfall and a couple flash flooding reports. The greatest totals were public reported in the 3 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported that US 65 Southbound off-ramp at Exit 77 in Pleasant Hill was under water. The water receded after nearly 5 hours and 45 minutes. Ramp was back open at the time of the report, this is a delayed report.

Specific weekly totals included 3.44 inches at Flandreau, 4.08 inches 6 miles north of Dell Rapids, 4.62 inches at Colton, 8.14 inches near Garretson, 5.67 inches near Hartford, 6.28 inches near Larchwood and a variety of reports from 5 to 7 inches around the Sioux Falls metro area. The river dropped below flood stage on June 24, but returned above flood stage with minor flooding (0.5 feet above flood stage) again on June 28 before falling back below flood stage on June 29.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed northwest of Marshalltown west of the little hamlet of Marietta.  The tornado traveled southeast on the western side of the Iowa River producing extensive crop and tree damage.  The tornado strengthened quickly and took on a wedge appearance as it entered northwest Marshalltown.  The tornado tracked more easterly as it entered town producing widespread EF1 and EF2 damage.  The tornado went directly through the business district of Marshalltown with EF2 and EF3 damage to many structures including the county courthouse which lost the spire off the clock tower.  The tornado continued east through housing areas before encountering the Lennox plant on the east side of town, producing EF3 damage once again.  The tornado began to weaken rapidly after this point and dissipated near power plant located on the east side of town.  The tornado produced a total of 22 direct injuries. An additional indirect injury resulted during the clean-up of storm debris when a tree fell on a man.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee reported that a vehicle was struck by lightning at exit 100 on Interstate 80, badly damaging the vehicle. There were no injuries to the 3 passengers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 3 inch diameter tree branches down along with a few farm sheds toppled. Wide swaths of corn were also reported bent down in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed on east side of the Perry Airport in Dallas County before moving into a housing development.  There were several trees damaged or uprooted along with shingles removed from the roof of a nearby house.  The tornado tracked slowly north northeast with damage mainly to trees and crops along its path.  The tornado crossed from Dallas into Boone County before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief reprieve from multiple days of activity, another round of storms greeted large parts of central Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 14th, through the rest of the morning, and into the afternoon. Traditional severe weather in the form of hail and damaging winds were sporadic, while heavy rain and flash flooding was the main player. The hail and wind reports were low end severe with generally quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and winds less than 70 mph. Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported water up to the bumpers on several vehicles in a parking lot near the Boone and Story county line on R38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall the pattern and antecedent environment were not extremely conducive to severe weather with an environment of around 1000 J/kg and effective shear under 30 kts, but it did not rule out the potential for periods of heavy rainfall. ||High pressure ridging was situated over the SW United States, leaving much of the Upper Midwest along the ring of fire, or northwesterly flow/transition from ridge to trough. A shortwave was moving through the upper level flow, initiating weak convection during the evening and overnight hours of the 7th into the 8th, which was then aided by the nocturnal low level jet. Severe weather was negligible, though several rural roads were eventually closed due to heavy rainfall and localized flooding. With storms initiating and propagating over similar areas much of the night, total heavy rainfall of up to 5.6 inches was reported, with most reports in the 2 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported heavy rainfall of 3.65 inches from about 3:20 am through 8:25 am. The 24 hour total through 8:25 am was 4.06 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 5 to 9 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 6.28 inches near Larchwood, 7.47 inches at Canton, 9.15 inches at Rock Valley, 9.19 inches near Lester and 5.01 inches at Hawarden. Major flooding occurred with South Dakota Highway 46 and Iowa Highway 10 going under water, as well as the city park in Hawarden. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were inundated as the river crested above Hawarden (gage site HWDS2) 13.5 feet above flood stage on June 22. After falling, a second crest 10.8 feet above flood stage occurred on June 27. The river remained at minor flood stage at months end.

Major flooding occurred with South Dakota Highways 48 and 50 as well as Iowa Highway 12 going under water. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were inundated as the river crested at Akron (gage site AKRI4) 7.5 feet above flood stage on June 23. After falling, a second crest 6.2 feet above flood stage occurred on June 28. The river remained at moderate flood stage at months end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled west of the area, across central Nebraska, southeast South Dakota, and central Minnesota, and acted as an initiation point for multiple rounds of storms on the 4th and into the evening and overnight hours. While no one round was tremendous, multiple rounds added up to heavy rainfall values in excess of 3 inches in a number of locations. Given a warm, moist environment as well including precipitable water values around 2 inches and warm cloud layer depths in excess of 11-12 kft, storms were predominantly efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ames Municipal Airport ASOS measured heavy rainfall of 2.81 inches between 4pm and 1am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous outflow boundaries from overnight convection re-fired across southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa around mid afternoon as diurnal heating weakened the cap and increased MLCAPE to around 2000 J/kg. The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, but overall deep-layer shear remained quite weak with right-moving and mean storm motion vector magnitudes at 10 knots or less northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of minor flooding of crop and pasture land as the Big Sioux River at Fairview (gage site FVWS2) crested 1.3 feet above flood stage on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering boundary found itself draped across Iowa on the 18th and into the 19th of June, acting as a continued focusing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms.  A weakly sheared environment took away form the potential for sustained severe weather, however with as much as 3000 to 4000 J/kg MUCAPE during the afternoon and early evening, opportunities for at least brief severe storms were possible. More concerning though was the heavy rainfall potential with the near stationary boundary, surface dew points in the low to mid 70s, precipitable water values around 2.0 inches, and at least a weak low level jet continuing to initiate storms and advect in more moisture. ||The day and overnight generally played out as much with brief strong to severe storms during or just after peak heating, followed by numerous rounds of showers and storms initiated along or near the boundary.  Besides the small handful of severe reports, heavy rainfall in the 2 to 4 inch range dominated, including a flooding report around Sac City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Denison Airport AWOS recorded heavy rainfall of 3.14 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 9 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 5 to 9 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20, 24 and 25. Specific weekly totals included 7.47 inches at Canton, 9.15 inches at Rock Valley, 4.47 inches at Akron and 5.01 inches at Hawarden. Major flooding occurred with South Dakota Highway 50 as well as Iowa Highway 3 going under water. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were inundated as the river crested near Richland (gage site BSRS2) 7.4 feet above flood stage on June 24. The river remained at major flood stage at months end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of moderate flooding. Significant amounts of agricultural land near the river were inundated. The Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) crested 3.4 feet above flood stage on October 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported numerous large trees down in St. Charles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled west of the area, across central Nebraska, southeast South Dakota, and central Minnesota, and acted as an initiation point for multiple rounds of storms on the 4th and into the evening and overnight hours. While no one round was tremendous, multiple rounds added up to heavy rainfall values in excess of 3 inches in a number of locations. Given a warm, moist environment as well including precipitable water values around 2 inches and warm cloud layer depths in excess of 11-12 kft, storms were predominantly efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.55inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Longer term dry conditions were made worse once again by short term acute dryness in portions of south central and southeast Iowa.  Rainfall deficits quickly mounted in southern Iowa during May into June with severe drought conditions returning to the area after relatively normal precipitation during the preceding cool season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of short term acute dryness and a 3 year longer term deficit led to the quick development of severe drought conditions in southern Wapello county in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported tress, power lines, and some outbuildings flipped and destroyed near the intersection of Highway 92 and County Road T17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief reprieve from multiple days of activity, another round of storms greeted large parts of central Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 14th, through the rest of the morning, and into the afternoon. Traditional severe weather in the form of hail and damaging winds were sporadic, while heavy rain and flash flooding was the main player. The hail and wind reports were low end severe with generally quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and winds less than 70 mph. Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kossuth County Sheriff's Office reported road submerged near Spring Valley Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, occluded surface low pressure system was situated northwest of the area with its associated boundaries draped across western and southern Iowa.  The environment south of the warm front/pseudo-stationary front was ripe with MUCAPE in excess of 300 J/kg, but devoid of many other supporting variables. Effective shear was borderline at best around 30 kts and DCAPE and hail CAPE were generally mediocre. Lending some credence to heavy rain, precipitable water values were around 1.7 inches or greater by the afternoon and the expectation of little overall movement to the surface fronts. ||Storms initiated in the early afternoon near/along the warm/stationary front and were predominantly sub-severe given the inability to organize. Even so, a few strong to damaging wind gusts were observed early along with a quarter inch hail report. As storms drifted north of the boundary, they quickly weakened and turned into more of rain threat. As the afternoon and evening progressed, numerous other storms initiated around the cold front and occluded fronts further to the north and west and slowly drifted eastward. Areas in northern Iowa found themselves within an area that saw multiple slow-moving storms track across, resulting in heavy rainfall and a couple flash flooding reports. The greatest totals were public reported in the 3 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported trees down and power outages. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms produced some damaging wind gusts across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of June 2nd. Trees and power lines were blown down near Osage (Mitchell County), Cresco (Howard County) and Charles City (Floyd County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and tree limbs were blown down near New Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started off wet and fairly active, with widespread rain moving across the state in the form of a degrading quasi-linear convective system. Initially, the strongest portion of the QLCS tracked across the southeast portions of Iowa, following the MUCAPE gradient (1000-3000 J/kg) and better effective shear environment. While heavy rainfall was the most widespread effect, severe wind gusts were recorded in and around Atlantic, IA.  Further north, convective activity was less severe, primarily just rain, until a wake low developed and continued to track eastward. A number of severe wind gusts and damage reports were recorded, including a measured 88 mph gust in Hampton.  Lastly, as the parent low tracked across the state later in the day, a funnel cloud was pictured in the Readlyn area along the primary boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a 1 and 1/2 foot diameter tree down and estimated 50 plus mph wind gusts.  Report came via social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reporter water over the road of unknown depth at Elm and Nelson Ave in the town of Coalville. Enough rocks were washed on the road that it needed to be cleared away by heavy equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall the pattern and antecedent environment were not extremely conducive to severe weather with an environment of around 1000 J/kg and effective shear under 30 kts, but it did not rule out the potential for periods of heavy rainfall. ||High pressure ridging was situated over the SW United States, leaving much of the Upper Midwest along the ring of fire, or northwesterly flow/transition from ridge to trough. A shortwave was moving through the upper level flow, initiating weak convection during the evening and overnight hours of the 7th into the 8th, which was then aided by the nocturnal low level jet. Severe weather was negligible, though several rural roads were eventually closed due to heavy rainfall and localized flooding. With storms initiating and propagating over similar areas much of the night, total heavy rainfall of up to 5.6 inches was reported, with most reports in the 2 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A river gauge location along the Winnebago River recorded heavy rainfall of 2.13 inches.

Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 3.65 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported about 1 foot of fast moving water across Highway 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded/occluding surface low pressure system slowly worked its way into western and southwest Iowa during the morning and afternoon of the 25th. Given its location, Iowa was situated north of the warm front, which was much further south into central Missouri. Even so, the environment was cool yet fairly saturated in the low levels with temperatures in the low to mid 70s and dew points in the mid to upper 60s. ||Low level flow was up over the occluded front, providing additional lift, within an environment of around 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, effective shear up to around 40 kts, lifted condensation levels approaching 500 m at times, and plenty of low level 0-1 km storm relative helicity in excess of 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it was a ripe day for general moderate to heavy rainfall, funnel clouds, and weak landspout tornadoes. The majority of the action revolved around heavy rainfall that totaled in excess of 3 inches in many locations, but also involved multiple funnel cloud reports and a lone brief tornado near Delphos in Ringgold County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down in the hamlet of Delphos with tree debris being tossed around.  A rather diffuse funnel for much of its existence, very little damage occurred with this tornado.  The event was captured on video by a few different storm chasers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, occluded surface low pressure system was situated northwest of the area with its associated boundaries draped across western and southern Iowa.  The environment south of the warm front/pseudo-stationary front was ripe with MUCAPE in excess of 300 J/kg, but devoid of many other supporting variables. Effective shear was borderline at best around 30 kts and DCAPE and hail CAPE were generally mediocre. Lending some credence to heavy rain, precipitable water values were around 1.7 inches or greater by the afternoon and the expectation of little overall movement to the surface fronts. ||Storms initiated in the early afternoon near/along the warm/stationary front and were predominantly sub-severe given the inability to organize. Even so, a few strong to damaging wind gusts were observed early along with a quarter inch hail report. As storms drifted north of the boundary, they quickly weakened and turned into more of rain threat. As the afternoon and evening progressed, numerous other storms initiated around the cold front and occluded fronts further to the north and west and slowly drifted eastward. Areas in northern Iowa found themselves within an area that saw multiple slow-moving storms track across, resulting in heavy rainfall and a couple flash flooding reports. The greatest totals were public reported in the 3 to 4 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.88 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening of June 9th. These severe storms produced up to 75 mph wind gusts in and near Rockford (Floyd County) with  extensive damage in town. At least 300 hundred trees were destroyed with some structural damage to buildings. A light pole at the high school football field was blown down onto storage buildings for the baseball and softball fields. An estimated 80 mph wind gust occurred west of Floyd (Floyd County). These storms also produced up to golf ball sized hail near Nora Springs (Floyd County) and west of Charles City (Floyd County). A section of the Avenue of the Saints was closed south of Charles City to allow utility crews to repair power lines. This complex was then followed by another round of thunderstorms later in the evening and the combined rainfall produced flash flooding that sent water over numerous roads across parts of Floyd, Fayette and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell south of Nora Springs.

These severe storms produced up to 75 mph wind gusts in and near Rockford (Floyd County) with  extensive damage in town. At least 300 hundred trees were destroyed with some structural damage to buildings. A light pole at the high school football field was blown down onto storage buildings for the baseball and softball fields. An estimated 80 mph wind gust occurred west of Floyd (Floyd County). These storms also produced up to golf ball sized hail near Nora Springs (Floyd County) and west of Charles City (Floyd County). A section of the Avenue of the Saints was closed south of Charles City to allow utility crews to repair power lines. This complex was then followed by another round of thunderstorms later in the evening and the combined rainfall produced flash flooding that sent water over numerous roads across parts of Floyd, Fayette and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down west of Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief reprieve from multiple days of activity, another round of storms greeted large parts of central Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 14th, through the rest of the morning, and into the afternoon. Traditional severe weather in the form of hail and damaging winds were sporadic, while heavy rain and flash flooding was the main player. The hail and wind reports were low end severe with generally quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and winds less than 70 mph. Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported along the Wapsipinicon River throughout the county and especially at Tripoli and along portions of the East Fork Wapsipinicon River throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early June activity continued with yet another day of convective activity that included severe storms and heavy rainfall. A relatively stationary boundary was situated across the western and southern portions of the state, acting as a focus for convective activity throughout the morning and early afternoon as convection lingered from the previous day. While there was not much opportunity to recover thermodynamically due to cloud cover, MUCAPE values remained in excess of 1000 J/kg in areas of convection and up to 2000 to 3000 J/kg in areas just to the west or south of the convection. Supporting parameters suggested the opportunity for a few organized storms with effective shear in the 30 to 40 kt range and low level helicity in the 100 to 200 m2/s2 neighborhood. Another concern was the potential for training storms with the stationary boundary in place and expectations of low level jet aided initiation in the overnight hours.||In the end, a few severe storms were seen during the afternoon and evening hours, including hail approaching 2 inches in diameter, wind gusts to near 70 mph, and a vehicle heavily damaged from a lightning strike.  Heavy rainfall was also experienced, though fortunately most locations were spared the heaviest totals, with most in the 2 to 3.5 inch range and a couple in excess of 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported multiple trees down, including one snapped at the trunk that was approximately 18 inches in diameter.  Several power lines were also reported down in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Without even looking at supporting variables, the setup for impactful weather could be gleaned from the general setup. A weak surface pressure system was slowly working its way across Iowa, situating its relevant boundaries primarily across southwest and southern Iowa. Within the warm sector, dew points were high in the low to mid 70s, especially considering afternoon air temperatures were only into the low 80s. Even north of the warm front and west of the cold front, the air mass was ripe for at least the potential for sustained rainfall with surface dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. ||Looking at the details, both heavy rainfall and tornadoes appeared in play. First, within the warm sector, MUCAPE values were within the 1000 to 2000+ J/kg neighborhood, lifted condensation levels were under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE was around or in excess of 100 J/kg, effective shear was mediocre away form the fronts, and surface vorticity was present. North of and wrapping around the surface low, any support for severe weather was understandably lackluster given its location, but had the potential for sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall given the humid environment and slow moving surface low.  ||Given the setup, there were concerns for flooding issues, especially given previous rainfalls in the month and moist soils, and severe weather including funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. What transpired was heavy rainfall and flooding in and around northwest Iowa and then multiple funnel cloud and weak tornado reports in central and southeast Iowa. In total, 4 weak tornadoes were confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported estimated wind gusts around 60 mph and that corn has been flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa remained entrenched in the relative ring of fire weather pattern. Morning convection lingered across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa through the morning, before generally subsiding. By the early afternoon though, ingredients for severe weather were well in place with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg, effective shear around 40 kts, and 1-3 km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.  All in all, it suggested a day with the potential for supercellular storms with all modes of severe weather possible. By the evening, as much as true with multiple funnel cloud or brief tornado touchdown reports, numerous hail reports, a couple of wind reports, and heavy rain in excess of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported up to 2.5 inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled west of the area, across central Nebraska, southeast South Dakota, and central Minnesota, and acted as an initiation point for multiple rounds of storms on the 4th and into the evening and overnight hours. While no one round was tremendous, multiple rounds added up to heavy rainfall values in excess of 3 inches in a number of locations. Given a warm, moist environment as well including precipitable water values around 2 inches and warm cloud layer depths in excess of 11-12 kft, storms were predominantly efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.95 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported large trees down, damage to vehicles, and power lines down near Boston Ave in Indianola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A setup for some rotating storms was in place over much of central and southern Iowa. A low pressure system situated northwest of the state slid to the northeast throughout the day, eventually moving into western and central Minnesota. Given its fairly mature frontal situation, an occluded front dropped into northwest Iowa with the warm front extending E/SE from central Iowa and the cold front dropping back to the SW. At the surface within the warm sector observations were favorable with temperatures into the mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Looking in more detail, lifted condensation levels were quite low with values under 750 meters, MUCAPE values were modest in the 1000-3000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40 kts, and effective storm relative helicity was around 200 m2/s2 to name a few. Resulting composite indices correctly highlighted an increased chance for supercells and tornadic potential as seen with supercell composite values in excess of 12 and significant tornado parameter values in the 3 to 5 range in the afternoon. All of which taking into account the general setup, understandably unable to account for storms potentially tapping into additional shear and helicity generated from rooting themselves along outflow boundaries. ||The result was a sizable tornado outbreak across Iowa, including more than a dozen confirmed tornadoes. The tornadoes of most note occurred in the NE Des Moines Metro (specifically Bondurant), Pella, and Marshalltown. The Bondurant area actually experienced two tornadoes, which were concurrently on the ground, but fortunately only one actually caused damage (EF2) within town. The Pella tornado tacked on the north side of town, directly hitting the Vermeer plant, causing EF3 damage. The Marshalltown tornado entered the town from the NW and tracked into the downtown area before exiting and weakening east of town. Widespread damage was experienced in Marshalltown including to the Lennox plant and the County Courthouse.  All in all, while numerous injuries were experienced and millions of dollars of damage, no direct fatalities were reported from the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado remained in agricultural fields northwest of Prairie City for entire path length.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very brief reprieve from multiple days of activity, another round of storms greeted large parts of central Iowa during the very early morning hours of the 14th, through the rest of the morning, and into the afternoon. Traditional severe weather in the form of hail and damaging winds were sporadic, while heavy rain and flash flooding was the main player. The hail and wind reports were low end severe with generally quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and winds less than 70 mph. Heavy rain was rather impressive with a number of spots receiving 4 to 6  inches of rain, which helped lead to periods of flash flooding. ||The environment lended itself to heavy rain potential and marginal severe weather. Much of the state found itself on the cool side of a warm frontal boundary situated off to the west and the south.  Given the location in relation to the warm front and nocturnal initiation, MUCAPE values were modest at best around 1000 to 2000 J/kg in central Iowa with better values to the wets and south and lesser values further north and east. Effective shear values increased through the overnight hours with aid from the nocturnal low level jet, generally finding itself in the 30 to 40+ kt range. The aforementioned nocturnal low level jet not only aided in initiation, but also ushered in plentiful moisture allowing precipitable water values to approach the 1.5 to 2.0 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ames Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.61 inches.

A shortwave was moving through the upper level flow, initiating weak convection during the evening and overnight hours of the 7th into the 8th, which was then aided by the nocturnal low level jet. Severe weather was negligible, though several rural roads were eventually closed due to heavy rainfall and localized flooding. With storms initiating and propagating over similar areas much of the night, total heavy rainfall of up to 5.6 inches was reported, with most reports in the 2 to 4 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unremarkable day setup-wise resulted in a few strong storms during the evening hours that produced a small handful of damaging winds and wind gust reports. ||A frontal boundary was positioned well west/northwest of the state, leaving Iowa firmly within the warm sector. Surface observations reflected at least as much with temperatures into the low 90s concurrent with dew points in the low to mid 70s. MUCAPE values, as one might expect, approached the 2000-3000 J/kg range but was devoid of supportive shear and any concentrated lift. Lingering morning showers and convection were working their way up from northeast Kansas, traveling to the northeast, before tapping into the moist, unstable Iowa air mass. Fortunately, with the lack of supportive shear profiles and hail CAPE (under 500 j/kg), severe weather was hard to come by. Though with DCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, a few storms were able to produce severe downdrafts and gust fronts leading to some minor to moderate tree damage and recorded wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT RWIS site recorded a 63 mph wind gust about 6 miles south of Denison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled west of the area, across central Nebraska, southeast South Dakota, and central Minnesota, and acted as an initiation point for multiple rounds of storms on the 4th and into the evening and overnight hours. While no one round was tremendous, multiple rounds added up to heavy rainfall values in excess of 3 inches in a number of locations. Given a warm, moist environment as well including precipitable water values around 2 inches and warm cloud layer depths in excess of 11-12 kft, storms were predominantly efficient rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.73 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: What started out as a cluster of low level jet induced storms over central Nebraska, turned into a derecho that had severe winds and left a path of wind damage from Nebraska into Illinois and then down into parts of southeast Missouri and western Kentucky and Tennessee. The initial convection initiated shortly after 3 am CDT in north central Nebraska within a very unstable nocturnal environment. MUCAPE values were in excess of 5000 J/kg throughout much of central Nebraska, along with DCAPE in excess of 1200-1500 J/kg, effective shear around 30 to 40 kts, roughly perpendicularly oriented 0-3 km shear vectors to the storm orientation, and supporting composite parameters such as MCS Maintenance and Derecho Composite that pointed towards a potentially long-lived convective complex. ||The environment (thermodynamic, moisture, and wind profiles) roughly remained the same as the complex moved eastward, before taking on a more southeast and eventually southern motion, following a strong CAPE gradient.  What transpired in Iowa was an eastward moving complex that took on a more linear orientation throughout the morning and quickly trekked across the southern third of the state through the morning and early afternoon. In its wake was dozens of reports of whole trees down, power lines and poles down, other damage reports, and measured wind gusts in excess of 65-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement replayed several reports of trees down between Martensdale and Indianola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A setup for some rotating storms was in place over much of central and southern Iowa. A low pressure system situated northwest of the state slid to the northeast throughout the day, eventually moving into western and central Minnesota. Given its fairly mature frontal situation, an occluded front dropped into northwest Iowa with the warm front extending E/SE from central Iowa and the cold front dropping back to the SW. At the surface within the warm sector observations were favorable with temperatures into the mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Looking in more detail, lifted condensation levels were quite low with values under 750 meters, MUCAPE values were modest in the 1000-3000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40 kts, and effective storm relative helicity was around 200 m2/s2 to name a few. Resulting composite indices correctly highlighted an increased chance for supercells and tornadic potential as seen with supercell composite values in excess of 12 and significant tornado parameter values in the 3 to 5 range in the afternoon. All of which taking into account the general setup, understandably unable to account for storms potentially tapping into additional shear and helicity generated from rooting themselves along outflow boundaries. ||The result was a sizable tornado outbreak across Iowa, including more than a dozen confirmed tornadoes. The tornadoes of most note occurred in the NE Des Moines Metro (specifically Bondurant), Pella, and Marshalltown. The Bondurant area actually experienced two tornadoes, which were concurrently on the ground, but fortunately only one actually caused damage (EF2) within town. The Pella tornado tacked on the north side of town, directly hitting the Vermeer plant, causing EF3 damage. The Marshalltown tornado entered the town from the NW and tracked into the downtown area before exiting and weakening east of town. Widespread damage was experienced in Marshalltown including to the Lennox plant and the County Courthouse.  All in all, while numerous injuries were experienced and millions of dollars of damage, no direct fatalities were reported from the tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Path continued into Marion county from Jasper county.  In Marion county, the tornado remained in rural cropland damaging crops and trees.

The tornado then encountered the Vermeer facility on the east side of town producing EF3 damage to one of the large buildings along with tossing numerous cars about the facility.  13 injuries occurred at the Vermeer facility with 6 requiring transport to the hospital.  The tornado then turned slightly eastward before passing into Mahaska county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Longer term dry conditions were made worse once again by short term acute dryness in portions of south central and southeast Iowa.  Rainfall deficits quickly mounted in southern Iowa during May into June with severe drought conditions returning to the area after relatively normal precipitation during the preceding cool season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of short term acute dryness and a 3 year longer term deficit led to the quick development of severe drought conditions in far southeast Monroe county in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a hail- and wind-filled day across large parts of Iowa as two distinct rounds of severe weather affected the state.  A remnant QLCS system moved out of the eastern Dakotas and southern Minnesota during the early morning hours, maintaining itself in a relatively ripe environment of 2000+ J/kg of MUCAPE and around 30 to 40 kts of effective shear (though fading through the morning). While the storms were able to maintain their vigor, sub-severe to severe hail was seen through parts of Pocahontas, Humboldt, Wright, and Calhoun Counties before they weakened and eventually subsided entirely. ||Later that afternoon, as the surface boundary entered the state, storms once again fired up across the northern to north central areas of the state. The convective environment across the western half or so of the state was quite ripe with as much as 5000 to 6000 J/kg MUCAPE. Effective shear was lacking, however, with predominantly 35 kts or less existing.  Hail CAPE approached 1000 J/kg and DCAPE to around 1400 J/kg respectively, indicating the potential for both severe hail and severe downdrafts even with the lackluster shear profile. Afternoon convection saw initial isolated and multi-cell clusters form with hail as the primary threat evolving into a QLCS with a primary wind threat by the mid-evening hours. In total, in excess of 50 severe hail and wind reports were received, including a 2 inch hail report and numerous reports of tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees and power lines downed in Knoxville.
