While some damaging wind was still ongoing, the primary concern was for heavy rainfall, flash flooding, and eventually a flash flood emergency. The Des Moines metro area took on widespread amounts of 6 or more inches, including a few reports of nearly 10 inches.  The main impacts revolved around flash flooding and the flash flood emergency with sewer systems and local streams absolutely overwhelmed. Dozens of vehicles were flooded or stranded, with numerous water rescues performed. Many streams were out of their banks, and an individual was swept away and killed during the flash flooding. One home had flooding and a gas leak that lead to an explosion of the home. Luckily all occupants had already been evacuated. Two streams with NWS stream gauge observations, Four Mile Creek and Walnut Creek, set all-time crest records.  Countless basements and businesses were flooded across the metro area and dozens of homes were completely flooded out on the southeast side where Four Mile Creek flooding was the worst. Two businesses had roof collapses from the heavy rain as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.71 inches over the last 24 hours. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that moved across eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours of August 28th. The line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds mainly south of a line from Cedar Rapids to Clinton Iowa. The heaviest rain fell south of Interstate 80, where 2 to 4 inch amounts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front interacting with a series of weak upper disturbances produced rounds of storms.  Flash flooding occurred with areas of 3 to 6 inches of rain. A few storms also produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported two paved roads in northern Keokuk County were closed with water over the roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dispatch center reported multiple roads with high water over them in the city of Muscatine. Multiple vehicles were reported stalled with water up to several feet deep in some locations. Local estimates of up to three inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported Highway 92 closed due to brief flooding near Muchakinock Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary frontal boundary remained over Iowa on the 9th, a day after the same boundary produced a couple of tornadoes, strong storms, and heavy rainfall. In similar fashion, the environment was conducive for rotating storms. Running down the list of conducive features, LCLs were under 1000m, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40kts, and effective storm relative helicity was in excess of 200 m2/s2. All in all, it was not going to take much for storms to rotate and have the potential to product funnel clouds and at least brief tornadoes. Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near the Centerville Municipal Airport with one of the hangars sustaining EF0 damage.  The tornado proceeded through cropland up to the southwest outskirts of Centerville before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period fell across much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin. This caused the Mississippi River to rise above major flood stage from Keithsburg, Illinois to Burlington, Iowa about two weeks later.  The extended duration between the forecast and actual flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin caused the Mississippi River to rise above major flood stage at Burlington. The extended duration between the forecast and actual lower end major flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream. Burlington rose above major flood stage of 18.0 feet at 11 PM CST on 8 September.  Then crested around 18.65 feet at 1245 CST on September 11th and fell below 18.0 feet at approximately 300 PM CST on 15 September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri during the afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance tracked across the region. One supercell thunderstorm produced four tornadoes as it tracked across Benton and Linn counties during the late afternoon and early evening.  Another storm produced a tornado on the west side of Cedar Rapids later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey determined an EF1 tornado touched down just northwest of Alburnett and moved east northeast along an intermittent path for around 5 miles. The tornado lifted southwest of Central City. The tornado damaged trees and crops, and some siding on one house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches over a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa caused the Wapsipinicon River to rise above major flood stage from Independence to DeWitt, about two to seven days after the end of the heavy rain events. The extended duration between the forecast and actual major river flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin caused the Iowa River to rise above major flood stage at Marengo. The extended duration between the forecast and actual lower end major river flooding occurrence allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream. Marengo rose above major flood stage of 18.5 feet at 345 PM CST on 5 September. Then crested around 19.14 feet at 630 feet at 630 AM CST on 6 September and then fell below 18.5 feet at approximately 715 AM CST on 7 September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms tracked east across eastern Iowa during the afternoon hours. These storms produced several tornadoes and damaging wind gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several healthy large tree branches down, the time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms tracked east across eastern Iowa during the afternoon hours. These storms produced several tornadoes and damaging wind gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined a brief tornado touched down and lofted two empty grain bins and damaged some corn.  Max winds are estimated near 80 mph. The maximum width was 20 yards and the path length was .05 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slid off the Rockies and into the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandle regions late in the day on November 30th. The surface low briefly deepened as it move northeast across Oklahoma and Kansas into December 1st before predominantly stalling out over the Kansas-Missouri border area. As a result, Iowa was predominantly north of the surface warm front and experiencing the overriding and wrap around precipitation bands and eventually lingering deformation induced precipitation as the low departed eastward on the 2nd. ||Precipitation through much of the state started as all rain with elevated and surface temperatures above freezing. While surface temperatures predominantly hovered around or just above freezing through most of the event bouts of light freezing rain and accumulating snow occurred within the more robust bands of precipitation, eventually resulting in relatively widespread reports of 2 to 4 inches of snow and a tenth of an inch of ice or less. A few locations did receive snowfall in excess of 4 inches, including reports approaching 8 inches in northern Kossuth County. ||Note: A combination of factors, including snowfall, ice accumulation, and strong winds, was used to verify portions of the Winter Storm Warning.

A cold front passed through the region in the evening and overnight hours with a line of showers and storms.  One storm produced a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado damaged and uprooted trees, snapped a power pole, and caused roof damage. Maximum wind speed was estimated around 100 mph. The tornado was on the ground less than 1 minute and travelled a little more than a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms tracked east across eastern Iowa during the afternoon hours. These storms produced several tornadoes and damaging wind gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined a stronger EF0 tornado touched down northwest of Winfield Iowa. Damage to trees and corn was noted along its over a quarter mile path. The max width was estimated to be 20 yards and strongest winds were estimated to be 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri during the afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance tracked across the region. One supercell thunderstorm produced four tornadoes as it tracked across Benton and Linn counties during the late afternoon and early evening.  Another storm produced a tornado on the west side of Cedar Rapids later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was observed by a storm spotter about 4 miles south southwest of Vinton, Iowa.  The tornado briefly touched down with no visible damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches over a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa caused the Wapsipinicon River to rise above major flood stage from Independence to DeWitt, about two to seven days after the end of the heavy rain events. The extended duration between the forecast and actual major river flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin caused the Wapsipinicon River to rise above major flood stage at Independence. The extended duration between the forecast and actual lower end major river  flooding occurring allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream. Independence rose above major flood stage of 15.0 feet at 915 PM CST on 4 September. Then crested around 16.86 feet at 315 PM CST on September 5th and fell below 15.0 feet at approximately 830 AM CST on 8 September.

Another storm produced a tornado on the west side of Cedar Rapids later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado was observed by Emergency Management about 4 miles south southeast of Vinton.  The tornado briefly touched down with no visible damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri during the afternoon and evening as an upper level disturbance tracked across the region. One supercell thunderstorm produced four tornadoes as it tracked across Benton and Linn counties during the late afternoon and early evening.  Another storm produced a tornado on the west side of Cedar Rapids later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined an EF-1 tornado with maximum winds around 100 mph touched down near the intersection of Skyline Drive NW and Meadowlark Lane NW in the northwest portion of Cedar Rapids, around 908 pm. The tornado moved northeast through residential neighborhoods. The tornado destroyed a garage, uprooted and snapped large trees and power poles, and caused minor damage to roofs. The tornado lifted near the intersection of 29th St. NW and Q Ave. NW around 914 pm. The storm also caused straight line wind damage at a business near the intersection of Collins Road NE and I-380. Winds estimated around 80 mph caused a partial collapse of a metal pole barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued across portions of central and northern Iowa through mid to late September. This allowed rivers across the area to go back into flood through the latter portion of the month. The West Fork of the Des Moines River remained in flood at the end of September and will be included in the October StormData event as well. The basins impacted by flooding were the Des Moines, Cedar, Winnebago, Shell Rock, and Boone River basins as well as Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg crested at 11.29 feet on 23 September 2018 at 0530 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches over a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa caused the Wapsipinicon River to rise above major flood stage from Independence to DeWitt, about two to seven days after the end of the heavy rain events. The extended duration between the forecast and actual major river flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Episodes of heavy rainfall of about 5 to 15 inches in a 10 day period in much of northeast Iowa and southwest Wisconsin caused the Wapsipinicon River to rise above major flood stage near DeWitt. The extended duration between the forecast and actual occurrence of lower end major river flooding allowed preparations to preclude major damage downstream. The gauge near DeWitt rose above major flood stage of 12.5 feet at 7 AM CST on 7 September. Then crested around 13.99 feet at 5 PM CST on September 10th and fell slightly below 12.5 feet for a few days at approximately 745 AM CST on 17 September.  It then rose again above major flood stage at 135 AM on 22 September and continued above major flood stage into October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong storms tracked east across eastern Iowa during the afternoon hours. These storms produced several tornadoes and damaging wind gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team determined an EF1 tornado traveled a little over one mile and caused damage to trees along its path.  Some houses were damaged by falling trees.  The tornado at its widest was around 200 yards.  Maximum winds were around 95 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front interacting with a series of weak upper disturbances produced rounds of storms.  Flash flooding occurred with areas of 3 to 6 inches of rain. A few storms also produced winds of 60 to 70 mph and isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gage on Big Bear Creek (LADI4) south of Ladora rose above flood stage at 430 AM and continues to rise rapidly.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager reported many creeks and streams across the county were still out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 4.65 inches over the last 24 hours. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued to affect the Midwest and portions of Iowa, though fortunately no severe weather (hail, winds, etc...) were experienced with this event. The overall pattern was not conducive for traditional severe weather, but with a short wave moving through the upper level flow, precipitable water values around 2 inches, decent 850 mb moisture transport, and MUCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg, heavy rainfall prevailed. ||The majority of values were held in check below some of the large values seen earlier in the year, but a wide area from southwest to northeast Iowa did record 2 to around 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.05 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.40 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet conditions continued across the state with yet another round of moderate to heavy rainfall on top of already mostly saturated conditions.  A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.75 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of areas of convection colluded to bring about heavy rainfall and sporadic severe storms into Iowa during the morning hours. Low level jet induced storms formed over southern South Dakota and worked their way eastward and eventually southeastward into Iowa, while convection over southern Minnesota was initiated along a southward moving boundary.  The two predominantly came together over northern Iowa before eventually moving straight southward through the state. Initially, the main results were sub-severe storms providing heavy rainfall, before organizing and tapping into enough instability to produce damaging winds and severe hail. ||The environment within Iowa was fairly conducive to severe storm possibility, with elevated MUCAPE values around and in excess of 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40+ kts, and hail CAPE values around 600+ J/kg.  Fortunately, given that storms had grown upscale overnight, there was little to no isolated convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT estimated winds at or above 60 mph along with hail a bit larger than peas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 6.48 inches for the last 24 hour period. Due to limitations in the database entry, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary frontal boundary remained over Iowa on the 9th, a day after the same boundary produced a couple of tornadoes, strong storms, and heavy rainfall. In similar fashion, the environment was conducive for rotating storms. Running down the list of conducive features, LCLs were under 1000m, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40kts, and effective storm relative helicity was in excess of 200 m2/s2. All in all, it was not going to take much for storms to rotate and have the potential to product funnel clouds and at least brief tornadoes. Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous spotters reported a tornado north of Brooklyn.  The tornado traveled north for a brief distance across cropland in Poweshiek County before entering Tama County.  Only minor crop damage occurred in Poweshiek County.

Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed in far northern Black Hawk County and moved due north through open cropland.   The tornado encountered a house along West Gresham Road just east of Leversee Road and produced EF0 damage to trees and shingles.  The tornado then moved north into open cropland and was captured on video for the remainder of its path, crossing into Bremer County before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across northern Iowa through early September resulted in many rivers going into flood through the early to middle portion of September. The flooding impacted the basins of the Cedar, Iowa, and Shell Rock Rivers, and also Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 13.62 feet on 4 September 2018 at 0800 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet conditions continued across the state with yet another round of moderate to heavy rainfall on top of already mostly saturated conditions.  A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported water over a few gravel roads near Grinnell north of Interstate 80 and other county roads closed near Searsboro due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported that a large tree was uprooted. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary frontal boundary remained over Iowa on the 9th, a day after the same boundary produced a couple of tornadoes, strong storms, and heavy rainfall. In similar fashion, the environment was conducive for rotating storms. Running down the list of conducive features, LCLs were under 1000m, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40kts, and effective storm relative helicity was in excess of 200 m2/s2. All in all, it was not going to take much for storms to rotate and have the potential to product funnel clouds and at least brief tornadoes. Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado was determined by a tornadic debris signature on radar and subsequently found via high resolution satellite imagery.   Any damage was to crops as the tornado remained across rural agricultural land east of Orient.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport recorded heavy rainfall of 2.61 inches over the last 24 hours. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded heavy rainfall of 3.15 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary resided predominantly right along the Iowa-Missouri border and remained relatively stationary throughout the event. Beginning on September 30th, 850mb flow rode over the front and helped initiated elevated showers and weak storms that persisted (scattered in nature) form the late afternoon of the 30th through the late afternoon on the 1st. While no severe weather was recorded, weak instability (MUCAPE under 1000 J/kg) and consistent moisture flow overriding the surface boundary, was able to produce total rainfall in excess of 3 inches. As a result, flooding was seen with numerous road closures across Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Hawk County Sheriff's Department reported numerous roads closed due to flooding throughout the county.

A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.41 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CocoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.44 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff's Office relayed reports of fairly widespread flooding in and around Traer. Reports included water in basements, standing water over roads, sewers backing up, etc... Time recorded as report time. Unknown start time of the issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of areas of convection colluded to bring about heavy rainfall and sporadic severe storms into Iowa during the morning hours. Low level jet induced storms formed over southern South Dakota and worked their way eastward and eventually southeastward into Iowa, while convection over southern Minnesota was initiated along a southward moving boundary.  The two predominantly came together over northern Iowa before eventually moving straight southward through the state. Initially, the main results were sub-severe storms providing heavy rainfall, before organizing and tapping into enough instability to produce damaging winds and severe hail. ||The environment within Iowa was fairly conducive to severe storm possibility, with elevated MUCAPE values around and in excess of 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40+ kts, and hail CAPE values around 600+ J/kg.  Fortunately, given that storms had grown upscale overnight, there was little to no isolated convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 4 inches over the last 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued rainfall helped to ease drought concerns in small portions of southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rainfall helped to end severe drought conditions across all areas of the county by the 11th of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rockwell City Iowa RWIS station recorded 61 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding in portions of the county with some roads impassible. Many roads have standing water and larger creeks and streams are rising out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong non-thunderstorm winds were seen across much of the state on the 20th. The general setup was ideal for strong winds with broad high pressure situated to the west, low pressure to the east/northeast, and a strong cold front moving through from north to south. As a result of the tight surface pressure gradient, strong 850 mb winds, and subsidence with the cold air advection, wind gusts as high was 59 mph were recorded along with multiple reports of tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary resided predominantly right along the Iowa-Missouri border and remained relatively stationary throughout the event. Beginning on September 30th, 850mb flow rode over the front and helped initiated elevated showers and weak storms that persisted (scattered in nature) form the late afternoon of the 30th through the late afternoon on the 1st. While no severe weather was recorded, weak instability (MUCAPE under 1000 J/kg) and consistent moisture flow overriding the surface boundary, was able to produce total rainfall in excess of 3 inches. As a result, flooding was seen with numerous road closures across Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager relayed a report of heavy rainfall of 3.60 inches. This is a delayed report. Times estimated. Due to limitations in entry, beginning time entered as midnight CST, October 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front blew through Iowa during the evening of October 3rd, and was accompanied by very strong non-thunderstorm winds. A strong surface pressure gradient, 50+ kt 850mb winds, and general subsidence, resulted in widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and a small handful of wind gusts around 60 mph. Only minor damage was seen or reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chariton Municipal Airport AWOS recorded a 58 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Raccoon River at Perry crested at 15.50 feet on 8 September at 1645 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.88 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.89 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary frontal boundary remained over Iowa on the 9th, a day after the same boundary produced a couple of tornadoes, strong storms, and heavy rainfall. In similar fashion, the environment was conducive for rotating storms. Running down the list of conducive features, LCLs were under 1000m, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40kts, and effective storm relative helicity was in excess of 200 m2/s2. All in all, it was not going to take much for storms to rotate and have the potential to product funnel clouds and at least brief tornadoes. Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado reported by a storm chaser south of Rathbun.  This tornado stayed in rural crop and grazing land with little to no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local dispatch reported a gravel road washed out along 120th St between 310th and 320th St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued to affect the Midwest and portions of Iowa, though fortunately no severe weather (hail, winds, etc...) were experienced with this event. The overall pattern was not conducive for traditional severe weather, but with a short wave moving through the upper level flow, precipitable water values around 2 inches, decent 850 mb moisture transport, and MUCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg, heavy rainfall prevailed. ||The majority of values were held in check below some of the large values seen earlier in the year, but a wide area from southwest to northeast Iowa did record 2 to around 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.99 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 3.93 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved into Emmet county from Dickinson county north of 150th Street.  The tornado remained in rural agricultural land producing only crop damage.  Tornado dissipated upon moving into a wetland area.

MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall into the first part of October led to continued improvement of drought conditions in southern Iowa with all severe drought ending.  Only a small area of moderate drought remains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued heavy rains into early October ended severe drought across the county by October 9th with only moderate drought left across central portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall into the first part of October led to continued improvement of drought conditions in southern Iowa with all severe drought ending.  Only a small area of moderate drought remains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued heavy rains into early October ended severe drought across the county by October 9th with only a small area of moderate drought left in far eastern Decatur county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued into early October with several streams and rivers already running high. This led to additional river flooding across portions of the Des Moines, Cedar  and Iowa River basins, and also along Black Hawk Creek in Hudson. The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville had been in flood already with its crest in late September and the flooding continued into early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Hawk Creek at Hudson crested twice, the first crest was at 15.9 ft on 2 October 2018 at 11:45 UTC and the second crest at 16.32 ft on 3 October 2018 at 10:00 UTC.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville crested at 10.62 ft on 21 September 2018 at 21:45 UTC. The flooding had carried over from September 2018.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued into mid October with many rivers going into flood again across much of the forecast area. Flooding was observed in the Des Moines, Iowa, Cedar, Chariton, Thompson and Raccoon River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 15.09 ft on 12 October 2018 at 21:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front blew through Iowa during the evening of October 3rd, and was accompanied by very strong non-thunderstorm winds. A strong surface pressure gradient, 50+ kt 850mb winds, and general subsidence, resulted in widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and a small handful of wind gusts around 60 mph. Only minor damage was seen or reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City Municipal Airport recorded a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT and local law enforcement reported flooding along Highway 3 in both directions in Dumont. The road was closed overnight and the flooding ended as the level of the Shell Rock River and Hargrave Creek near Dumont both started to fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported US 65 closed due to flooding between County Road H50 and US 34.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Iowa found itself north of a relatively stationary surface boundary, which was laid out across northern Missouri. An 850mb front was situated a bit further north, over much of southern Iowa, with a decent moisture fetch from the Gulf of Mexico. Given the relation to the surface front, elevated showers and storms were the name of the game. MUCAPE values were generally around 1000 to 1500 J/kg, along with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches, climatologically anomalous for the time of the year. Lacking supporting parameters, no severe weather was seen, but heavy rainfall did lead to localized flooding. As thunderstorms moved through the Des Moines metro area, three individuals were struck by lightning walking to their cars in a parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported water covering Highway 92 between Highway T17 and Highway 163 in Mahaska County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary resided predominantly right along the Iowa-Missouri border and remained relatively stationary throughout the event. Beginning on September 30th, 850mb flow rode over the front and helped initiated elevated showers and weak storms that persisted (scattered in nature) form the late afternoon of the 30th through the late afternoon on the 1st. While no severe weather was recorded, weak instability (MUCAPE under 1000 J/kg) and consistent moisture flow overriding the surface boundary, was able to produce total rainfall in excess of 3 inches. As a result, flooding was seen with numerous road closures across Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Hawk County EM reported the following roads closed due to flooding: Eastman Rd between Cotter Rd and Foulk Rd, Cotter Rd between Miller Creek Rd and Ripple Rd, and Miller Creek Rd between Highway 218 and Cotter Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall into the first part of October led to continued improvement of drought conditions in southern Iowa with all severe drought ending.  Only a small area of moderate drought remains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued heavy rains into early October ended severe drought across the county by October 9th with only a small area of moderate drought left in northwest Davis county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ames Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.03 inches over the last 24 hours. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.25 inches and still raining as of time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poweshiek County Sheriff's Office reported a gravel road washed out; 120th Street between 320th and 310th streets.

A cold front was situated just north of the state during the morning of the 18th, slowly progressing into the state during the afternoon and evening hours. Ahead of the front, temperatures were warm and relatively humid with temperatures in the upper 80s to low 90s and dew point values around 70. Atmospheric conditions yielded MUCAPE values around 2000 J/kg, hail CAPE around 500 J/kg, bulk effective shear around 40 kts, and DCAPE values around 1200 J/kg. While conditions warranted concern for the potential for multiple severe storms, they just could not tap into their full potential yielding low end severe wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hancock County Sheriff reported power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across northern Iowa through early September resulted in many rivers going into flood through the early to middle portion of September. The flooding impacted the basins of the Cedar, Iowa, and Shell Rock Rivers, and also Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 16.45 feet on 7 September 2018 at 1815 UTC.

Very strong non-thunderstorm winds were seen across much of the state on the 20th. The general setup was ideal for strong winds with broad high pressure situated to the west, low pressure to the east/northeast, and a strong cold front moving through from north to south. As a result of the tight surface pressure gradient, strong 850 mb winds, and subsidence with the cold air advection, wind gusts as high was 59 mph were recorded along with multiple reports of tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed pictures of tree branches down in town from the strong winds. Wind gust estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued into mid October with many rivers going into flood again across much of the forecast area. Flooding was observed in the Des Moines, Iowa, Cedar, Chariton, Thompson and Raccoon River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa River at Marshalltown crested twice, the first crest was at 15.05 ft on 10 October 2018 at 08:15 UTC, the second was at 15.28 ft on 11 October 2018 at 10:00 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions saw improvement across south central into southeast Iowa with several rounds of rainfall in southern Iowa from late August through much of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the month helped ease drought concerns across the county with extreme drought backing off to severe and moderate drought. River and stream flows improved along with a recharge of farm ponds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions saw improvement across south central into southeast Iowa with several rounds of rainfall in southern Iowa from late August through much of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the month helped ease drought concerns across the county with extreme drought backing off to severe and even moderate drought in some places. River and stream flows improved along with a recharge of farm ponds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary resided predominantly right along the Iowa-Missouri border and remained relatively stationary throughout the event. Beginning on September 30th, 850mb flow rode over the front and helped initiated elevated showers and weak storms that persisted (scattered in nature) form the late afternoon of the 30th through the late afternoon on the 1st. While no severe weather was recorded, weak instability (MUCAPE under 1000 J/kg) and consistent moisture flow overriding the surface boundary, was able to produce total rainfall in excess of 3 inches. As a result, flooding was seen with numerous road closures across Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager relayed a report of 2.65 inches of heavy rainfall. This is a delayed report. Time estimated. Due to limitations in entry, beginning time entered as midnight CST, October 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: QLCS tornado developed along the leading edge of the convection in northeast rural Guthrie County.  The tornado remained in cropland for its entire path producing plenty of crop damage along with topping trees in a few different groves.  The path was initially narrow but broadened as it continued northeast.  Only toward the end of the track did it narrow once more.  Damage path was also visible on high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported that Highway 141 between Grimes and Granger was essentially a sheet of water, with speeds of only 10 mph possible.

Heavy rain continued into mid October with many rivers going into flood again across much of the forecast area. Flooding was observed in the Des Moines, Iowa, Cedar, Chariton, Thompson and Raccoon River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raccoon River at Des Moines at Fleur Drive crested twice, the first crest at 15.13 ft on 11 October 2018 at 02:30 UTC, the second crest at 15.06 ft on 13 October 2018 at 09:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 3.50 inches. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST. Actual estimated start time of 1130pm CST on August 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet conditions continued across the state with yet another round of moderate to heavy rainfall on top of already mostly saturated conditions.  A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 1.63 inches since midnight and 3.89 inches since Friday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.

Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding in portions of the county with some roads impassible. Many roads have standing water and larger creeks and streams are rising out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT passed along a report of a tree blocking one lane along Highway 69 south of Osceola. The tree was too big to move by hand. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain continued into mid October with many rivers going into flood again across much of the forecast area. Flooding was observed in the Des Moines, Iowa, Cedar, Chariton, Thompson and Raccoon River basins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa River at Tama Highway E49 crested at 13.23 ft on 12 October 2018 at 19:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Iowa found itself north of a relatively stationary surface boundary, which was laid out across northern Missouri. An 850mb front was situated a bit further north, over much of southern Iowa, with a decent moisture fetch from the Gulf of Mexico. Given the relation to the surface front, elevated showers and storms were the name of the game. MUCAPE values were generally around 1000 to 1500 J/kg, along with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches, climatologically anomalous for the time of the year. Lacking supporting parameters, no severe weather was seen, but heavy rainfall did lead to localized flooding. As thunderstorms moved through the Des Moines metro area, three individuals were struck by lightning walking to their cars in a parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.69 inches over the last 24 hours.
