EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of areas of convection colluded to bring about heavy rainfall and sporadic severe storms into Iowa during the morning hours. Low level jet induced storms formed over southern South Dakota and worked their way eastward and eventually southeastward into Iowa, while convection over southern Minnesota was initiated along a southward moving boundary.  The two predominantly came together over northern Iowa before eventually moving straight southward through the state. Initially, the main results were sub-severe storms providing heavy rainfall, before organizing and tapping into enough instability to produce damaging winds and severe hail. ||The environment within Iowa was fairly conducive to severe storm possibility, with elevated MUCAPE values around and in excess of 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40+ kts, and hail CAPE values around 600+ J/kg.  Fortunately, given that storms had grown upscale overnight, there was little to no isolated convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a tree down on a camper, with the roof buckled. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Franklin County Sheriff's Office reported a number of roads across the county with water running over them, including a few impassable at the time of the call.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet conditions continued across the state with yet another round of moderate to heavy rainfall on top of already mostly saturated conditions.  A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported several road closures due to water over the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail larger than golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.71 inches over the last 24 hours. Due to database entry limitation, beginning time coded as midnight CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 16th allowing several sites across south central and southeast Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

A cold front blew through Iowa during the evening of October 3rd, and was accompanied by very strong non-thunderstorm winds. A strong surface pressure gradient, 50+ kt 850mb winds, and general subsidence, resulted in widespread wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and a small handful of wind gusts around 60 mph. Only minor damage was seen or reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to 6 inch diameter tree limbs were down. The wind gusts were from convective showers without lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued to affect the Midwest and portions of Iowa, though fortunately no severe weather (hail, winds, etc...) were experienced with this event. The overall pattern was not conducive for traditional severe weather, but with a short wave moving through the upper level flow, precipitable water values around 2 inches, decent 850 mb moisture transport, and MUCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg, heavy rainfall prevailed. ||The majority of values were held in check below some of the large values seen earlier in the year, but a wide area from southwest to northeast Iowa did record 2 to around 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.47 inches over the last 24 hours.

A relatively stationary frontal boundary remained over Iowa on the 9th, a day after the same boundary produced a couple of tornadoes, strong storms, and heavy rainfall. In similar fashion, the environment was conducive for rotating storms. Running down the list of conducive features, LCLs were under 1000m, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear was in excess of 40kts, and effective storm relative helicity was in excess of 200 m2/s2. All in all, it was not going to take much for storms to rotate and have the potential to product funnel clouds and at least brief tornadoes. Scattered showers and storms were persistent through the afternoon and early evening, with a number producing brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed in rural western Ringgold county and moved almost due north across open cropland.  Little damage was noted.  Tornado was found using high resolution satellite imagery.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed between Creston and Cromwell in western Union county and traveled on a somewhat unusual northwest path.  Tornado was mostly in open rural cropland for nearly two miles producing only crop damage.  However, the tornado encountered farmsteads along Clover Avenue about one half mile south of 170th Street.  A garage was destroyed on the west side of the road and was rated EF1 damage given generally poor construction.  Additional EF1 damage was observed with the nearby house with a portion of the roof and truss structure removed.  Tree damage was observed across the road at an adjacent house.  Additional minor damage was found about one quarter mile farther up the road with the last damage noted north of 170th Street on the west side of the road where a few roof panels were blown off an old metal shed.

Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 12th allowing several sites across northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season.

A frontal boundary resided predominantly right along the Iowa-Missouri border and remained relatively stationary throughout the event. Beginning on September 30th, 850mb flow rode over the front and helped initiated elevated showers and weak storms that persisted (scattered in nature) form the late afternoon of the 30th through the late afternoon on the 1st. While no severe weather was recorded, weak instability (MUCAPE under 1000 J/kg) and consistent moisture flow overriding the surface boundary, was able to produce total rainfall in excess of 3 inches. As a result, flooding was seen with numerous road closures across Black Hawk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported that several creeks were out of their banks across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Note, a portion of this event began on August 31st (heavy rainfall namely) and carried over into September 1st. ||The general setup for the day was less so for severe weather and more so for potential heavy rainfall. Synoptically, there were no major systems affecting the Plains, though a weak warm frontal boundary found itself slowly moving northward into the state, eventually giving way to a weak cold front later in the day. MUCAPE values ramped up throughout the day on the heels of strong warm air advection and moisture advection, reaching the 2000-3000+ J/kg during the evening and overnight hours. Effective bulk shear shear values were marginally supportive, sporadically reaching 30 to 40 knots. As hinted at previously, strong warm air advection and moisture advection at and around the 850 mb level boosted widespread lift and raised precipitable water values around and above 2.0 inches. ||The primary result of the storms was heavy rainfall, though a few were able to form strong enough updrafts and/or organize long enough to produce brief periods of marginally severe hail and damaging winds. The greatest rainfall amounts were around and in excess of 4 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.04 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet conditions continued across the state with yet another round of moderate to heavy rainfall on top of already mostly saturated conditions.  A shortwave moving through the upper level flow out of the southwest, a surface front situated to the west and northwest of the state, and strong 850 mb flow were the main contributors to showers and storms.  ||Conditions were certainly conducive for heavy rainfall, including MUCAPE of at least 1000 J/kg and ranging up to 2000+ J/kg, precipitable water values near and in excess of 2.0 inches, and strong moisture advection into and through the state throughout the event. Additionally, conditions were relatively favorable for a funnel cloud or brief spin up or two with lifted condensation levels predominantly under 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE values around 175-200 J/kg, and 0-1 km storm relative helicity values around 100-150 m2/s2.  ||In the end, a couple of funnel cloud and brief tornado reports were seen across the most favorable areas in northwest Iowa, followed up by nearly exclusively heavy rainfall and flash flooding reports from southwest Iowa into central Iowa and even parts of northern Iowa.  Greatest reports included multiple 3+ inch reports across northern and northwest Iowa and 2 day totals in excess of 6 inches into parts of central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Family members of a trained spotter sent in a video of a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. Fortunately, with widespread precipitation and limited instability, storms remained sub-severe during this portion of the heavy rainfall event. By late afternoon and evening, as the surface low progressed across the area, stronger, more organized convection moved across the area and produced severe winds. The severe wind event will be covered by another entry focused on September 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media relayed public report of heavy rainfall of 3.28 inches over the last 24 hours and still raining.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marshall County 911 reported the shoulder washed out on Hwy 146 south of 290th St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of areas of convection colluded to bring about heavy rainfall and sporadic severe storms into Iowa during the morning hours. Low level jet induced storms formed over southern South Dakota and worked their way eastward and eventually southeastward into Iowa, while convection over southern Minnesota was initiated along a southward moving boundary.  The two predominantly came together over northern Iowa before eventually moving straight southward through the state. Initially, the main results were sub-severe storms providing heavy rainfall, before organizing and tapping into enough instability to produce damaging winds and severe hail. ||The environment within Iowa was fairly conducive to severe storm possibility, with elevated MUCAPE values around and in excess of 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40+ kts, and hail CAPE values around 600+ J/kg.  Fortunately, given that storms had grown upscale overnight, there was little to no isolated convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a tree down at Lincoln High School. This is a delayed report, time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a dugout at a local baseball field was destroyed and that the bowling alley experienced partial roof destruction. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across northern Iowa through early September resulted in many rivers going into flood through the early to middle portion of September. The flooding impacted the basins of the Cedar, Iowa, and Shell Rock Rivers, and also Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shell Rock River at Shell Rock crested at 14.49 feet on 6 September 2018 at 1415 UTC.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa River at Tama Highway E49 crested at 13.04 feet on 6 September 2018 at 0430 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued across portions of central and northern Iowa through mid to late September. This allowed rivers across the area to go back into flood through the latter portion of the month. The West Fork of the Des Moines River remained in flood at the end of September and will be included in the October StormData event as well. The basins impacted by flooding were the Des Moines, Cedar, Winnebago, Shell Rock, and Boone River basins as well as Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Fork of the Des Moines River at Algona crested at 19.16 feet on 22 September 2018 at 1915 UTC.

A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall was seen across the area, primarily situated over the northern third of the state, as a surface boundary sat across central Iowa and a shortwave moved through the upper level flow. Overall, conditions remained ripe and very much summer-like as opposed to mid-September including precipitable water values were around and in excess of 1.7 inches, moderate to strong low level moisture flow, modest MUCAPE around 500-1000+ J/kg, strong bulk effective shear of 40 to 60 kts, and continued lifting mechanisms from the aforementioned surface boundary and upper level short wave.||Over a roughly 24 hour period, a number of sites experienced havy rainfall in the 3 to 4 inch range, with a few reporting as much as 5+ inches. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.65 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued across portions of central and northern Iowa through mid to late September. This allowed rivers across the area to go back into flood through the latter portion of the month. The West Fork of the Des Moines River remained in flood at the end of September and will be included in the October StormData event as well. The basins impacted by flooding were the Des Moines, Cedar, Winnebago, Shell Rock, and Boone River basins as well as Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 18.96 feet on 23 September 2018 at 1930 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued to affect the Midwest and portions of Iowa, though fortunately no severe weather (hail, winds, etc...) were experienced with this event. EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL relayed images of street flooding in Cedar Falls. The water appeared to be fairly rapidly moving in the image.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was situated just north of the state during the morning of the 18th, slowly progressing into the state during the afternoon and evening hours. Ahead of the front, temperatures were warm and relatively humid with temperatures in the upper 80s to low 90s and dew point values around 70. Atmospheric conditions yielded MUCAPE values around 2000 J/kg, hail CAPE around 500 J/kg, bulk effective shear around 40 kts, and DCAPE values around 1200 J/kg. While conditions warranted concern for the potential for multiple severe storms, they just could not tap into their full potential yielding low end severe wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail along with heavy rain and estimated 45 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak, relatively stationary, boundary found itself across portions of western and central Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd.  While surface observations were not tremendous, especially for early September, in the upper 70s to mid 80s, SBCAPE values approached 4000+ J/kg in areas of southwest and south-central Iowa concurrent with dew points into the upper 70s. Support for organized storms was marginal to weakly supportive with bulk effective shear generally less than 40 kts and hail CAPE around 800 J/kg. Heavy rain was also favored with the slow moving boundary, expected 850mb jet, and strong moisture advection into the area. ||Resulting weather involved initially discrete cells producing severe hail, quickly morphing into multicellular convection and messier convection as the low level jet began to influence conditions. Early on, severe hail dominated reports, eventually giving way to heavy rainfall and flash flooding. The most impressive reports included up to 2 inch hail on the north side of the Des Moines metro area and heavy rainfall in excess of 6 inches in portions of Poweshiek County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.10 inches on the north side of Ames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornadoes made an October appearance along with other strong storms and heavy rainfall as a number of conditions came together. A frontal boundary stalled out across Iowa, situated from southwest to northeast, roughly cutting the state in half. Dew points were on the high side given the time of the year, in the upper 60s to low 70s, yielding low lifted condensation levels. In addition, low level turning resulted in 0-1km SRH values of 100-200+ m2/s2 within an environment of 1000+ J/kg SBCAPE. With all of that in play, a pair of tornadoes were confirmed during the evening hours, before giving way to predominantly sub-severe storms and heavy rainfall. Heavy rainfall conditions were bolstered by strong 850mb/moisture flow throughout the event with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported two roads closed across the county due to culvert failure and approximately 10 culverts damaged from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of areas of convection colluded to bring about heavy rainfall and sporadic severe storms into Iowa during the morning hours. Low level jet induced storms formed over southern South Dakota and worked their way eastward and eventually southeastward into Iowa, while convection over southern Minnesota was initiated along a southward moving boundary.  The two predominantly came together over northern Iowa before eventually moving straight southward through the state. Initially, the main results were sub-severe storms providing heavy rainfall, before organizing and tapping into enough instability to produce damaging winds and severe hail. ||The environment within Iowa was fairly conducive to severe storm possibility, with elevated MUCAPE values around and in excess of 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40+ kts, and hail CAPE values around 600+ J/kg.  Fortunately, given that storms had grown upscale overnight, there was little to no isolated convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Iowa found itself north of a relatively stationary surface boundary, which was laid out across northern Missouri. An 850mb front was situated a bit further north, over much of southern Iowa, with a decent moisture fetch from the Gulf of Mexico. Given the relation to the surface front, elevated showers and storms were the name of the game. MUCAPE values were generally around 1000 to 1500 J/kg, along with precipitable water values approaching 1.5 to 1.75 inches, climatologically anomalous for the time of the year. Lacking supporting parameters, no severe weather was seen, but heavy rainfall did lead to localized flooding. As thunderstorms moved through the Des Moines metro area, three individuals were struck by lightning walking to their cars in a parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3 inches. Beginning and ending times estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning heavy rainfall over northern Iowa, conditions cleared out for the late morning and afternoon hours ahead of an impending cold front. As a result, MUCAPE values were able to climb from below 1000 J/kg to over 2000 J/kg by the mid to late afternoon hours, coincident with 30 to 40+ kts of effective bulk shear,  and DCAPE approaching 1000 J/kg ahead of the boundary.  By the afternoon, storms initiated along the impending cold front, quickly taking a linear form with the near-parallel 0-6km shear, and continued to move eastward across the state. While much of the line remained sub-severe, sections of the line were able to produce both severe wind gust and wind damage, west of Interstate 35.  A tornado also occurred in far northwest Emmet county which moved in from Dickinson county but produced only crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a tree uprooted. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

Cold air settled into the state through the early morning hours on the 15th allowing several sites across central and northern Iowa to receive a killing freeze ending the growing season. A few of the counties that received a freeze a few days earlier were duplicated to account for the whole county, as some of the urban areas may not have frozen earlier or portions of the county stayed closer to freezing or slightly above.

Heavy rainfall continued across portions of central and northern Iowa through mid to late September. This allowed rivers across the area to go back into flood through the latter portion of the month. The West Fork of the Des Moines River remained in flood at the end of September and will be included in the October StormData event as well. The basins impacted by flooding were the Des Moines, Cedar, Winnebago, Shell Rock, and Boone River basins as well as Black Hawk Creek at Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt crested at 12.83 feet on 23 September 2018 at 0400 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave disturbance rotated around a large southwest U.S. closed low aloft and into confluent flow aloft across the Northern Plains, light wintry precipitation developed on December 31. An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 45 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After less than an inch of snowfall, winds increased to 35 to 45 mph as temperatures plummeted, taking wind chill values as low as -20 at Sioux Falls and -22 at Sheldon during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall continued to affect the Midwest and portions of Iowa, though fortunately no severe weather (hail, winds, etc...) were experienced with this event. The overall pattern was not conducive for traditional severe weather, but with a short wave moving through the upper level flow, precipitable water values around 2 inches, decent 850 mb moisture transport, and MUCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg, heavy rainfall prevailed. ||The majority of values were held in check below some of the large values seen earlier in the year, but a wide area from southwest to northeast Iowa did record 2 to around 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.10 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain fell across northeast Iowa on October 8th and 9th with amounts up to two inches. The ground was still saturated from earlier rainfall in late September and early October and the runoff from the rains on the 8th and 9th caused flooding along the Turkey River at Spillville (Winneshiek County), Elkader and Garber (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Spillville. The river crested less than a foot above the flood stage at 9.8 feet on October 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft. Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary, with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow. Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 3 to 6 inches across extreme northwest Iowa. Snowfall of 3.5 inches was measured at Sibley. Warm surface temperatures limited impacts to brief slushy accumulations on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa on October 1st. These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain most of Clayton County. With already saturated ground from rain in late September, the runoff caused river flooding to occur along the Turkey River at both Elkader and Garber. Flooding along Peck Creek sent water over a road west of Millville and creeks in North Buena Vista also went out of their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Elkader. The river crested less than a foot above the flood stage at 12.67 feet on October 1st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks in Elkader. The river crested almost three above the flood stage at 14.72 feet on October 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged and exceptionally wet fall combined with successive flash flood events in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois to bring widespread minor to moderate river flooding to nearly all rivers in this area. In addition, major flooding occurred on rivers, including the Mississippi, Iowa, Wapsipinicon, English, North Skunk, Maquoketa, and Rock Rivers. This major flood event stretched through much of mid October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding on the Mississippi River at the Keokuk, IA, LD19 gage began at 9 PM, October 9th, when it exceeded 19 feet. It crested at 20.8 feet, 1050 PM, October 14th, before falling below 19 feet, which is major flood stage, at 8 PM, October 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms occurred along the Highway 20 corridor during the afternoon and evening hours. Spotters and Emergency Managers reported rainfall amounts of  2 to 6 inches on already saturated ground, which caused flash flooding  of roads and along creeks and streams.  Highway 52 in Dubuque County was closed along with roads in Delaware County and Buchanan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed numerous roads in Delaware County. Flash flooding along a creek resulted in three feet of standing water and stranded cars in Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms swept through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the evening. These storms produced widespread gusty winds and heavy rain, and a brief tornado in Jones County Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a field and flattened corn as well as snapped tree branches about a 12 inches in diameter before crossing a road and dissipating. Peak winds with this tornado were 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong storms moved over portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon. A brief tornado occurred in Benton County with one of these storms. No damage was observed with this tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was observed by a broadcast meteorologist. The tornado touched down in a field and lasted for less than 30 seconds. No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused washouts of gravel roads, and closed some roads in the Winthrop area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved over the same areas through the early morning hours in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. A band of 2 to 4 inches of rain fell on already saturated soils, causing a stripe of flash flooding from Keokuk County through Clinton County, and Stephenson County in northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa DOT closed Highway 21 in What Cheer due to flooding from Coal Creek. The water over the road was a foot deep, resulting in a detour. The water receded enough for one lane to be reopened around 445 AM CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Iowa City, and locations southeast of Iowa City where creeks flow into the Iowa River. In Iowa City, Burlington Street near Van Buren Street was closed due to flooding, and a car was stalled in the floodwater. Flooding subsided by 510 AM CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported the Little Maquoketa River out of its banks and over portions of Millville Road.

The river crested at 13.9 feet at 730 AM, October 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged and exceptionally wet fall combined with successive flash flood events in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois to bring widespread minor to moderate river flooding to nearly all rivers in this area. In addition, major flooding occurred on rivers, including the Mississippi, Iowa, Wapsipinicon, English, North Skunk, Maquoketa, and Rock Rivers. This major flood event stretched through much of mid October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended flood took place on the lower Wapsipinicon River October, 2018. Major flooding was ongoing at the De Witt (4 miles south) gage,  from September at the start of the month of October and continued with only a short 1 day break on the 5th, all the way to October 21 at 1 AM when it fell below major flood stage of 12.5 feet. The river crested at 13.9 feet at 730 AM, October 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11.  Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A steady moderate rainfall during October 9-10 - resulting in 1.50 to 2.00 inches of rainfall - produced yet another period of moderate flooding.  The Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) crested 4.6 feet above flood stage on October 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Rock River were impacted by minor flooding as river levels crested at 0.5 feet above flood stage near Rock Rapids (gage site RAPI4) on October 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low-lying crop and pasture land was flooded between Everly and Spencer as river levels  crested at 0.9 feet above flood stage on the Ocheyedan River near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) on October 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued to ease on the Little Sioux River with drier weather at the start of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from September, river levels on the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) continued to fall in early October. Impacts were limited to minor agricultural flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Kansas into Central Illinois bringing heavy snows of 4 to 13 inches to the area.  Strong north winds gusted to 40 to 50 mph and combined with the heavy snow to produce widespread blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources of snow amounts of 4 inches up to over 10 inches over the southeast one half of the county, combined with strong north winds of over 30 mph with frequent gusts over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions. These conditions started in the afternoon and lasted into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved out of the central Rockies and produced snowfall of 2 to 11 inches across southwest Iowa. Blizzard conditions were met in southwest Iowa with measured wind gusts 40 to 62 mph and widespread whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat lackluster event, originally expected to produce around 4 inches of snow or less, outproduced the forecasts and dropped as much as 6 to 7 inches of snow across portions of southern Iowa. Meteorologically, a number of weak to moderate factors lined up to produce the aforementioned snowfall including a weak upper level wave entering the region, an 850mb - 700mb front aloft situated over southern portions of the state, and likely some weak right entrance jet streak influence.  ||Fortunately, much of the snowfall occurred during the mid afternoon to late evening hours on a weekend, limiting travel and other impacts. The greatest impacts were seen along Interstate 35 in Clarke and Decatur counties where numerous vehicles were reported in the ditches. Interestingly, many of those reports occurred during the early portions of the snowfall, when only a couple inches had fallen.  Rural counties with similar snowfall reported few to no impacts, and all impacts trailed off as the snow accumulated throughout the evening.

An amplified upper level trough, cut off at times, swept through Kansas, Missouri and Illinois. The attendant surface low also swept through Kansas and Missouri on a similar path, well south of Iowa. The surface low was tightly wound, slowly deepening from about 998mb to 994mb as it traveled south of the state. Precipitation associated with the warm advection wing and wrap around areas initially saw rainfall during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th into the 25th before transitioning over to snow as colder air filtered in. An extremely tight precipitation/snowfall gradient set up roughly along Interstate 80 and south as precipitation processes battled a cold, dry air intrusion from the north. As a result, gradients in the snowfall were so tight that some areas that received 6 to 8 inches of snowfall or more were only a dozen or so miles south of areas that received as little as an inch or less.  Throughout the day, the heaviest banded snowfall remained situated over southern Iowa, resulting in numerous reported snowfall amounts in excess of a foot and as high as 16 to 17 inches. While the snow tended to be on the heavy and wet side, strong winds on the backside of the low, gusting in excess of 40 mph regularly, helped degrade conditions and keep visibilities well below a quarter to half mile throughout much of the mid to late morning and afternoon. ||As one might imagine, numerous travel issues were reported in the heaviest hit areas, including dozens of vehicles in ditches along Interstate 35 south of Des Moines, and temporary closures of the interstate due to disabled vehicles on the roadway. Fortunately, reported power issues were generally intermittent, and tree damage was not substantial.

As a result, gradients in the snowfall were so tight that some areas that received 6 to 8 inches of snowfall or more were only a dozen or so miles south of areas that received as little as an inch or less.  Throughout the day, the heaviest banded snowfall remained situated over southern Iowa, resulting in numerous reported snowfall amounts in excess of a foot and as high as 16 to 17 inches. While the snow tended to be on the heavy and wet side, strong winds on the backside of the low, gusting in excess of 40 mph regularly, helped degrade conditions and keep visibilities well below a quarter to half mile throughout much of the mid to late morning and afternoon. ||As one might imagine, numerous travel issues were reported in the heaviest hit areas, including dozens of vehicles in ditches along Interstate 35 south of Des Moines, and temporary closures of the interstate due to disabled vehicles on the roadway. Fortunately, reported power issues were generally intermittent, and tree damage was not substantial.
